1.4: The forty-day post-resurrection period (1:3) before Pentecost matches the Omer count (Lev 23:15-16) — fifty days from Passover to Shavuot (Weeks/Pentecost). Whether the chronological match was providential design or Luke's deliberate framing, the Spirit's arrival at the feast when Jews celebrated the giving of Torah at Sinai is theologically loaded: the new law is the Spirit written on hearts (Jer 31:33; Ezek 36:26-27).
1.12: The Mount of Olives was associated in prophetic tradition with eschatological events (Zech 14:4); its proximity to the temple (a Sabbath's journey ≈ 2,000 cubits / 880 meters, m. 'Eruvin 4:3) meant the disciples returned to the symbolic center of Jewish religious life. The upper room (hyperōon) was likely a substantial space — Jerusalem houses of the affluent had rooftop rooms used for private prayer and assembly. The eleven plus women plus Mary and brothers (total ~120, v. 15) represents a deliberately quorate gathering: ten men was the minimum for a synagogue minyan.
1.1: Luke's address to Theophilus (kratiste Theophile, 'most excellent Theophilus') places Acts in the literary genre of Hellenistic historiography — the same dedicatory convention used by Josephus (Against Apion 1.1) and other educated writers addressing noble patrons. The reference to the 'first account' (ton proton logon) creates explicit literary continuity with Luke's Gospel, making Luke-Acts the longest single work in the NT. Whether Theophilus was a real individual or a symbolic 'lover of God' (the Greek meaning of the name) has been debated since antiquity.
1.2: The 'day he was taken up' frames the ascension as the narrative boundary between the two volumes. Apostoloi (apostles) means 'sent ones' — those commissioned with specific delegated authority; Luke restricts the term more carefully than Paul, who applies it more broadly. The phrase 'through the Holy Spirit he had given them commands' establishes the pneumatic basis for the apostolic mission — the Spirit-authorized instructions of the risen Christ are the church's foundational charter.
1.3: Forty days of post-resurrection appearances evokes the OT pattern: Moses's forty days on Sinai (Exod 24:18), Israel's forty years in the wilderness, Elijah's forty days to Horeb (1 Kgs 19:8). The number marks a period of formation and transition. Basileian tou theou (kingdom of God) is the central theme of Jesus's teaching throughout Luke; its appearance here as the post-resurrection teaching subject signals continuity between pre- and post-Easter proclamation.
1.5: The distinction between John's water-baptism and Spirit-baptism reflects a genuine historical development the early church had to explain. John's baptism was a well-known initiatory rite connected with eschatological expectation; many converts had received it. The Baptist himself had distinguished his baptism from a coming greater one (Luke 3:16). The promise here is that the transition anticipated by John will happen 'not many days from now' — Pentecost fulfills what John's movement pointed toward.
1.6: The disciples' question — 'are you at this time restoring the kingdom to Israel?' — reflects standard Second Temple eschatological expectation: the Davidic kingdom would be restored, Roman occupation ended, and Israel vindicated over the nations. This hope drew from texts like Amos 9:11 (the fallen booth of David), Ezek 37 (dry bones), and Zech 9-14. Jesus does not dismiss the expectation but reframes it: the timing is not theirs to know; the mission is universal rather than nationally restricted.
1.7: Chronous ē kairous (times or seasons) is a Jewish apocalyptic formula for the divinely determined calendar of eschatological events. Chronos typically refers to duration; kairos to the appointed moment (the decisive time). The Father retains sovereign control of both. The rebuke has eschatological precedent: Daniel (12:9) and Ezra (4 Ezra 4:33-37) were told the end-time calendar was not disclosed. The disciples' curiosity is legitimate but misdirected — the commission, not the calendar, is their concern.
1.8: The geographic structure — Jerusalem → Judea → Samaria → ends of the earth — is both a mission outline and a social-boundary expansion. Each step crosses a cultural barrier that the restoration community would have found challenging: Judea extends from the city, Samaria crosses the ancient Jewish-Samaritan ethnic rift (rooted in the Assyrian resettlement of 2 Kgs 17), and 'ends of the earth' opens to Gentiles. The same sequence of 'ends of the earth' language appears in Isa 49:6, connecting Paul's Gentile mission to the Servant's vocation.
1.9: Cloud reception (nephele hypelaben auton) is the OT theophanic vehicle: the Shekinah-cloud of Sinai and the Tabernacle (Exod 13:21; 16:10; 33:9), the cloud at the Transfiguration (Luke 9:34-35), and the Son of Man coming on clouds (Dan 7:13). The ascension confirms Jesus's exaltation to the divine throne rather than mere departure; his disappearance into cloud signals transportation into the divine sphere. Jewish Second Temple texts had developed the concept of heavenly ascent for Enoch and Moses (1 Enoch 70-71; Assumption of Moses).
1.10: Two men in white garments (v.10) mirror the two angels at the tomb (Luke 24:4) and structurally connect the Resurrection and Ascension. Angelic messengers in white are a standard feature of Jewish visionary literature (Dan 10:5; 2 Macc 3:26). Their appearance signals a heavenly interpretation of an earthly event — the disciples need interpretive help to grasp what the physical scene means theologically. Their question reframes watching the ascension into watching for the parousia.
1.11: 'This Jesus who was taken up from you into heaven will come in the same way as you saw him go into heaven' — the promise of parousia in the same mode as ascension is eschatologically precise. Jewish tradition associated the Mount of Olives with the end-time return of the Shekinah (Zech 14:4; Ezek 43:2); the angelic announcement specifies that it is Jesus, not a diffuse divine presence, who returns. The identification of the disciples as 'men of Galilee' marks them as provincials — those least expected to receive and transmit world-historical revelation.
1.13: The upper room gathering lists the Eleven in an order similar to Luke 6:14-16 (but not identical, suggesting fluid oral tradition). The room's identification as an hyperōon (upper story room) may connect to the supper room of Luke 22:12 — the same word, possibly the same location. By the second century, Christian tradition located an 'upper room' in Jerusalem as a sacred site. The list includes James son of Alpheus and Judas son of James (not Iscariot) — names otherwise poorly attested in the tradition.
1.14: The community gathered in prayer includes 'the women' (likely including those named in Luke 24:10), Mary the mother of Jesus, and Jesus's brothers (named in Mark 6:3 as James, Joseph, Judas, and Simon). The brothers' presence is remarkable: Mark 3:21 and John 7:5 indicate that Jesus's family did not believe during his ministry. Their inclusion here reflects the transformative impact of the resurrection appearances (1 Cor 15:7 specifically notes an appearance to James). Mary appears here for the last time in the NT narrative.
1.15: One hundred twenty believers constituted the restored community. Twelve was the symbolic number of Israel's tribes; 120 is the number required to establish a Sanhedrin in Jewish tradition (m. Sanhedrin 1:6 — a town needed 120 men to merit its own court). The number is also 12 × 10, suggesting a community constituted as a complete Israel-of-twelve. Peter takes leadership in the gathered assembly — a role continuous with his pre-crucifixion designation as foundation-stone (Luke 22:32).
1.16: Peter's address frames Judas's betrayal as Scripture that 'must be fulfilled' (edei plērōthēnai) — the same divine necessity governing Jesus's passion. The Davidic authorship of Psalms was the standard assumption of first-century Judaism (Josephus calls the Psalms hymns of David, Ant. 7.305). Peter reads Ps 69:25 and Ps 109:8 as typological — David writing prophetically about his descendant's betrayer, as the Spirit speaking through David. This hermeneutical method — reading OT suffering-psalms as prophetic script — is characteristic of early Christian exegesis.
1.17: Klēros (lot/share) in 'he was numbered among us and was allotted his share in this ministry' echoes the priestly lot-casting of the Levitical system (1 Chr 24-25 — lots determined which priests served and when). The twelve disciples were 'allotted' their roles by divine choice, not self-selection. Judas's betrayal is thus a corruption of a divinely-given assignment, making it the more grievous. The lot-casting for Matthias (v.26) restores the sacred number.
1.18: Luke's account of Judas's death ('falling headlong, he burst open in the middle') differs from Matthew's 'hanged himself' (Matt 27:5). These are not necessarily contradictory: the traditional harmonization is that Judas hanged himself, and afterward the body fell and decomposed or was cut down. The field's purchase — here from the reward money that went from Judas to the priests and then to property — differs in narrated sequence from Matthew's account. The 'Field of Blood' (Akeldama) was a known Jerusalem site, confirming an early historical tradition behind both accounts.
1.19: Akeldama is Aramaic: haql demā (field of blood). Aramaic was the primary spoken language of Jerusalem and Judea in this period; Greek was the administrative and trade language. Luke's note that the name is 'in their own language' (i.e., the local Aramaic) authenticates the tradition as genuinely Palestinian rather than diaspora-derived. The persistence of the name in the local memory is the kind of geographical detail that corroborates historical tradition.
1.20: Two Psalms applied to Judas: Ps 69:25 ('Let his camp become desolate, and let no one dwell in it') and Ps 109:8 ('Let another take his position of overseer'). Ps 69 and Ps 109 are both lament psalms in which the psalmist (David) prays for judgment on enemies and vindication for himself. Early Christians read them as Davidic prophecy pointing to the ultimate David (Jesus) and his situation — making Judas the type of the enemy-of-the-Anointed that the psalms describe. The 'overseer' (episkopē, office/oversight) language of Ps 109:8 specifically enables the replacement.
1.21: The qualification for apostolic succession — 'one of the men who have accompanied us the whole time the Lord Jesus came and went among us, beginning from the baptism of John' — establishes eyewitness presence as the foundational criterion. This explains why Paul's apostleship is contested (he was not a disciple during the ministry) and why he argues strenuously for the Damascus road as a genuine resurrection appearance (1 Cor 9:1; 15:8). The community's memory of the whole ministry arc, from baptism to ascension, is the qualified testimony.
1.22: 'Witness to his resurrection' defines the apostolic function more precisely than general discipleship. The resurrection is the legally contested claim requiring eyewitness attestation; the apostles are the legal witnesses (martyres). This judicial-witness framing of apostleship explains why Paul insists so strongly that he 'saw the Lord' (1 Cor 9:1; 15:8) — the apostolic credential requires it. In Roman legal practice, eyewitness testimony was the highest category of proof; the apostolic witness is calibrated to this standard.
1.23: Two candidates: Joseph called Barsabbas (surnamed Justus) and Matthias. Barsabbas ('son of the Sabbath' in Aramaic, or possibly 'son of the elder') is otherwise unknown in the NT canon; Matthias appears only here. Both had the qualifications of v.21-22 — the community of 120 had more than two qualified members but nominated these two specifically. The designation of Joseph as 'Justus' (the righteous one) was a common diaspora agnomen reflecting the bearer's reputation for Torah-observance.
1.24: Prayer before lot-casting: 'You, Lord, who know the hearts of all, show which of these two you have chosen'. Kardiognōstēs (heart-knower) is the attribute invoked — the same term used of God in Acts 15:8. First-century Judaism believed that lots expressed divine will when cast in a proper ritual frame (Prov 16:33: 'the lot is cast into the lap, but its every decision is from the LORD'). The prayer makes explicit that the lot will reveal God's prior election, not create it.
1.25: 'To take the place in this ministry and apostleship from which Judas turned aside to go to his own place' — eis ton topon ton idion (to his own place) is a euphemism for Judas's judgment; rabbinic literature uses maqom (place) similarly for one's ultimate destination. The phrase 'turned aside' (parebē, deviated/transgressed) implies wilful defection rather than predestined failure; the narrative maintains Judas's moral responsibility while reading his betrayal as scripturally anticipated.
1.26: Lot-casting (klērous edōkan autois) was a legitimate Jewish method of discerning divine will (1 Chr 24-25 for priestly assignments; Lev 16:8 for the Day of Atonement goats; Neh 10:34 for wood offerings). The lot fell on Matthias, and he was 'numbered with the eleven apostles' — restoring the twelve to symbolic completeness. This is the last lot-cast in the NT; after Pentecost, discernment of leadership comes through the Spirit and community recognition rather than lot. Matthias never appears by name again in Acts.

2.1: Shavuot (Pentecost/Weeks) was one of three pilgrimage festivals requiring attendance at Jerusalem (Deut 16:16); the diaspora Jews catalogued in vv. 9–11 represent the full geographic range of Jewish settlement — from Parthia in the east to Rome in the west. Philo (De Specialibus Legibus 2.176-188) describes the festival crowds and the joy of first-fruits offering. Luke's list of nations in 2:9–11 closely mirrors ancient ethnographic schemes (cf. the Table of Nations, Gen 10) and may function as an implicit counter-Babel: the scattered nations are now hearing in their own tongues.
2.14: Peter's sermon in 2:14–36 is the most carefully constructed apologetic speech in Acts. Its structure: appeal to LXX citation (Joel 2:28-32), witness testimony (v. 32), Ps 16:8-11 proof (vv. 25-28), Ps 110:1 proof (vv. 34-35), conclusion (v. 36). This is the pesher method of biblical interpretation developed at Qumran (1QpHab): a text → 'this is that' (touto estin to eiremenon) → identifying the historical referent. Peter's pesher identifies the referent as Jesus of Nazareth, crucified and risen.
2.46: Daily temple attendance (proseuchomenoi kata ten horan tes thysias, 3:1 — at the ninth hour prayer, 3:00 pm) alongside house-church eucharist reflects the earliest community's dual allegiance: they remained observant Jews attending temple liturgy while also practicing distinctively Christian fellowship. This dual practice continued until the temple's destruction in 70 CE made it impossible. The breaking of bread kat' oikon (house to house) documents the earliest Christian meeting architecture: private homes, not dedicated buildings.
2.2: A 'violent rushing wind' (ēchos hōsper pheromenēs pnoēs biaias) filling the house evokes the theophanic vocabulary of Sinai: the voice like thunder, the fire, the smoke. In Hebrew, rūaḥ (spirit) is also wind/breath — the Spirit's arrival in wind form is a natural theophanic sign. Ezekiel 37:9 similarly invokes wind as Spirit: 'Come from the four winds, O breath (rūaḥ), and breathe on these slain.' The sound (ēchos) is heard before anything is seen — auditory theophany preceding visual.
2.3: Divided tongues of fire (diamerizomenai glōssai hōsei pyros) settling on each one individually: fire is the OT marker of divine presence (burning bush, Sinai, the kābōd manifestations). The distribution to each individual rather than a collective flame signals that each person, not merely the community corporately, receives the Spirit. The fire-imagery also anticipates John the Baptist's prophecy: 'he will baptize you with the Holy Spirit and fire' (Luke 3:16).
2.4: Speaking in other tongues as the Spirit gave them utterance: the Pentecost glossolalia is specifically identified as recognizable foreign languages (v.6-11), distinguishing it from the ecstatic prayer-tongue of 1 Cor 14. Jewish tradition associated the giving of Torah at Sinai with the word going out in all seventy languages of the nations (Midrash Exodus Rabbah 5:9 — 'the voice divided into seventy languages'). Pentecost reverses Babel (Gen 11) and extends the Sinai revelation to the diaspora.
2.5: Devout Jews (Ioudaioi eulabes) from every nation present for Shavuot: eulabes (devout, careful about religious duty) is a specifically Jewish piety-term — not merely 'religious' in a generic sense but attentive to Torah. The pilgrimage feast drew diaspora Jews to Jerusalem from across the Mediterranean world and the Parthian East. Josephus (Jewish War 6.420-427) estimates millions gathered for pilgrimage festivals; even allowing for exaggeration, Jerusalem was crowded at Shavuot with a precisely multilingual audience.
2.6: Each heard in their own dialect (tē idiai dialektō): dialektos is the regional variety of a language — the Galilean dialect was recognizable to Jerusalemites (cf. 22:2: Paul silences the crowd by switching to the Hebrew/Aramaic dialect). The miracle was specifically linguistic comprehension, not merely emotional experience; the crowd's reaction is cognitive confusion ('how is this?') before spiritual openness. Luke emphasizes the public, verifiable character of the event.
2.7: 'Are not all these who are speaking Galileans?' — Galilee had a reputation in Jerusalem as the cultural periphery: its dialect was recognizable (Peter's accent betrayed him, Mark 14:70), its scholarship suspect (Am ha-aretz, 'people of the land', was a Jerusalem pejorative for rural Torah-ignorance). The disciples who will preach to the whole world are identified by the metropolitan audience as provincial outsiders — a Lukan irony consistent with the Gospel's pattern of reversals.
2.8: The question 'how is it that each of us hears in his own native language?' emphasizes individual comprehension across the gathered crowd. Dialektō appears twice (vv. 6, 8) — Luke is precise that the miracle is of language, not merely of spiritual atmosphere. For diaspora Jews whose Greek might be stronger than their ancestral tongue, being addressed in the language of their home region would be both surprising and authenticating.
2.9: The nations list begins east to west: Parthians, Medes, Elamites, Mesopotamia — the Babylonian and Persian diaspora communities, descendants of the northern and Judean exiles. Josephus (Ant. 11.133) notes extensive Jewish communities in Parthia and Babylonia. These eastern communities maintained strong Jewish identity and regular Jerusalem contact. The list's geographic reach confirms that Pentecost's audience represents the full scope of the diaspora, not merely Greco-Roman Jewry.
2.10: Cappadocia, Pontus, Asia, Phrygia, Pamphylia — the interior and coastal regions of Anatolia (modern Turkey). By Paul's time, Jews had been settled in Anatolia for centuries; the Maccabean correspondence with Rome (1 Macc 15:23) lists Cos, Rhodes, and multiple Anatolian cities as having Jewish communities. 'Egypt and parts of Libya near Cyrene' — Egyptian Jewry was enormous; Philo of Alexandria estimates one million Jews in Egypt. Cyrene (Libya) had a large enough community to have its own synagogue in Jerusalem (Acts 6:9).
2.11: 'Both Jews and proselytes' (Ioudaioi te kai prosēlytoi): proselytes were Gentile converts who had fully joined the Jewish community through circumcision (males) and immersion. They had equal legal standing in synagogue communities; by pilgrimage, they demonstrated full Torah-observance. Cretans and Arabs complete the list's compass: Crete was a known Jewish settlement area; Arabia (the Nabataean kingdom and its environs) bordered Judea. The nations list is not meant to be exhaustive but representative of the known world.
2.12: 'Amazed and perplexed, they asked one another, What does this mean?' (ti thelei touto einai) — the crowd's response is cognitive bewilderment, not emotional transport. Diastazō (perplexed) is the word for genuine intellectual confusion. The question sets up Peter's interpretive speech: Pentecost without explanation is a sign that demands a referent. The crowd's honest question is the rhetorical opening that the apostolic proclamation fills.
2.13: 'They are filled with new wine' (gleukous memestōmenoi eisin): gleukos is sweet, freshly-pressed grape juice or young wine — not wine in the normal sense but the most intoxicating first-run of the vintage. The charge is that the disciples are drunk on the best wine, which might explain unusual behavior. Peter's rebuttal (v.15) is factual: it is only nine in the morning, too early even for festive drinking. The charge is the earliest recorded dismissal of Pentecostal experience as mere intoxication.
2.15: The third hour (about 9:00 am) was too early for intoxication in Jewish and Greco-Roman convention — normal eating began later; festival drinking certainly not at dawn. Peter's temporal argument is a practical refutation: the accusation of drunkenness fails on basic factual grounds. He then turns from refutation to explanation, the classical rhetorical move from refutatio to confirmatio.
2.16: 'This is what was spoken through the prophet Joel' (touto estin to eiremenon dia tou prophētou Iōēl): the touto estin ('this is') formula is the pesher hermeneutic — a method developed at Qumran for identifying the fulfillment of prophetic texts in contemporary events. Peter identifies the Joel passage not as an analogy but as the text-event being fulfilled in real time before the crowd's eyes. This is the founding hermeneutical claim of Christian biblical interpretation: Scripture is fulfilled, not merely illustrated.
2.17: Joel 2:28 (LXX 3:1): 'I will pour out my Spirit on all flesh.' En tais eschatais hēmerais (in the last days) replaces Joel's 'after these things' — a deliberate interpretive expansion identifying Pentecost as the inauguration of the eschatological era. The fourfold social inclusivity — sons and daughters, young men and old men, servants and handmaids — dismantles the social stratifications by which prophetic reception was typically restricted in the OT (to professional prophets, male elders, and recognized seers).
2.18: 'Even on my male slaves and female slaves in those days I will pour out my Spirit' — the inclusion of slaves (douloi kai doulai) in prophetic reception was genuinely radical. In Greco-Roman society, slaves were property without legal or religious standing; in Jewish tradition, slaves had partial Torah-participation but were not expected vehicles of prophetic inspiration. The Spirit's democratization overturns every category by which spiritual authority was restricted to the credentialed, propertied, or male.
2.19: Joel's cosmic signs — blood, fire, columns of smoke — are the theophanic imagery of the Day of the Lord in OT prophetic tradition (Isa 13:10; Ezek 32:7-8; Amos 8:9). Peter quotes them as part of the Joel citation without claiming their literal fulfillment in the Pentecost event itself; they belong to the larger eschatological frame that the Spirit's outpouring inaugurates. First-century apocalyptic texts (1 Enoch, 4 Ezra) had intensified this cosmic-signs vocabulary as standard end-time imagery.
2.20: The 'great and glorious day of the Lord' (hēmera kyriou tēn megalēn kai epiphane) frames the Pentecost outpouring within the trajectory of the Day of YHWH — the OT concept of a decisive divine intervention in history for judgment and salvation. Peter's citation does not announce that the Day of the Lord is already fully accomplished; it announces that the eschatological era has begun, and the full day remains ahead. The Spirit's coming is the down-payment (Eph 1:14 — arrabōn) of what the Day will consummate.
2.21: 'Everyone who calls on the name of the Lord will be saved' — Joel 2:32's promise closes the citation. Pas (everyone) is the universal sweep that the speech's conclusion applies to both Israel and the nations. In context, Paul will cite the same verse in Rom 10:13 to demonstrate the universality of the gospel. The 'name of the Lord' in Joel refers to YHWH; Peter's application to Jesus (v.38: 'be baptized in the name of Jesus Christ') is an implicit Christological identification — calling on Jesus is calling on the name of the Lord.
2.22: 'Jesus of Nazareth — a man attested to you by God' (andra apodedeigmenon apo tou theou eis hymas): the Nazareth identification places Jesus in a specific, verifiable location (cf. John 1:46 — 'can anything good come from Nazareth?'). The attestation through miracles, wonders, and signs (dynamesi kai terasi kai sēmeiois) is the standard OT vocabulary for the legitimizing works that authenticate a prophet (Exod 4:8-9; Deut 13:1-2). Peter addresses an audience who had seen these works.
2.23: 'Delivered up by the definite plan and foreknowledge of God, you put to death through the hands of lawless men': the paradox — divine sovereignty and human responsibility simultaneously affirmed. Hōrismenē boulē kai prognōsei (definite plan and foreknowledge) uses the strongest vocabulary for divine determination; yet 'you put to death' and 'hands of lawless men' (the Romans) are equally affirmed. This is not a tension to be resolved but the NT's characteristic way of holding together providence and guilt.
2.24: 'God raised him up, having loosed the pangs (tas ōdinas) of death' — ōdinas normally means birth-pangs; applied to death, the image may draw on Ps 18:4-5 (LXX: 'snares of death', but the Hebrew ḥebelei mawet — cords/pangs of death — allows the birth-imagery). Resurrection is conceptually a birth out of death; the 'pangs' that held Jesus are the death-grip that God broke. The phrase also echoes Isa 53:11 (he 'saw of the labor of his soul') in the Suffering Servant context.
2.25: Ps 16:8-11 quoted from the LXX, which differs from the Hebrew at v.10 — the LXX reads hosion sou (your holy one) rather than the MT's ḥasidekā (your faithful one), and this LXX version is the form Peter quotes. Peter's argument: David wrote Psalm 16 but died and his tomb is still present (v.29); therefore David cannot be the referent of the psalm. The one who did not see corruption must be someone else — the descendant David anticipated would occupy his throne (v.30).
2.26: Ps 16:9: 'my flesh also will dwell in hope' (sarkos mou kataskēnōsei ep' elpidi): the body's hope is resurrection, not dissolution. In Hellenistic philosophical tradition (especially Platonic), the soul's release from the body was the goal; the LXX-Psalm here affirms bodily hope. Peter's use of the psalm as resurrection-proof demonstrates that resurrection was not a novelty invented for Jesus but the fulfillment of David's own hope.
2.27: Ps 16:10: 'you will not abandon my soul to Hades, nor let your holy one see corruption' (diaphthoran). Hades (the realm of the dead) translates Hebrew sheol; the point is that God's faithful one will not be left in the realm of death permanently. The key word diaphthoran (decay/corruption) is what Peter uses to prove Jesus's resurrection in v.31: unlike David, who did see corruption, Jesus did not — which fulfills the psalm's promise.
2.28: Ps 16:11: 'you have made known to me the paths of life; you will make me full of gladness with your presence' — the psalm closes with resurrection-life in God's presence. This is the positive side of the promise that began with preservation from Hades. The 'paths of life' (hodous zōēs) stand opposite the corruption of death; the psalmist anticipates entrance into the divine presence as the ultimate destination. Peter reads this as the resurrection-path that Jesus walked ahead of David.
2.29: Peter addresses his audience as 'brothers' (andres adelphoi) — the standard address within Jewish assembly. The patriarch David is acknowledged with full respect; the point is not to diminish David but to use his own acknowledged prophecy against the assumption that Ps 16 refers to him. 'His tomb is with us to this day' (to mnēma autou estin en hēmin achri tēs hēmeras tautēs): the Davidic tomb was a known Jerusalem site (Neh 3:16; Josephus, Ant. 7.392-394). The visible, accessible tomb proves David's body did see corruption.
2.30: David as prophet: the OT affirms David's prophetic role (2 Sam 23:1-2; the psalms as Spirit-inspired). Peter invokes the Davidic covenant promise of 2 Sam 7:12-16 and Ps 132:11 — that God swore to set a descendant on David's throne. David 'foresaw and spoke' about the resurrection because he knew his throne would be occupied by a successor who would reign forever, which required overcoming death. This is the hermeneutical basis for reading royal psalms as Christological prophecy.
2.31: 'He foresaw and spoke of the resurrection of the Christ, that he was not abandoned to Hades, and his flesh did not see corruption' — Peter's interpretive conclusion links the Davidic throne-promise to Ps 16's resurrection language. The Christ (Messiah, Anointed One) is the promised Davidic king; his resurrection is the means by which the eternal throne-promise is fulfilled. This is the exegetical heart of the Pentecost sermon and the model for subsequent apostolic preaching in Acts.
2.32: 'This Jesus God raised up, and of that we all are witnesses' — the witnesses claim moves from exegetical proof to personal testimony. The 'we all' (hoi pantes hēmeis) is the assembled eyewitness community. Jewish legal tradition required two or three witnesses (Deut 19:15); the apostolic community constitutes an overwhelming body of witness far beyond the legal minimum. The resurrection, unlike the crucifixion, is not a public event; its authentication depends entirely on witness testimony.
2.33: 'Exalted to the right hand of God' — exalted is the aorist passive of hypsoō, the Johannine exaltation-word. The right hand position echoes Ps 110:1 (cited explicitly in v.34-35) and was the standard Jewish designation for the highest position of honor and power. Having received from the Father 'the promised Holy Spirit', Jesus 'poured out this' — the Pentecost gift is channeled through the exalted Christ, not directly from the Father. Trinitarian structure is implicit: Father promises, Christ pours out, Spirit comes.
2.34: Ps 110:1 — 'The LORD said to my Lord: sit at my right hand until I make your enemies your footstool' — is the most frequently cited OT text in the NT (Matt 22:44; Mark 12:36; Luke 20:42; Acts 2:34-35; 1 Cor 15:25; Eph 1:20; Col 3:1; Heb 1:13; 10:13). Peter uses the same argument structure for Ps 110 as for Ps 16: David wrote it but didn't ascend to heaven himself, so the 'Lord' he addresses must be his descendant. The psalm's 'sitting at the right hand' confirms Jesus's post-resurrection location and authority.
2.35: 'Until I make your enemies your footstool' — the footstool image is from ancient Near Eastern iconography of victorious kings placing their feet on the necks of defeated enemies (Josh 10:24; Ps 47:3). The 'enemies' in the psalm-context are the kings and nations opposing the Anointed (Ps 110:5-6); in the NT application, the enemies include death itself (1 Cor 15:25-26) and all powers hostile to God's reign. The 'until' implies an ongoing process that has begun but is not yet complete.
2.36: 'Let all the house of Israel know with certainty that God has made him both Lord (kyrios) and Christ (christos)' — the sermon's christological thesis. Kyrios is the LXX term for YHWH throughout the OT; applying it to Jesus is the most significant christological move in the speech. Christos (Anointed/Messiah) identifies Jesus as the promised Davidic king. 'This Jesus whom you crucified' — the crucified man and the exalted Lord are the same person; the gap between what they did and who he is is the moral ground for the charge of v.37.
2.37: 'They were pierced to the heart and said... what shall we do?' (katenygesan tēn kardian): katanyssō (pierce, prick, sting) occurs only here in the NT. The image of piercing/pricking suggests a sudden, sharp conviction — the word penetrating like a weapon. This is the fulfillment-in-reverse of the Pentecost swords: instead of tongues of fire going out, conviction comes in. The 'what shall we do?' is the response to genuine forensic conviction — not merely emotional distress but recognition of guilt requiring remedy.
2.38: 'Repent and be baptized every one of you in the name of Jesus Christ for the forgiveness of sins' — the first Christian baptismal formula. The sequence: repentance → baptism → forgiveness → Spirit-gift. Baptism 'in the name of Jesus' distinguishes Christian practice from John's baptism and from Jewish proselyte immersion; the name invoked establishes the mediating authority. The three thousand who respond (v.41) represent one of the two occasions in Acts where a specific conversion number is given (cf. 4:4: five thousand men).
2.39: 'The promise is for you and for your children and for all who are far off' — the promise of Joel 2:32 extended from the immediate audience to their descendants and to the distant Gentiles ('far off' is the standard OT term for the nations, Isa 57:19; Eph 2:13). Hosous an proskalesētai kyrios ho theos hēmōn (as many as the Lord our God calls): the scope is determined by divine call, not ethnic or geographic limit. The 'far off' language would receive its fullest application in Paul's Gentile mission.
2.40: 'Save yourselves from this crooked generation' (apo tēs geneas tēs skolias tautēs): Skolias (crooked, perverse) echoes Deut 32:5 — the wilderness generation that was faithless to YHWH. Peter applies the Deuteronomic generation-language to his contemporaries: they are the new wilderness generation, unfaithful to the God who has now acted in Christ. The call to 'save yourselves' places moral agency on the hearers even while the preceding verses have established divine sovereignty — the two poles Luke consistently holds together.
2.41: Three thousand baptized on the day of Pentecost: Acts' first and most dramatic mass conversion. The number three thousand may intentionally echo Exod 32:28, where three thousand died at the golden calf incident — the anti-Sinai event. At the new Sinai (Pentecost), three thousand receive life instead of death. Whether the parallel is deliberate or coincidental, Luke records it in a way that invites the comparison. The scale signals that the new community is not a small sect but a mass movement.
2.42: The four marks of the Jerusalem community — apostolic teaching, fellowship (koinōnia), breaking of bread, and prayers — became the prototype community description. The apostolic teaching is the distinct curriculum of the risen Christ's instructions; koinōnia encompasses shared property and mutual aid; breaking of bread likely includes eucharistic meals; 'the prayers' may refer to specific prayer services. The definite article ('the prayers') suggests established liturgical forms rather than spontaneous prayer.
2.43: 'Fear came upon every soul, and many wonders and signs were being done through the apostles' — the phobos (fear, awe) that accompanies divine presence in the OT theophanic narratives. The signs and wonders (terata kai sēmeia) continue the verification function they had in Jesus's ministry: they authenticate the apostolic commission in continuity with the authenticated ministry that preceded it. The imperfect tense ('were being done') signals ongoing rather than one-time miraculous activity.
2.44: 'All who believed were together and had all things in common' (koina): the Jerusalem community experiment in voluntary goods-sharing has been compared to both Qumran (yahad — the community's shared property system, 1QS 6:19-22) and Pythagorean philosophical brotherhood. But the Jerusalem model differs: Qumran required permanent property transfer; Acts 5:4 confirms private ownership was retained and sharing was voluntary. The motivation was need-driven rather than ideological — a fluid response to the sudden influx of three thousand new members including many diaspora Jews who had extended their stay.
2.45: 'They were selling their possessions and belongings and distributing the proceeds to all, as any had need' — the distribution follows demonstrated need, not mathematical equality. Kathoti an tis chreian eichen (as each one had need) is the distribution principle that Paul later uses for the collection (2 Cor 8:13-15, citing Exod 16's manna principle). The economic sharing is the material expression of the koinōnia announced in v.42 — shared Spirit produces shared goods.
2.47: 'Having favor with all the people, and the Lord was adding to their number day by day those who were being saved' (hoi sōzomenoi, the ones being saved): sōzomenoi is a present participle — a dynamic, ongoing process of salvation rather than a static class. The community's public favor reflects the early period before Jewish-Christian tensions intensified; the daily additions confirm organic growth rather than episodic revivals. Luke's passive — 'the Lord was adding' — attributes growth to divine agency rather than human strategy.

3.1: The Beautiful Gate (horaia pyle) is not directly identified in first-century sources. The Mishnah (m. Middot 2:3) describes the Nicanor Gate between the Court of Women and the Court of Israel as exceptional in bronze and size; many scholars identify this with Luke's 'Beautiful Gate'. It was the main thoroughfare for Jewish worshippers proceeding inward — a highly visible location for a beggar dependent on the alms-giving piety of pilgrims. The ninth-hour prayer (3:00 pm) was the afternoon Tamid sacrifice, drawing maximum temple traffic.
3.17: Peter's 'you acted in ignorance' (kata agnoian epraxate) corresponds to the Torah's distinction between inadvertent (bishegagah) and high-handed (beyad ramah) sins (Num 15:27-31; Lev 5:15-19). Inadvertent sins had sacrificial remedy; deliberate high-handed sins did not. Peter's appeal gives his audience a path: the crucifixion, done in ignorance, can be covered by the same mercy that covers inadvertent sin — if they now respond to the revelation of who Jesus was.
3.2: The man lame from birth (chōlos ek koilias mētros autou) had been carried to the Beautiful Gate daily. Congenital lameness was incurable by ancient medicine; his forty-plus years of age (v.22 implies the duration of the condition) is emphasized in v.22 to establish the miracle's completeness. Professional beggars positioned themselves at temple gates because alms-giving to the poor was a Torah obligation (Deut 15:7-11) — temple-goers were primed for generosity. The Beautiful Gate's high traffic made it the optimal location.
3.3: The man asked for alms (ērōta eleēmosunēn labein): eleēmosunē (alms) is the material expression of eleos (mercy/steadfast love). In Jewish practice, alms-giving was one of the three pillars of piety alongside prayer and fasting (Matt 6:2-4; Tob 12:8-9). The high priest's household may have been exempt from giving to beggars (since all temple revenue was theoretically already consecrated), but individual worshippers were expected to give. Peter and John's poverty ('silver and gold I do not have') reflects genuine economic reality.
3.4: 'Peter looked directly at him, as did John, and said, Look at us' (atenisas eis auton... blepson eis hēmas): the sustained direct eye contact before miraculous action is a feature of both prophetic and Hellenistic healing narratives (the healer's eyes focusing attention). The command to look is also a demand for undivided attention to what is about to happen — the healing will not be performed on an inattentive recipient. Luke's detail establishes the personal, focused character of apostolic healing distinct from magical formulae.
3.5: The man gives them his full attention (epieichen autois, prosdokōn ti par autōn labein) expecting to receive money. His expectation is material — he does not ask for healing (he may not have known it was possible). The gap between what the man expects (coins) and what he receives (restored legs) is Luke's irony: the greater gift arrives disguised as a refusal of the lesser. This pattern recurs throughout Luke-Acts: divine provision exceeds human asking.
3.6: 'In the name of Jesus Christ of the Nazarene, walk' (en tō onomati Iēsou Christou tou Nazōraiou, peripatei): the name as the vehicle of healing power establishes that Peter is an agent, not the source. The Nazarene title (Nazōraios) is the precise identification used at the trial and crucifixion (John 18:5, 7; 19:19); Peter uses the name the authorities had inscribed on the cross as the healing power. The irony is compressed: Rome's label of execution becomes the apostles' invocation of life.
3.7: Peter took him by the right hand and raised him up (piasas auton... ēgeiren auton): egeirō (raise up) is the resurrection verb throughout the NT. The physical action — taking the right hand and lifting — mirrors the gesture Paul used for Eutychus (Acts 20:10, who fell from the window) and Elijah/Elisha for the dead children they raised. Luke's use of the resurrection vocabulary for this healing implicitly connects healing and resurrection as expressions of the same divine life-giving power.
3.8: 'Jumping up, he stood and began to walk, and he entered the temple walking and jumping and praising God' — the threefold physical response (stand, walk, enter) followed by jumping (exallomenos) and praise reverses the congenital condition with exuberant completeness. The image echoes Isa 35:6: 'then the lame will leap like a deer' — one of the restoration-of-creation signs associated with the eschatological era. Luke presents the healing as more than a medical miracle: it is a sign that Isaiah's promised restoration has begun.
3.9: 'All the people saw him walking and praising God' — the public character of the healing is emphasized: the gate was the most visible point of temple entry, and the man who had been there daily as a well-known fixture was now walking and leaping. The eyewitness public is the community of attestation. Luke's careful establishment of witnesses — this man known to have been lame from birth (v.10) — is consistent with his historiographical method: the miraculous is not private but publicly verifiable.
3.10: 'They recognized him as the one who sat at the Beautiful Gate of the temple, asking for alms, and they were filled with wonder and amazement (thambous kai ekstaseōs) at what had happened to him' — thamboa and ekstasin are the standard Lukan reaction to the inexplicable divine act. Ekstasis (standing outside oneself, ecstasy) appears at the healing of the paralytic (Luke 5:26), at the empty tomb (Mark 16:8), and at Peter's Joppa trance (Acts 10:10). The crowd's amazement is credentialing evidence that the healing was genuine and recognized.
3.11: Solomon's Portico (Stoa tou Solomōnos) was the colonnade along the eastern wall of the outer court (Court of the Gentiles). Josephus (Ant. 20.220-222; Jewish War 5.185) describes it as the only structure surviving from Solomon's time, or as a Herodian reconstruction on older foundations. It was a known gathering place for teachers: Jesus taught there (John 10:23), and the early community met there regularly (Acts 5:12). Its eastern orientation was associated with the rising sun and messianic expectation (Ezek 43:1-4).
3.12: 'Men of Israel, why are you amazed at this? Or why do you stare at us, as if by our own power or godliness we had made him walk?' — Peter's denial of personal credit is the standard prophetic disclaimer (cf. Elisha: 'as the LORD lives, before whom I stand', 2 Kgs 5:16). The crowd's amazement is misdirected toward the instruments; Peter redirects to the agent. The two terms — dynamis (power) and eusebeia (godliness/piety) — address both the supernatural and the moral aspects of the crowd's possible misconceptions.
3.13: 'The God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob, the God of our fathers' — the threefold patriarchal formula (Exod 3:6, 15; spoken at the burning bush) establishes continuity between the God who acts in the healing and the God of Israel's entire history. 'Has glorified his servant Jesus' (edoxasen ton paida autou Iēsoun): pais (servant/child) echoes the Servant-vocabulary of Isa 52:13 ('my servant will be exalted and lifted up'). The glorification through resurrection is the direct reversal of the crowd's handing him over.
3.14: 'You denied the Holy and Righteous One (ton hagion kai dikaion) and asked for a murderer to be granted to you' — the Barabbas reference (Luke 23:18-19). Hagios (holy) and dikaios (righteous/just) are Messianic throne titles with OT weight: tsaddiq (righteous) is the Isaianic Servant's description (Isa 53:11). The exchange — the Holy and Righteous One for a murderer — is the moral inversion that defines the crucifixion. Peter's charge is precise: they chose wrongly in the specific legal moment that mattered.
3.15: 'You killed the Author of life (ton archēgon tēs zōēs apeктeinate), whom God raised from the dead. We are witnesses of this' — archēgos (pioneer, originator, leader) in Acts is used twice for Jesus: here (3:15) and in 5:31. The word was used for the founder of a city or colony — the first one through the gates who establishes the settlement. Peter's compressed paradox is the sharpest in Acts: they destroyed the source of life, and the source of life defeated their destruction. The witness claim follows immediately: not an abstract theological assertion but personal testimony.
3.16: 'By faith in his name, his name has made this man strong' — the double onoma (name) and double pistis (faith) in this verse are unusual; the syntax is compressed almost to the point of opacity. The onoma here carries the full Semitic weight: the name is the power and person concentrated. The faith is not the lame man's (he expected money, not healing) but the faith of those who invoked the name — or the faith the name itself generates (hē pistis hē di autou: the faith through him). Either reading credits Christ as the healing agent.
3.18: 'What God foretold by the mouth of all the prophets, that his Christ would suffer, he thus fulfilled' — the claim that 'all the prophets' predicted the Messiah's suffering is a hermeneutical claim about the whole OT, not a list of specific proof-texts. First-century Judaism generally did not read the suffering Servant texts (Isa 53) or the royal lament psalms (Ps 22, 69) as pointing to a suffering Messiah; the dominant expectation was of a victorious king. Peter's pesher — reading the whole prophetic tradition as a network pointing to the Messiah's passion — represents the early church's major interpretive contribution.
3.19: 'Repent therefore and turn back (epistrepsate), that your sins may be blotted out, that times of refreshing may come from the presence of the Lord' — epistrephō (turn/return) is the LXX equivalent of Hebrew shub, the covenant-return vocabulary of the prophets (Hos 6:1; Jer 3:12-14; Zech 1:3). Anapsyxis (refreshing) is a hapax in the NT — a rare word suggesting renewal after exhaustion (cf. Exod 23:12 LXX: 'on the seventh day you shall rest and be refreshed'). The refreshing is both personal (forgiveness) and cosmic (the eschatological restoration of v.21).
3.20: 'That he may send the Christ appointed for you, Jesus' (aposteilē ton prokecheirismenon hymin Christon Iēsoun): prokecheirismenon (appointed/designated beforehand) uses the same verb as Paul's Damascus commission (Acts 22:14; 26:16). Jesus is the pre-appointed Christ who has already come and will be sent again. The parousia here is conditional on Israel's repentance — a theological claim that has generated debate about whether national repentance accelerates the end-time (cf. Rom 11:25-26). The sending language echoes the prophetic tradition of divine envoys sent repeatedly to Israel.
3.21: 'Heaven must receive until the time of restoration of all things' (apokatastaseōs pantōn): apokatastasis (restoration to original condition) appears only here in the NT in this specific phrase. In Jewish tradition it referred to the end-time renewal when all things would be set right (the ingathering of exiles, cosmic restoration, the defeat of evil). Origen later used this term for universal salvation, but here the context is the OT prophetic vision — everything spoken through the holy prophets from old being restored to its proper order.
3.22: Deut 18:15 cited: 'Moses said, The Lord God will raise up for you a prophet like me from your brothers. You shall listen to him in everything he tells you' — the Mosaic prophet-expectation was a major Second Temple hope. 1 Macc 4:46 and 14:41 defer certain decisions 'until a prophet should arise'; Qumran expected 'the prophet' alongside the priestly and royal Messiahs (1QS 9:11). Peter identifies Jesus as this prophet. The command 'listen to him' echoes the Transfiguration voice (Luke 9:35: 'hear him'), connecting the prophetic commission to that scene.
3.23: 'Every soul who does not listen to that prophet shall be destroyed from the people' (Deut 18:19; Lev 23:29) — the consequence of rejecting Moses's appointed successor is excision from the covenant community (exolethreuthēsetai ek tou laou). Peter applies this to his audience's response to Jesus: rejecting Jesus is rejecting the prophet Moses appointed, which carries the Torah's own threatened consequence. The warning is not ethnic judgment against Israel but a covenant-faithfulness appeal: the same Torah that established Israel demands loyalty to the one Moses foretold.
3.24: 'And all the prophets who have spoken, from Samuel and those who came after him, also proclaimed these days' — Samuel is the beginning of the classical prophetic tradition; the inclusion of 'all the prophets from Samuel' encompasses the entire prophetic canon from the period of the monarchy. Peter is arguing for a continuous prophetic consensus: the days of Messiah's coming have been the subject of all genuine prophetic speech since Israel's prophetic office began. This totalizing claim about prophetic Scripture is the basis for the Christian OT.
3.25: 'You are the sons of the prophets and of the covenant that God made with your fathers, saying to Abraham, And in your offspring shall all the families of the earth be blessed' (Gen 22:18; 12:3) — the Abrahamic covenant is the theological warrant for the Gentile mission already embedded in Peter's speech to a Jewish audience. Huioi... tēs diathēkēs (sons of the covenant) is a Semitism for covenant-members. The quotation of Gen 22:18 in en tō spermati sou (in your seed/offspring) is the same text Paul exegetes in Gal 3:16 as referring to Christ.
3.26: 'God, having raised up his servant, sent him to you first, to bless you by turning each of you from your wickedness' (en tō apostrephein hekaston apo tōn ponēriōn hymōn): the servant language (pais) echoes Isa 52:13 again. The 'first' (prōton) is the Pauline priority of Rom 1:16: 'to the Jew first and also to the Greek'. The blessing of the nations through Abraham's seed — just quoted — begins with Israel's own conversion. The sequence first-to-Israel then-to-the-nations is built into the Abrahamic covenant's own structure.

4.36: Barnabas ('son of encouragement' — Luke's translation of Aramaic Bar-Nabas; though some argue the Aramaic root is naba/prophet, making him 'son of a prophet') is introduced as a Levite from Cyprus. Levites theoretically could not own land (Num 18:20), but Diaspora conditions had long made this impractical; his property sale represents voluntary conformity to the community ideal rather than strict Torah compliance. He becomes the key bridge figure between Paul and the Jerusalem apostles (9:27; 11:22-26) and co-missionary on the first journey.
4.1: The captain of the temple guard (stratēgos tou hierou) was a high-ranking priestly official second only to the high priest, responsible for security and order in the temple precincts. Josephus mentions the role frequently (Ant. 20.6.2; J.W. 2.17.2). The Sadducees (a priestly aristocratic party) and the temple captain respond together because Peter's proclamation in Solomon's Portico threatens both the institutional order they maintain and the theological framework they hold (Sadducees denied resurrection, v.2).
4.2: The Sadducees' specific objection — that the apostles were 'proclaiming in Jesus the resurrection of the dead' (katangellousin en tō Iēsou tēn anastasin) — identifies the core theological dispute. The Sadducees accepted only the Pentateuch as binding and denied bodily resurrection (cf. Mark 12:18-27; Acts 23:8). The apostles' proclamation that Jesus had risen was simultaneously an empirical claim (a specific man rose) and a theological provocation (resurrection is real and has begun). For the Sadducean leadership, both the claim and the implication were intolerable.
4.3: Evening arrest meant an overnight detention; Jewish law (m. Sanhedrin 4:1) required capital cases to begin and conclude before nightfall and never on a feast-day eve. Holding Peter and John until the next day kept the matter within normal judicial procedure while preventing the crowd from disrupting proceedings. Josephus describes similar tactical delays in politically sensitive arrests (Ant. 14.9.4).
4.4: 'Five thousand men' (andrōn hōsei pentakischilioi) — the count of andres (adult males) was the standard census-unit in antiquity; the total community including women would be larger. The rapid growth from 3,000 at Pentecost (2:41) to 5,000 after the lame man's healing illustrates Luke's consistent pattern of persecution followed by growth: the arrest of the leaders does not slow the mission but accelerates it. The Jerusalem church at this point represents a significant fraction of the city's Jewish population.
4.5: The convening of 'rulers, elders and scribes' (archontes, presbyteroi, grammateis) describes the Sanhedrin's three constituent orders: (1) chief priests and high-priestly families (archontes), (2) tribal/lay elders (presbyteroi), and (3) legal scholars/scribes (grammateis). The Sanhedrin (synedrion = sitting-together) was the supreme Jewish council of Jerusalem, numbering 71 members according to m. Sanhedrin 1:6, with both judicial and legislative authority over Jewish life in Palestine.
4.6: Annas had served as high priest from 6-15 CE before being deposed by the Romans; his son-in-law Caiaphas held the office 18-36 CE. Roman policy required annual high-priest appointments but Jewish tradition recognized the position as lifelong; Annas retained enormous de facto authority (cf. John 18:13, 24 where Jesus is brought to Annas first). The listing of Annas before Caiaphas reflects this reality: Annas was the effective head of the high-priestly clan that dominated Jerusalem's religious politics for decades.
4.7: 'By what power or what name' (en poia dynamei ē en poiō onomati) — the question combines two distinct categories: dynamis (power, the miraculous force employed) and onoma (name, the authority invoked). In ancient Jewish and Hellenistic magical practice, both components were essential: the power behind an act and the name by which that power was accessed. The Sanhedrin's question is simultaneously a legal investigation and a theological probe: whose authority do you invoke, and by what right?
4.8: 'Filled with the Holy Spirit' (plēstheis pneumatos hagiou) — Luke's pneumatological formula for empowered speech throughout Acts (2:4; 4:8, 31; 6:3, 5; 7:55; 9:17; 11:24; 13:9, 52). The address 'rulers and elders of the people' (archontes tou laou kai presbyteroi) uses the formal Sanhedrin designation from v.5, positioning Peter's response as an official defense (apologia) before the highest Jewish court — exactly the setting Jesus prophesied in Luke 12:11-12 ('when they bring you before... the authorities... the Holy Spirit will teach you what to say').
4.9: 'An act of kindness shown to a man who was lame' (euergesiai anthrōpou asthenous) — euergesia is the Greek civic-benefaction term: the vocabulary of public benefactors who performed services for the city. Peter reframes the Sanhedrin's legal inquiry as a question about a beneficent deed. The healed man's presence throughout the proceedings (v.14 — 'standing there with them') provides the visible, living evidence that cannot be dismissed. Josephus uses euergesia language in describing miraculous healers, situating Peter's vocabulary in contemporary religious discourse.
4.10: 'Jesus Christ of Nazareth, whom you crucified but whom God raised from the dead' — Peter names the precise historical acts of two specific agents: the Sanhedrin's action (crucifixion) and God's action (resurrection). The geographical designation 'of Nazareth' (Nazōraios) appears in the accusation placard on the cross (John 19:19) and throughout Acts as a precise identification tag that prevents confusion. The naming of the healers' identity follows the legal convention of specifying the full name and origin of the authority invoked.
4.11: Psalm 118:22 ('the stone the builders rejected has become the cornerstone') was already a recognized messianic proof-text in early Judaism (cf. 1QS 8:7-8 from Qumran; 1 En. 62:3). Jesus himself quoted it against the Sanhedrin (Luke 20:17). The cornerstone (kephalē gōnias — head of the corner) is the foundation stone that determines the building's alignment. Luke's insertion of the Psalm citation into Peter's Sanhedrin speech maps the rejection-and-vindication pattern onto the specific legal situation: the Sanhedrin plays the role of the builders who rejected, God plays the role of the architect who vindicated.
4.12: 'Salvation is found in no one else, for there is no other name under heaven given to mankind by which we must be saved' — the universalism of the claim ('no other name') stands in deliberate tension with the Jewish particularism of the Sanhedrin setting. The dei (must be saved — divine necessity) frames salvation through Jesus's name as the divinely ordained mechanism, not one option among many. In the first-century context where Hellenistic religion offered a pluralistic market of saving deities, this exclusive claim was culturally radical as well as theologically decisive.
4.13: 'Unschooled, ordinary men' (agrammatoi kai idiōtai) — agrammatoi are those without formal scribal/legal training (not illiterate, since most Jewish males could read); idiōtai are laymen without professional expertise (in rhetorical contexts, non-specialists). The Sanhedrin's astonishment at the quality of Peter and John's reasoning despite their lack of formal training mirrors the Galilean fishermen's surprise role throughout Luke-Acts. Josephus mentions the Galileans' reputation for rough directness in contrast to the sophistication of Jerusalem's educated classes (Vita 37).
4.14: The healed man's presence with the apostles (the same man who had lain at the Beautiful Gate for years — Acts 3:2; known to all Jerusalem, v.16) was epistemically decisive: no verbal defense was needed or possible against a living miracle. Josephus describes similar moments in legal proceedings where physical evidence (living witnesses, cured bodies) silenced legal objection (Ant. 17.6.4). The Sanhedrin's inability to deny the sēmeion (notable sign/miracle, v.16) shapes their subsequent strategy from legal refutation to social suppression.
4.15: The Sanhedrin's deliberation 'among themselves' (anakrinantes... en tō synedriō) follows normal judicial procedure: the council conferred privately before issuing a ruling. The deliberative process (vv.15-17) shows the Sanhedrin functioning as a legal body recognizing the constraints of evidence: they cannot deny the sign (v.16), cannot punish the apostles without popular backlash (v.21), and cannot allow the movement to continue (v.17). The solution — a non-punitive injunction — was the least-damage option available given their legal and political constraints.
4.16: 'A notable sign' (gnōston sēmeion — a known/recognized miracle) — the Sanhedrin's private admission that a genuine miracle occurred is historically significant: the opponents of the apostles do not deny the miracle but attempt to suppress its interpretation. This pattern — conceding the fact while disputing the meaning — appears in rabbinic discussions of Jesus's miracles (b. Sanhedrin 43a, where Jesus's miracles are attributed to sorcery). The Sanhedrin's own acknowledgment of the sign is Luke's strongest authentication of the healing narrative.
4.17: The injunction 'not to speak or teach at all in the name of Jesus' — mē phenggesthai mēde didaskein epi tō onomati tou Iēsou — uses phthengesthai (to produce sound, to utter — a stronger term than ordinary speech) combined with didaskein (to teach). The double prohibition covers both informal speech and formal instruction. The 'name' (onoma) is the operative legal concept: invoking Jesus's name in healing, preaching, or teaching is what the injunction targets. The focus on the name reflects the Sanhedrin's understanding that authority-claims made in Jesus's name were the movement's operational mechanism.
4.18: The formal command (parēngeilan... mē phenggesthai mēde didaskein) is a legal injunction (paraggelia — an official order, the same word used for military commands and magistrates' directives). Peter and John's response in vv.19-20 constitutes a public refusal to comply — a dangerous legal position that the Sanhedrin could not immediately punish because of popular support (v.21). The dilemma of complying with human authority vs. divine command will recur in 5:29.
4.19: 'Which is right in God's eyes: to listen to you or to him?' (ei dikaion estin enōpion tou theou hymōn akouein mallon ē tou theou krinete) — Peter's formulation follows the Socratic pattern of appealing to a higher moral authority than the current tribunal: the question of what is dikaion (right/just) before God supersedes what is legal before the Sanhedrin. Socrates made a similar appeal at his trial (Plato, Apology 29d — 'I shall obey the god rather than you'). The structural parallel was likely intentional in Luke's presentation of Peter as a philosopher-martyr type.
4.20: 'We cannot help speaking about what we have seen and heard' (ou dynametha hēmeis ha eidamen kai ēkousamen mē lalein) — the combination of eyewitness categories (eidamen = what we saw; ēkousamen = what we heard) is the standard formula for legally valid testimony in antiquity: two senses, two modes of perception. Peter and John claim not theological conviction but empirical witness — they are reporting what happened, and reporting it under legal compulsion would be impossible. The ou dynametha (we are not able/cannot) frames the speech as a necessity, not a preference.
4.21: 'All the people were praising God for what had happened' — popular support was the Sanhedrin's primary constraint throughout Acts 1-6. The laos (the people of Israel as a covenant body, distinct from the ethnē/Gentiles) who support the apostles represent the same political reality that had constrained the Sanhedrin from arresting Jesus during the temple days (Luke 19:47-48; 20:19). The healed man being 'over forty years old' (v.22) adds to the evidentiary weight: a disability of four decades' standing, known to all Jerusalem, had been instantly removed.
4.22: The man's age ('over forty years old') serves several narrative functions: it establishes the severity and duration of the disability (the longer the condition, the greater the miracle), it confirms that the healing could not be dismissed as psychosomatic or temporary (four decades of atrophied muscles restored instantly), and it gives the readers a verifiable detail that anchors the account in historical memory. Luke's characteristic precision with numbers (2:41 — three thousand; 4:4 — five thousand; 27:37 — 276 people) reflects his stated historiographical method (Luke 1:1-4).
4.23: 'Went back to their own people' (pros tous idious) — the community of believers to whom Peter and John returned is the Jerusalem church assembled somewhere near the temple. The full report of 'what the chief priests and the elders had said' (hosa pros autous hoi archiereis kai hoi presbyteroi eipan) triggers the corporate prayer of vv.24-30. The pattern: individual apostolic action → corporate response → communal prayer → divine confirmation (the shaking of the place, v.31). Luke consistently presents the early community as a praying corporate body rather than a loose collection of individuals.
4.24: 'Sovereign Lord (Despota), you who made the heavens and the earth and the sea and everything in them' — Despota is the absolute-sovereignty title for God, contrasting with Kyrios (Lord, the relational covenant-name). The prayer's opening creation-invocation ('maker of heaven and earth and sea and everything in them') cites Exod 20:11 and Psalm 146:6 verbatim from the LXX — the community prays in scripture-words, invoking the God who controls all creation to act within the specific historical crisis they face. The creation claim is the basis for the prayer: the One who made everything can act in everything.
4.25: The prayer attributes Psalm 2 to 'your servant our father David' (tou paidos sou Dauid) through the Holy Spirit's mouth — a claim about the nature of scriptural inspiration. The same formulaic attribution appears in the DSS (4QFlor 1:11-13 attributes Psalm 2 to David) and Mishnah citations. Psalm 2 was widely used in early Judaism as a messianic text: the 'nations raging' and 'rulers gathering against the LORD and his anointed' (v.26 — kata tou Kyriou kai kata tou Christou autou) provided the interpretive frame for understanding the crucifixion as anticipated in scripture.
4.26: The application of Psalm 2's 'kings of the earth' (hoi basileis tēs gēs) and 'rulers' (hoi archontes) to the specific historical cast of the crucifixion — Herod Antipas (tetrarch, v.27), Pontius Pilate (Roman governor), Gentiles (Roman soldiers), and 'the people of Israel' (the Jerusalem crowd) — is a remarkably precise historical-typological reading. Luke 23:6-12 records Herod and Pilate's collaboration during Jesus's trial; the prayer's citation applies the psalm to a scene documented in the Gospel. The application method is pesher-style exegesis (contemporary-historical fulfillment of ancient text), the same technique used at Qumran.
4.27: 'Herod and Pontius Pilate met together with the Gentiles and the people of Israel' — the fourfold listing of the crucifixion's human agents (Herod Antipas, Pilate, Gentiles/Romans, and Jewish crowd) maps perfectly onto the Psalm 2 categories (kings + rulers + nations + peoples). Luke 23:6-12 narrates the Herod-Pilate collaboration; the prayer in Acts 4 interprets it through the psalm. The theological claim is significant: all human agents who participated in the crucifixion were, without knowing it, fulfilling a predetermined divine script.
4.28: 'They did what your power and will had decided beforehand should happen' (poiēsai hosa hē cheir sou kai hē boulē sou proōrisen genesthai) — the proorismos (predetermination/foreordaining) of the crucifixion as an act of divine boulē (will/counsel) is the early church's theodicy for the cross: God did not fail to prevent it but used it as the instrument of redemption. The same concept appears in Acts 2:23 ('handed over by God's deliberate plan and foreknowledge') and reflects the early church's primary theological task: explaining how the death of the Messiah was consistent with divine sovereignty.
4.29: 'Enable your servants to speak your word with great boldness' (parrēsia) — parrēsia (bold free-speech, speaking without concealment) was the Athenian democratic virtue of frank public speech. In the context of a legal injunction commanding silence, requesting parrēsia is a prayer for the freedom to disobey the Sanhedrin's order. The prayer does not request the removal of the threat but the courage to face it — a pattern of petition (not for relief but for endurance) that appears throughout the Psalms (Ps 27:1; 31:1; 56:4, 11) and Paul's prison letters (Phil 1:20; 4:11-13).
4.30: 'Stretch out your hand to heal and perform signs and wonders through the name of your holy servant Jesus' — the imagery of God's 'outstretched hand' (en tō tēn cheira ekteinein se) is the Exodus vocabulary for divine intervention: the ten plagues are enacted through God's extended hand (Exod 3:20; 4:4; 7:5; 9:3; Deut 4:34; 5:15; 7:19). The prayer invokes the Exodus God to perform new-exodus signs through Jesus's name, explicitly linking the apostolic healing ministry to the foundational narrative of Israel's liberation from Egypt.
4.31: The place being 'shaken' (ekaleuthe ho topos) after prayer is a theophany-sign throughout the OT: earthquake accompanies divine presence at Sinai (Exod 19:18; Ps 68:8), in prophetic visions (Isa 6:4; Ezek 38:19-20), and at the resurrection (Matt 28:2). The shaking of the prayer-meeting location fulfills the petition of v.29-30: God answers their prayer for boldness with a physical sign of divine presence. 'They were all filled with the Holy Spirit' — the repetition of the Pentecost-formula (2:4) signals that this post-arrest prayer meeting is a second Pentecostal filling for the same community.
4.32: 'All the believers were one in heart and mind' (tou de plēthous tōn pisteusantōn ēn kardia kai psychē mia) — the idealized description of the community in 4:32-35 is one of Luke's three community-summaries (2:42-47; 4:32-35; 5:12-16). The phrase 'one in heart and mind' (kardia kai psychē mia) echoes Aristotle's description of perfect friendship (NE 9.8 — 'a friend is another self') and Deuteronomy's 'love God with all your heart and soul' (Deut 6:5). The community-of-goods arrangement described here is not compulsory communism (Peter's rebuke of Ananias in 5:4 confirms that property remained private) but voluntary sharing.
4.33: 'God's grace was so powerfully at work in them all' (charis te megalē ēn epi pantas autous) — charis here functions in two registers: the theological sense (divine favor) and the social-political sense (favor/goodwill in the community). Luke's usage parallels the use of charis for favor both with God and with people (Luke 2:52 — Jesus grew in wisdom and in charis with God and people). The community's social cohesion and external reputation are here attributed to the same grace that produces their theological unity: charis is the operative force in both dimensions.
4.34: 'There were no needy persons among them' (gar endees tis en en autois) — the verbal echo of Deuteronomy 15:4 ('there should be no poor among you') is deliberate: the messianic community instantiates the Deuteronomic ideal for Israel's covenant life. The mechanism (selling land and houses, distributing to anyone who had need) resembles the Essene community's sharing arrangements described by Josephus (J.W. 2.8.3-4) — both movements response to the same Deuteronomic ideal, though with different institutional structures. The voluntary and occasional nature ('from time to time' — hosoi ktētores... epipraskon) distinguishes this from the Essene's permanent communal ownership.
4.35: The 'apostles' feet' (para tous podas tōn apostolōn) as the receiving point for the community's funds reflects the ancient custom of placing gifts at a leader's feet as an act of submission and honor (Gideon receiving gold earrings in Judg 8:24-25; tribute placed at the feet of rulers throughout ancient Near Eastern imagery). The apostolic administration of the common fund is consistent with the Twelve's role as community leaders whose authorization legitimizes both the giving and the distribution. Acts will later (6:1-6) show this arrangement requiring structural adjustment as the community grows.
4.37: The field sale by Barnabas (v.36-37) provides the paradigmatic positive example of the community practice just described — immediately before the negative counter-example of Ananias and Sapphira (5:1-11). Luke's pairing of exemplary and cautionary cases (Barnabas // Ananias, Simon Magus // the lame man) is a consistent narrative technique. Barnabas's Levitical identity (a Levite from Cyprus) is noteworthy: Levites technically were not to own inherited land in Israel (Num 18:20-24; Deut 10:9), but diaspora Levites owned property in their diaspora locations without this restriction applying.

5.34: Gamaliel I (the Elder) was one of the most respected Pharisaic teachers of the early first century (Paul studied under him, 22:3). The Mishnah (m. Avot 1:16; m. Sanhedrin 2:6) preserves traditions of his teachings. His advice — wait and see whether this movement is from God — represents the classical Pharisaic strategy of avoiding premature judgment. His two historical examples: Theudas and Judas the Galilean. Josephus (Ant. 20.97-98) places Theudas under Fadus (44-46 CE), creating a chronological problem since this Gamaliel speech is set ca. 30-32 CE. Either Luke has a different Theudas, or Josephus's Theudas is the same person misdated, or there was an earlier failed Theudas movement.
5.1: The Ananias and Sapphira episode (5:1-11) immediately follows the paradigmatic community sharing of 4:32-37. The structural juxtaposition — community ideal (4:32-35), positive example (4:36-37 Barnabas), negative counter-example (5:1-11) — is Luke's moral-exemplary technique, common in Hellenistic historical writing (exempla / paradeigmata). Ananias (Ḥananyah — 'YHWH is gracious') and Sapphira (Shappirah — 'beautiful' in Aramaic) are Jewish names; the couple are members of the covenant community, not outsiders. Their sin is internal — covenant-community betrayal, not pagan opposition.
5.2: 'With his wife's full knowledge he kept back part of the money' (enosphisato apo tēs timēs syneidyias kai tēs gynaikos) — nosphizomai (to pilfer, to keep back for oneself) is the LXX word for Achan's keeping back of devoted goods at Jericho (Josh 7:1 LXX — enosphisanto); Luke's word-choice signals that the Ananias episode is a new-Achan story. Achan's sin (withholding from ḥerem/the devoted thing) is the defining act of covenant-betrayal that disrupted Israel's conquest. The parallel frames the Jerusalem community as a new covenant body whose integrity is similarly at stake.
5.3: 'Satan has so filled your heart' (ti hoti eplērōsen ho Satanas tēn kardian sou) — the direct attribution of Ananias's action to Satanic influence parallels Judas's betrayal ('Satan entered into Judas,' Luke 22:3). The Satan-filling language does not remove human moral responsibility (v.4 — 'you have not lied to men but to God' confirms Ananias's culpability); it frames the deception as a manifestation of the cosmic adversary's work within the covenant community. 'To lie to the Holy Spirit' (pseusasthai se to pneuma to hagion) identifies the Spirit as the community's governing presence — lying to the community's shared fund is lying to the Spirit who inhabits the community.
5.4: 'It belonged to you... wasn't the money at your disposal?' — Peter's twofold rhetorical affirmation establishes that the sin was not failure to donate but deception about the donation. The property was Ananias's own before the sale and the proceeds were at his disposal after (en tē sē exousia hypērchen); no compulsion existed. The Qumran community's rule on property retention provides an illuminating contrast: the Damascus Document (CD 9:13-15) mandated full disclosure of retained property but allowed staged donation over multiple years. Luke's account implies no such staged system; the Jerusalem community's sharing was voluntary and unregulated, making deception rather than retention the offense.
5.5: Ananias's instant death (exepsyxen — breathed out, expired) when hearing Peter's judgment parallels the immediate death of Achan's household at Jericho (Josh 7:24-26) and Uzzah's death for touching the ark (2 Sam 6:6-7). Luke does not speculate on the mechanism — natural, miraculous, or divine fiat — but records the fact and its effect: 'great fear seized all who heard' (phobos megas). The phobos megas formula appears at every major divine-action event in Luke-Acts (1:12; 2:43; 5:5, 11; 19:17) as the appropriate human response to immediate divine presence and action.
5.6: 'Young men (neoteroi) came forward, wrapped up his body' — the neoteroi (younger men, the junior members of the community) perform the burial — the customary role of the young in Jewish burial practice. Jewish custom required burial on the day of death whenever possible (m. Sanhedrin 6:5; Deut 21:23 — 'you shall not leave his body... overnight but shall bury him the same day'). The speed (no time to notify Sapphira, who arrives three hours later knowing nothing) is consistent with this practice.
5.7: 'About three hours later his wife came in, not knowing what had happened' — Sapphira's ignorance of her husband's death (three hours is enough time for the burial of v.6 but apparently not enough for news to reach her) provides the narrative occasion for Peter's separate test and judgment. The three-hour gap may reflect the distances involved in the Jerusalem community's spread across the city, or the community's deliberate decision not to inform her. Peter's question ('Tell me, is this the price...?') constitutes both a test and a judgment-scenario: she has an opportunity to tell the truth.
5.8: Peter's question ('is this the price you and Ananias got for the land?') is a test offering Sapphira the opportunity to break with her husband's deception. The couple's 'full knowledge' (syneiduias, v.2) of the deception is retested now in the husband's absence — will she maintain the lie without him? Her confirmation of the false price (nai, tosoutou — 'yes, that much') is the moment of judgment. The judicial structure (accusation, opportunity to speak, judgment, execution) mirrors formal legal proceedings.
5.9: 'How could you conspire to test the Spirit of the Lord?' (ti hoti synephōnēthē hymin peirasai to pneuma Kyriou) — synephōnēthē (to agree-together, to conspire) is the conspiracy-word: the couple's agreement to deceive constitutes a coordinated test of the Holy Spirit's capacity to detect falsehood. 'Testing the Spirit of the Lord' (peirasai to pneuma Kyriou) echoes Israel's testing of God in the wilderness (Exod 17:2, 7; Num 14:22; Ps 95:9; Heb 3:8-9) — the deliberate provocation of a known divine presence to see if it will actually act.
5.10: Sapphira's death 'at Peter's feet' (pros tous podas autou) exactly mirrors the location of the community's financial offerings (4:35 — 'laid at the apostles' feet'). The spatial irony is Luke's: the location of voluntary giving becomes the location of involuntary judgment. The young men's return to find her dead and their burial of her alongside her husband completes the chiastic structure: the couple who conspired together in deception die apart (three hours between the deaths) but are buried together (pros ton andra autēs — beside her husband).
5.11: 'Great fear seized the whole church (ekklēsia) and all who heard about these events' — this is the first use of ekklēsia in Acts (the term that will become the standard word for the Christian community worldwide). The term had both a civic sense (the democratic assembly of citizens in a Greek polis, Acts 19:39-41) and an LXX sense (the assembly/congregation of Israel, used 73x in the LXX). Luke's use here positions the Jerusalem community as both a civic assembly and the continuation of Israel's covenant assembly. The phobos megas that falls on both the church and the wider community mirrors the fear after Pentecost (2:43).
