5.12: 'Solomon's Colonnade' (stoa Solomōnos) — the eastern portico of the outer temple court, associated with Solomon's original temple and retained in Herod's rebuilding. Josephus describes it as the oldest structure remaining from Solomon's period (Ant. 15.11.3; 20.9.7). The portico provided covered space for large gatherings and was the site of Jesus's winter teaching (John 10:23) and the early community's daily assembly. The apostles' use of the Solomon's Colonnade creates continuity with both Jesus's temple ministry and Israel's longest-standing architectural tradition.
5.13: 'No one else dared join them, even though they were highly regarded by the people' — the tension between being 'highly regarded' (emegalynen autous ho laos — the people were magnifying them) and no outsider 'daring to join' (tolma kollasthai autois) reflects the community's ambiguous social status after the Ananias episode. The 'fear' of v.11 had a deterrent effect on casual adherents; the price of entry (genuine commitment under divine scrutiny) was now visible. Despite this deterrent, v.14 reports continued numerical growth — a distinction between casual association and genuine membership.
5.14: 'More and more men and women believed in the Lord and were added to their number' — the gender-explicit listing (andres te kai gynaikes — men and women) is characteristic of Luke's careful attention to female inclusion (Luke 8:1-3; 24:10-11; Acts 1:14; 2:17-18; 9:36-42; 16:13-15; 18:26). The numerical growth despite — or because of — the Ananias episode demonstrates Luke's consistent pattern: divine discipline produces community integrity, which produces credibility, which produces growth.
5.15: 'Peter's shadow might fall on them as he passed by' — the contact-healing through Peter's shadow has no direct OT parallel but reflects the popular understanding that dynamis (power) radiated from holy persons. Josephus mentions similar expectations attached to Elisha's bones (2 Kgs 13:21 — the dead man revived when he touched Elisha's bones; Ant. 9.8.6). In the Hellenistic world, the shadows and garments of wonder-workers were attributed healing properties. Luke does not confirm that Peter's shadow itself healed (he says people 'laid out' the sick hoping for it) but records the popular behavior without theological comment.
5.16: 'Tormented by impure spirits' (ochloumenous hypo pneumatōn akathartōn) — the geography of the healings expands beyond Jerusalem to 'the towns around Jerusalem' (tōn peri Ierousalēm poleōn). The same movement from Jerusalem outward characterizes the mission as a whole (1:8 — Jerusalem → Judea → Samaria → ends of earth). The specific mention of 'impure spirits' alongside diseases distinguishes the apostolic healing ministry as covering both domains of human affliction: physical illness and demonic oppression. Both are healed/expelled (etherapeunto apantes — all were being healed).
5.17: 'The high priest and all his associates, who were members of the party of the Sadducees, were filled with jealousy' (plēsthēsan zēlou) — zēlos (zeal/jealousy) is a powerful motivator in ancient Jewish literature: the zeal of Phinehas (Num 25:11) and the Maccabean warriors were celebrated as righteous; but jealousy-zēlos (the destructive envy of another's success) was condemned. The high priest's party acts from zēlos — rivalry with a successful popular movement — rather than genuine theological concern. Luke's characterization is pointed: the Sadducean establishment responds to miraculous success with envy.
5.18: The arrest of 'the apostles' (the plural, not just Peter and John as in ch.4, suggesting a wider arrest) and their placement in the 'public jail' (tērēsei dēmosia — public custody, as distinct from a private or temple jail) escalates the previous individual arrest (4:3). The public jail is the Roman-administered prison of Jerusalem, more severe than the Sanhedrin's own detention facilities. The escalation pattern (arrest → release → re-arrest → flogging → eventual martyrdom) follows the progression typical of Roman persecution of unauthorized religious movements.
5.19: The angel of the Lord who opens the prison doors (angelos Kyriou dia nyktos ēnoixen tas thyras tēs phylakēs) belongs to the 'divine rescue' genre widespread in Jewish literature (Dan 3; 6; Judith 13; 3 Macc. 6:18-21) and Hellenistic romances (Euripides, Bacchae 443-444; Ovid, Metamorphoses). Luke is not borrowing the genre but deploying it as typology: the God who rescued Daniel from the lions and Israel from Egypt now rescues the apostles from the Sanhedrin's custody. The angel's instruction ('go, stand in the temple courts') redirects the apostles immediately to the scene of their previous teaching.
5.20: 'Tell the people all about this new life' (lalōtte... panta ta rhēmata tēs zōēs tautēs — speak all the words of this life) — zōē (life) here is eschatological life, the resurrection-life that the apostles proclaim as having begun in Jesus. The instruction to return immediately to the temple courts (rather than flee Jerusalem) is significant: the divine rescue is not an escape from the mission but an enabling of its continuation. The apostles are returned to the exact location of the confrontation to resume exactly the activity that produced the confrontation.
5.21: 'At daybreak they entered the temple courts, as they had been told' — the apostles' early-morning temple entry corresponds to the temple's opening hours: the gates were opened at dawn for the morning sacrifice. The Sanhedrin's simultaneous convening ('the full assembly of the elders of Israel' — synedrion kai pasan tēn gerousian tōn huiōn Israēl) for the apostles' formal trial creates the dramatic irony: the council assembles to try prisoners who are already free and preaching.
5.22: 'The officers did not find them there' — the empty jail parallels the empty tomb: in both cases, the expected prisoner/body is absent, guarded doors are found locked, and the first witnesses are confused by the absence (Luke 24:3-4 — 'they did not find the body... two men in gleaming clothes stood beside them'). Luke's narrative echoes are deliberate: the apostles' prison-release participates in the same resurrection pattern that their preaching proclaims.
5.23: 'We found the jail securely locked, with the guards standing at the doors' — the guards' detailed report emphasizes the security of the containment: the phulakē (jail) was locked (kekleismenēn en pasē asphaleia — locked with all security) and the guards were at their posts. The impossibility of ordinary escape is Luke's apologetic point: no natural explanation (bribed guards, hidden exits, complicit jailers) accounts for the empty locked cell. The report mirrors the empty-tomb apologetic in Matthew 28:11-15 (guards bribed to deny the resurrection).
5.24: 'The captain of the temple guard and the chief priests were at a loss (diēporoun — thoroughly perplexed/at a complete loss), wondering what this might develop into' (ti an genoito touto) — diaporeomai (to be completely at a loss) is Luke's word for the disciples' bewilderment at the resurrection (Luke 24:4) and the crowd's at Pentecost (Acts 2:12). The same theological confusion that accompanied the resurrection now accompanies the prison-release that proclaims the resurrection. The authorities who orchestrated the containment cannot explain the escape.
5.25: The news that the imprisoned apostles are 'standing in the temple courts teaching the people' is delivered mid-Sanhedrin session as a messenger's intrusion — the dramatic timing maximizes the council's embarrassment. The apostles' location 'in the temple courts' (en tō hierō) is the most public and religiously significant space in Jerusalem: they have returned not to a private house but to the center of Jewish religious life, teaching as if they have never left.
5.26: 'They did not use force, because they feared that the people might stone them' — the Sanhedrin's officers bring the apostles 'without force' (ou meta bias) because they fear the crowd (lithasthōsin — might be stoned). The threat of stoning by the people reverses the Sanhedrin's institutional power: the council that has authority to stone the apostles dare not arrest them forcefully because the crowd might stone the council. The reversal of stoning-threat is one of Luke's consistent ironies in Acts 1-7 (prior to Stephen's actual stoning).
5.27: The second Sanhedrin appearance is more formal than the first (4:5-22): now the 'full assembly' (pan to synedrion) is convened, the charge is more specific (v.28), and the intended punishment is death (v.33). The pattern of escalating persecution mirrors the progression in the Synoptic Passion narrative (interrogation → formal trial → execution), and Luke's structuring of the apostolic persecution as a passion-parallel is intentional.
5.28: 'You have filled Jerusalem with your teaching' (peplērōkate tēn Ierousalēm tēs didachēs hymōn) — the high priest's accusation is simultaneously an inadvertent compliment: the apostolic mission has saturated the city. The charge of 'bringing this man's blood upon us' (epagagein eph' hēmas to haima tou anthrōpou toutou) echoes Matthew 27:25 ('his blood be on us and our children') — the Sanhedrin fears the legal and moral consequences of having authorized the crucifixion. The refusal to name Jesus ('this man') reflects both contempt and a kind of superstitious avoidance.
5.29: 'We must obey God rather than human beings' (peitharchein dei theō mallon ē anthrōpois) — the formulation is structurally identical to 4:19 but now uses peitharchein (to obey authority) rather than akouein (to listen/hear). The dei (must/necessity) frames apostolic disobedience as a divine necessity: they cannot choose otherwise. Plato attributes a similar principle to Socrates (Apology 29d); Philo applies it to Jewish resistance to Caligula's demands (Legatio 196-197). The principle — human law yields to divine law when they conflict — was recognized across Hellenistic Jewish and Greek ethical traditions.
5.30: 'The God of our ancestors raised Jesus from the dead — whom you killed by hanging him on a tree (xylon)' — xylon (tree/wood) rather than stauros (cross) invokes Deuteronomy 21:23 ('anyone who is hung on a tree is under God's curse') — as Paul makes explicit in Galatians 3:13. The Deuteronomic curse-of-the-tree was the Sanhedrin's argument that Jesus's crucifixion proved his divine rejection. Peter's use of xylon appropriates the Deuteronomic language and reframes it: the one hung on the tree was raised by the God of the ancestors, reversing the curse's implication.
5.31: 'God exalted him to his own right hand as Prince (archēgos) and Saviour (sōtēr)' — archēgos (pioneer/prince/leader/founder) appears in 3:15 ('author of life'), and in Hebrews 2:10 ('pioneer of salvation') and 12:2 ('pioneer and perfecter of faith'). The title combines the sense of one who goes first/ahead (pioneer) with one who rules (prince/leader). Sōtēr (saviour) is the imperial title claimed by Augustus and subsequent emperors; Luke's application of it to Jesus via the Sanhedrin-speech is a counter-imperial designation. 'To give repentance and forgiveness of sins' — the purpose of the exaltation is the provision of the gifts that bring Israel back to covenant relationship.
5.32: 'We are witnesses of these things, and so is the Holy Spirit, whom God has given to those who obey him' — the apostolic witness (martyres) and the Spirit's witness are presented as two distinct but coordinated testimonies to the same facts. The witness-theology follows Deuteronomy 17:6 and 19:15 (two or three witnesses required for legal testimony): the human witnesses (apostles) plus the divine witness (Spirit) constitute the required two-testimony basis for the resurrection claim. The condition 'to those who obey him' (tois peitharchounsin autō) uses the same peitharchein as v.29 — the Spirit is given to those who practice the apostolic obedience-to-God-rather-than-men.
5.33: 'They were furious and wanted to put them to death' (dieprionto kai eboulonto anelein autous) — dieprionto (were sawn-through, cut to the quick) is a vivid image for extreme internal agitation — the emotion that will also describe the Sanhedrin's reaction to Stephen (7:54 — dieprionto tais kardiais autōn). The progression from legal injunction (4:18) to flogging (5:40) to the intended death that only Gamaliel's intervention prevents mirrors the escalation that will end in Stephen's stoning. The Sanhedrin moves through available legal remedies toward extrajudicial violence.
5.35: Gamaliel's speech structure follows the standard Hellenistic deliberative rhetorical form: (1) directive to remove the accused from hearing (v.34), (2) statement of the principle at stake (v.35), (3) historical examples (vv.36-37), (4) conclusion drawn from the examples (vv.38-39). The paired historical examples (Theudas + Judas the Galilean) are the exempla central to deliberative argument. The speech's purpose — to interrupt the slide toward judicial murder — is rhetorically achieved: the Sanhedrin is persuaded to substitute flogging for execution (v.40).
5.36: The Theudas reference has troubled historians: Josephus places Theudas under the procuratorship of Fadus (44-46 CE; Ant. 20.5.1), which would be after Gamaliel's speech (ca. 30-35 CE) and after the 'Judas the Galilean' rebellion of 6 CE (which Gamaliel places 'after him'). The most common solutions: (1) Luke made a chronological error; (2) Luke's Theudas was a different Theudas from Josephus's; (3) Josephus's chronology has its own problems. The name Theudas (Thaddaios) was common enough that multiple figures with this name existed in the turbulent first-century Palestinian Jewish political landscape.
5.37: 'Judas the Galilean appeared in the days of the census' — the census of Quirinius (6 CE) and Judas the Galilean's revolt is described by Josephus (Ant. 18.1.1; 20.5.2; J.W. 2.8.1). Judas founded what Josephus calls the 'Fourth Philosophy' — a movement combining Pharisaic theology with resistance to Roman rule on the grounds that God alone was Israel's king. The movement's violent end ('he too was killed, and all his followers were scattered') matches Josephus's description. The historical reference grounds Gamaliel's argument in recognizable recent history.
5.38: 'If their purpose or activity is of human origin, it will fail' (ean ē ex anthrōpōn hē boulē hautē ē to ergon touto, katalythēsetai) — Gamaliel's prudential argument is not a theological endorsement of the apostles but a tactical restraint: the test of divine origin is historical durability. The argument has the structure of a Rabbinic proof-from-consequence (kal va-chomer — if A (human movements fail), then B (wait to see if this movement fails)). The argument's limitation — it could validate any movement that survives long enough — is precisely what Gamaliel does not address; it was not his concern.
5.39: 'If it is from God, you will not be able to stop these men; you will only find yourselves fighting against God (theomachoi)' — theomachos (god-fighter) is a Greek concept with strong negative connotations: Euripides's Bacchae presents Pentheus as the paradigmatic theomachos who fights against Dionysus and is destroyed. Jewish tradition used theomachein for Pharaoh's resistance to YHWH (Philo, Vit. Mos. 1.96). The risk of being a theomachos is the most compelling deterrent Gamaliel can invoke before the Sanhedrin — no council wants to be recorded as having fought God and lost.
5.40: 'They called the apostles in and had them flogged' (deirantes — having beaten/flayed) — the flogging inflicted by the Sanhedrin was the 'forty lashes minus one' (malkot) prescribed in Deuteronomy 25:3 and described in m. Makkot 3:10-12. The sentence was a moderate judicial punishment within Jewish law, far short of execution (which Gamaliel had prevented). Paul will later reference receiving this punishment five times (2 Cor 11:24 — 'five times I received from the Jews the forty lashes minus one'). The apostles are released with both a punishment and a repeated injunction not to speak in Jesus's name.
5.41: 'Rejoicing because they had been counted worthy of suffering disgrace for the Name' (katēxiōthēsan atimasthēnai hyper tou onomatos) — the paradox of rejoicing at dishonor (atimia) for the sake of honor (onoma) is the martyr-theology of the beatitudes (Matt 5:11-12 — 'blessed are you when people insult you, persecute you... rejoice and be glad') applied to actual suffering. The suffering for 'the Name' (hyper tou onomatos — literally 'for the name,' without specifying whose) is the early Christian formula for suffering on account of Jesus's identity and authority. 3 Maccabees 6:30 and 4 Maccabees use similar language for Jewish martyrs.
5.42: 'Day after day, in the temple courts and from house to house, they never stopped teaching and proclaiming the good news that Jesus is the Messiah' — the dual-venue ministry (temple + house) describes the early Jerusalem church's bifurcated institutional life: public, formal proclamation in the publicly accessible temple courts; intimate, family-sized instruction in private homes (kat' oikon — home by home, house by house). This pattern anticipated the eventual expulsion from the temple and the shift to house-church-only structure that became normative for Christianity outside Jerusalem within a generation.

7.1: Stephen's speech (7:1-53) is the longest speech in Acts and the most complex LXX survey in the NT. Its structure: Abraham narrative (vv. 2-8), Joseph narrative (vv. 9-16), Moses narrative (vv. 17-43), tabernacle/temple argument (vv. 44-50), accusation (vv. 51-53). The cumulative argument: Israel has consistently rejected the prophets, preferred idols to YHWH, and confused God's dwelling with a human building. The heroes (Abraham, Joseph, Moses) are all figures of rejection and vindication — typological of Jesus. Stephen's speech is widely recognized as independent tradition (its Christology is archaic: 'the Righteous One', 7:52; 'the Son of Man', 7:56).
7.58: Saul's first appearance in Acts is as a passive witness: hoi de martyres apethento ta himatia autōn para tous podas neaniou kaloumenou Saulou ('the witnesses laid their garments at the feet of a young man called Saul'). He is neanias — probably 25-40 years old. His 'consent' (syneudokōn, 8:1) marks him as an approving bystander who will become the active persecutor of 8:3. The contrast between Stephen's death (full of Spirit, seeing the Son of Man, forgiving his killers) and Saul's role in it frames the conversion of ch. 9 as the more dramatic precisely because of this starting point.
7.2: Theos tēs doxēs ('God of glory'): a title without exact parallel in the OT, though 'glory' language (kābôd/doxa) consistently marks YHWH's manifest presence (Exod 16:10; Ps 29:3). Stephen opens by placing Abraham's call within theophanic context — God's glory appearing — before any temple existed. en Mesopotamia: Acts specifies Mesopotamia as the site of the first theophany, before Harran. Genesis 11:31 has Terah's family leave Ur; Stephen's emphasis on Mesopotamia may reflect Gen 15:7 ('I am YHWH who brought you out of Ur of the Chaldeans') or LXX tradition. The point is that God's self-revealing to Israel began outside the promised land, in a pagan context — the pattern that runs through the whole speech.
7.3: The citation of Gen 12:1 ('Leave your country and your people') follows the LXX wording — ek tēs gēs sou kai ek tēs syngenias sou. The command is unconditional: no destination is named, only the assurance 'the land I will show you.' This pattern — God commanding departure without specifying arrival — is the structural template Stephen applies to Moses (v.34) and implicitly to the disciples: obedient departure in response to divine call, destination unknown to the human recipient. The audience of the speech would have heard this as paradigmatic of faithful response to God's word.
7.4: After the death of his father: a chronological detail that creates tension with the MT of Genesis. Genesis 11:26 gives Terah's age as 70 when Abraham was born; Gen 11:32 puts Terah's death at 205. If Abraham left Harran at 75 (Gen 12:4), Terah would have been 145 — not yet dead by 60 years. The LXX tradition resolves this: some witnesses read Terah's death at 145 rather than 205. The Samaritan Pentateuch similarly places Terah's death before Abraham's departure. Stephen follows a textual tradition that harmonized the timeline; this is a text-critical datum, not a careless error, and his audience likely knew the harmonizing tradition.
7.5: Oudé bēma podos ('not even a foot of ground'): the phrase echoes Deut 2:5, where Israel receives 'not so much as a footstep's worth of land' from Edom. Applied to Abraham, it highlights the covenant paradox: God promises a land while giving Abraham none of it during his lifetime. The patriarchal promise was believed on word alone — the land held in hope without possession. Hebrews 11:8-16 celebrates precisely this faith; Stephen's speech draws on the same tradition of land-as-promise rather than land-as-possession. Kata kataschein (for an inheritance): the LXX vocabulary of land-grant (Num 26:53-55), signaling the covenantal register.
7.6: Tetrakosi etē ('four hundred years'): follows Gen 15:13 LXX, where God reveals the future at the formal covenant ceremony. Exod 12:40 gives 430 years, counting from a different starting point. Both are traditional rounded figures: the 400 counts from the promise to Abraham; the 430 from Israel's entry into Egypt. Paul uses 430 years in Gal 3:17 for a different argument. The covenant-making context of Gen 15 matters here — God reveals the slavery-and-exodus pattern within the covenant-ratification ritual, indicating that even the suffering of the Egypt period was within the covenant frame, not outside it.
7.7: En tō topō toutō ('in this place'): the divine promise blends Gen 15:14 ('they will come out with great possessions') with Exod 3:12 ('you will worship God on this mountain'). The 'this place' of Gen 15 contextually means Canaan; applied to Exod 3, it means Sinai/Horeb. Stephen appears to exploit this ambiguity deliberately — God's promised place of worship was Sinai before it was Jerusalem, and a land before it was a temple. The blending of texts anticipates his argument in v.48 that God's presence is not fixed to any human structure.
7.8: Diathēkēn peritomēs ('covenant of circumcision'): the formal covenant of Gen 17, where circumcision seals the body as the covenant sign. The eight-day timing (Gen 17:12; 21:4) distinguishes Israelite infant circumcision from adult initiation rites in surrounding cultures. Stephen's rhetorical structure: he names the circumcision covenant here, then accuses his audience of 'uncircumcised hearts and ears' (v.51) — they possess the bodily sign but not its heart-reality (Deut 10:16; Jer 4:4; 9:26). The twelve patriarchs' list closes the genealogical bracket and opens the Joseph section, which is the first of three rejection-and-vindication cycles.
7.9: Zēlōsantes ('jealous'): the Joseph story (Gen 37-50) circulated in expanded forms in Second Temple Judaism — Josephus (Ant. 2.1-10) and the Testament of Joseph elaborate it substantially. The jealousy term (zēloō) is the same root as the zeal that drove Saul of Tarsus (8:1-3); the irony is available to any audience that has read Acts to this point. Ho theos ēn met' autou ('God was with him'): the refrain throughout the Joseph narrative (Gen 39:2, 21, 23) is the theological key to Joseph's reversals — human rejection met by divine accompaniment. This phrase is the capsule of Stephen's argument: human rejection of God's chosen does not undo divine election.
7.10: Charin enantion Pharaō ('grace/favor before Pharaoh'): the LXX vocabulary of charis (favor) before a superior is standard court language of the patronage system. Joseph's rise from slave to second-in-command (Gen 41:40-44) is the OT's most dramatic status-reversal — the sold slave rules over his sellers. Josephus (Ant. 2.9.1) and Philo (De Josepho) developed Joseph's wisdom as a Hellenistic philosophical virtue, making him a Jewish culture-hero; Stephen's mention of 'wisdom' draws on this tradition even if not explicitly citing it.
7.11: Thlipsis megalē ('great suffering'): the famine in Gen 41:54-57 is depicted as universal — 'all the earth' had no grain. The word thlipsis (distress, tribulation) connects the Joseph narrative to the biblical pattern of tribulation preceding vindication — the same pattern Stephen traces through Moses and applies implicitly to the church's persecution. The entire Joseph section demonstrates that God's purposes advance through, not despite, the suffering of the rejected.
7.12: Sitia ('grain/food'): Jacob hears of grain in Egypt through rumor — ordinary human report, not divine revelation. The contrast between Jacob's dependence on secondhand information and God's providential arrangement of Joseph's presence beforehand is the narrative's theological point. Providence operates beneath the surface of apparently ordinary human decisions — exactly the pattern Stephen will apply to Moses's rejection and vindication, and implicitly to the apostles' situation in Jerusalem.
7.13: En tē deutera ('on the second visit'): Genesis 43-45 narrates the second trip to Egypt, culminating in the revelation scene (Gen 45:1-4) — one of the most emotionally charged moments in the Pentateuch. Joseph's weeping and self-disclosure — 'I am Joseph your brother, whom you sold into Egypt' (Gen 45:4) — is a moment of total reversal: the dead-to-them brother stands alive as their ruler. Stephen's compression (v.13) names the self-disclosure without dwelling on it; the rhetorical point is that recognition was delayed, not immediate, and came only after multiple rejections. The pattern: rejected → exalted → returns to reveal himself to those who rejected him.
7.14: Hebdomēkonta pente psychas ('seventy-five persons'): the MT of Gen 46:27 and Deut 10:22 gives 70; the LXX of Gen 46:27 gives 75 by including Joseph's grandsons (Manasseh's and Ephraim's sons, not in the MT list). Stephen follows the LXX number — confirming that the Greek OT is his and his audience's Bible. This is not a historical discrepancy but a text-critical datum: the Diaspora community read the Septuagint, and its figure was 75. The LXX's expanded number reflects a counting tradition that adds additional descendants; both figures count 'those who went down to Egypt' but apply different inclusion criteria.
7.15: Katebē Iakōb eis Aigupton ('Jacob went down to Egypt'): 'going down' to Egypt and 'going up' from Egypt is a consistent theological geography in the OT — Egypt is always spatially and symbolically 'below,' Canaan-Jerusalem 'above.' Jacob's descent was driven by famine, enabled by Joseph's position. The brevity of 'he and our ancestors died' anticipates v.16's burial note. Death in foreign soil — outside the promised land — was a theological anomaly requiring resolution: their bodies needed to return to the promised land, just as God's people would ultimately need to return from exile.
7.16: Eis Sychem ('to Shechem'): one of the most studied textual difficulties in Acts. Genesis 23 records Abraham purchasing a tomb at Machpelah near Hebron from Ephron the Hittite; Joshua 24:32 records Joseph's burial at Shechem in the plot Jacob purchased from the sons of Hamor (Gen 33:19). Stephen appears to combine both transactions, attributing the Shechem purchase to Abraham rather than Jacob. Ancient solutions include: (a) a lost tradition of Abraham also buying land at Shechem; (b) scribal conflation in the speech tradition; (c) a summary that attributes the entire patriarchal land-purchase history to the period generally. Luke records Stephen's speech as delivered, not corrected; the difficulty belongs to the speech tradition, not Luke's editorial process.
7.17: Ēngizen ho chronos tēs epangelias ('the time of the promise drew near'): divine timing language — God's promises operating on a schedule invisible to the recipients but recognizable in retrospect — is characteristic of Luke-Acts throughout (cf. Luke 1:20; Acts 1:6-7; Gal 4:4). The population growth (ēuxēsen ho laos) echoes Exod 1:7-12, itself echoing Gen 1:28 ('be fruitful and multiply'). Increase of God's people is a covenant-fulfillment sign; Pharaoh's suppression of it is the counter-movement that the exodus will reverse. The 'drawing near' of the promise's fulfillment increases the pressure on both sides.
7.18: Basileùs heteros ('a different king'): citing Exod 1:8 LXX — 'a new king arose over Egypt who did not know Joseph.' In Second Temple interpretation, the 'not knowing Joseph' was understood as willful political amnesia — the new dynasty suppressed memory of Joseph's administration because acknowledging dependence on a Hebrew slave was politically inconvenient. Josephus (Ant. 2.9.1) connects the change to an ethnic succession. The theological point is that human political power forgets God's prior acts of provision; the structural point is that the pattern of rejection-amnesia is endemic — it recurs at each generation's turning point.
7.19: Ektithesthai ta brephē autōn ('to expose/throw out their newborn babies'): the Greek verb ektithēmi is the technical term for infant exposure — a common Greco-Roman practice in which unwanted infants were left in public places to die or be collected by slave traders. The Hebrew practice Stephen describes (Exod 1:22, throwing male infants into the Nile) is the Pharaonic decree. Stephen uses Hellenistic vocabulary to describe a Semitic practice, making it comprehensible to his bilingual audience. The explicit purpose — 'so that they would die' (eis to mē zōogoneisthai) — strips away any euphemism from Pharaoh's policy.
7.20: Asteios tō theō ('beautiful/pleasing to God'): translates Exod 2:2's Hebrew tôb ('good/beautiful child') with the intensifier tō theō ('to God'). Philo (De Vita Mosis 1.2-9) develops Moses's extraordinary beauty and gifts in detail, connecting them to divine calling. Heb 11:23 uses the same Greek phrase. The three-month concealment (Exod 2:2) was an act of civil disobedience against Pharaoh's decree — a repeated pattern in the exodus story where the vulnerable (midwives, Moses's mother) resist imperial power through quiet non-compliance.
7.21: Thugatēr Pharaō ('Pharaoh's daughter'): Exodus 2:5-10 gives no name; Josephus (Ant. 2.9.5) names her Thermouthis. She adopts Moses formally (anethrefsato heautē eis huion, 'brought him up as her own son') — in Egyptian royal court context this gave Moses the legal status and education of royalty. The adoption bridges the paradox of Moses's identity: biologically Hebrew, legally Egyptian, called by God as Israel's deliverer. The irony that the instrument of Israel's liberation is trained in the household of Israel's oppressor is one of Stephen's key structural points: God uses the world's own structures to accomplish what the world opposes.
7.22: Epaideuthe Mōysēs pasē sophia Aigyptōn ('Moses was educated in all the wisdom of the Egyptians'): not stated in Exod 2, which moves directly from adoption to adulthood; this is a tradition attested in Philo (De Vita Mosis 1.5-23 — philosophy, arithmetic, geometry, music) and Josephus (Ant. 2.9.7 — military leadership). The 'wisdom of the Egyptians' included hieroglyphic writing, astronomical knowledge, and administrative expertise. This tradition explained how a former slave's grandson could function as a royal court negotiator; it was also part of Jewish apologetics showing that Moses surpassed pagan wisdom-traditions — not inferior to Greek philosophy.
7.23: Plēroumenō tesserakontaetēs chronos ('when he was forty years old'): the three-part structure of Moses's life as three 40-year periods (40 in Egypt, 40 in Midian, 40 in wilderness) is not stated in the biblical narrative but is a standard Second Temple interpretive framework. Philo, Josephus, and the rabbis all use it. The 40-year period is the biblical number for a generation of probation (40 years in the wilderness = one generation). Stephen's use of the three-period structure shows his dependence on this interpretive tradition; his audience would have recognized it immediately.
7.24: Ēmynato tō kataponoumenō ('he went to his defense, helped the oppressed one'): Stephen characterizes Moses's killing of the Egyptian (Exod 2:12) as ekdikēsis (advocacy/defense) on behalf of the oppressed — a legitimizing interpretation. The LXX of Exod 2:12 simply says 'he killed the Egyptian'; Stephen interprets the act as juridical advocacy for the vulnerable. This is consistent with a legal reading: Moses as judge (explicitly his assigned role in v.27) administering justice to a vulnerable Israelite. The contrast between Moses's advocacy and Israel's rejection of it (v.27) is the central irony of the episode — the deliverer is rejected by those he delivers.
7.25: Enomizan synienai ('he supposed they would understand'): this psychological motivation is absent from Exodus. Exod 2:12-13 gives no indication of Moses's reasoning; Stephen adds it. This is a significant hermeneutical move — it transforms Moses's act from individual vigilante violence into a failed attempt at public ministry. The failure to understand (ou synēkan) anticipates the reception of Jesus in Luke 19:44 ('you did not recognize the time of your visitation') and the parable of the wicked tenants (Luke 20:9-16). The pattern of people failing to recognize their own deliverer is Stephen's organizing theme.
7.26: Synēllasen eis eirēnēn ('tried to reconcile them'): synallassō (reconcile, bring together) is legal and diplomatic vocabulary — Moses attempts to mediate a dispute between parties who are in the wrong. The scene (Exod 2:13-14) shows Moses intervening as a would-be judge/arbitrator. The irony Stephen's audience is meant to catch: the man who will formally adjudicate disputes for millions (Exod 18:13-26) is here unable to resolve a conflict between two countrymen. The rejection of small-scale mediation anticipates the rejection of Moses as national deliverer.
7.27: Tis se katestēsen archonta kai dikastēn ('who made you ruler and judge over us?'): the question from Exod 2:14 LXX is cited twice in Stephen's speech (here and v.35), becoming the paradigmatic instance of Israel rejecting God's appointed deliverer. The double citation is rhetorical — repetition drives home the structural parallel with Jesus's rejection. In OT legal context, the question is technically valid: authority as 'ruler and judge' (archōn kai dikastēs) required formal appointment. Moses had no such appointment yet. But the questioner, while technically correct, was spiritually blind to who Moses was and why he acted.
7.28: The exposure of Moses's killing — 'Are you thinking of killing me as you killed the Egyptian yesterday?' — is the moment of his legal vulnerability: he had committed manslaughter outside any judicial structure, without the Torah's provisions for distinguishing premeditated from accidental killing (not yet given). Moses's exposure by a fellow Hebrew is the ultimate irony: the one he defended becomes the instrument of his flight. Echthes ('yesterday') signals the brevity between act and accusation — a compressed crisis that mirrors the speed of the Sanhedrin's response to Stephen's speech.
7.29: Paroikos en gē Madiam ('resident alien in the land of Midian'): paroikos (sojourner, resident alien) is the LXX term for a person living in a land not their own without full civic rights — the same condition as Israel in Egypt (v.6). Moses becomes what his people were: a foreigner without standing. He names his son Gershom (Exod 2:22, gēr shom = 'a sojourner there'), encoding his alien status in the child's name. The Midianites were descendants of Abraham through Keturah (Gen 25:2), so Moses's refuge was in kinship territory, not entirely foreign — a detail his audience would know and that mitigates the sense of total estrangement.
7.30: En tē erēmō tou orous Sina ('in the desert of Mount Sinai'): Stephen uses Sinai rather than Horeb (the name used in Exod 3:1; Deut consistently uses Horeb). Sinai and Horeb are two names for the same sacred mountain; LXX uses Sinai more often in Exodus. The forty years in Midian completes the second of three 40-year periods. The burning bush — fire in a bush that does not consume — was visually and conceptually the opposite of the ordinary: normal fire destroys vegetation; this fire sustained and illuminated it. The paradox of the burning-but-unburned bush signals the supernatural quality of the theophany before God speaks.
7.31: Thaumazōn to horama ('marveling at the sight'): Moses approaches to investigate (Exod 3:3) — the natural curiosity of an alert shepherd. The approach triggers divine address: God speaks when the human draws near. This pattern — divine address requiring prior human attention — recurs throughout OT theophany (Abraham in Gen 22; Samuel in 1 Sam 3; Elijah in 1 Kgs 19). Phonē kyriou egeneto ('the voice of the Lord came'): the LXX vocabulary for prophetic reception, marking Moses's experience as continuous with the prophetic tradition — not a private mystical experience but a commission parallel to Isaiah's (Isa 6) and Jeremiah's (Jer 1).
7.32: Egō ho theos tōn paterōn sou ('I am the God of your fathers'): the triple name — Abraham, Isaac, Jacob — is the formal covenant identification that grounds all subsequent appeals to the covenant promises (Exod 3:6). Moses 'trembled and did not dare to look' (entromos genomenos ouk etolma katanoeō): trembling before divine presence is a standard theophanic response (Dan 8:17; Ezek 1:28; Rev 1:17). The contrast between Moses's reverent fear and the Sanhedrin's reaction (v.54, grinding teeth) is a structural irony available to Stephen's audience: those who should tremble do not; Moses, the deliverer, did.
7.33: Lyson to hypodēma tōn podōn sou ('take off your sandals'): removing sandals before holy ground was practiced across the ancient Near East — not uniquely Israelite. It appears in Jewish, Egyptian, and Babylonian contexts as a mark of reverence before sacred space. The rabbinic prohibition against wearing sandals in the temple courts derived from this principle. Hē hagia (holy ground): the sanctification of ground by divine presence — at Sinai, without a constructed building — is precisely the point Stephen is building toward in v.48. Sinai is holy because God is present there; no human structure created that holiness.
7.34: Idōn eidon tēn kakōsin ('I have indeed seen the oppression'): the cognate absolute (idōn eidon) follows the Hebrew infinitive absolute construction, intensifying the verb — 'I have certainly/truly seen.' This is LXX Exod 3:7. The divine 'coming down' (katebēn) is an anthropomorphism for direct intervention (cf. Gen 11:5, God 'comes down' to see Babel). The commissioning formula — 'Now come, I will send you back to Egypt' — repeats the pattern of divine call to return to the place of danger, the pattern of Abraham's call, the prophets' calls to hostile audiences, and the disciples' mission to the nations.
7.35: Touton ton Mōysēn hon ērnounto ('this same Moses whom they refused'): the demonstrative touton ('this one') repeats three times in vv.35-38, marking each statement as a typological pivot. The combination of archōn (ruler) and lytrotēs (deliverer/redeemer) is significant: lytrotēs is a NT hapax, but the related lytrōsis (redemption) and lytrōtēs link to Isaianic new-exodus vocabulary (Luke 1:68; 2:38; 24:21). 'Through the angel' reflects the Second Temple tradition of angelic mediation in theophanies — a tradition that elevated theophanies rather than diminishing them, situating them within the cosmic order of divine messengers.
7.36: Terata kai sēmeia ('wonders and signs'): the paired terms are the LXX vocabulary for the plague-signs of Egypt (Exod 7-12; Deut 4:34; 6:22). The sequence — Egypt, Red Sea (en Erythrō thalassē), wilderness (40 years) — matches the Deuteronomic summary (Deut 1:1-3; Josh 24:5-7). Stephen compresses 40 years into one verse, assuming audience knowledge; the point is the sustained series of divine acts accomplished through Moses over his entire 40-year ministry. The summary validates Moses as the paradigmatic prophet-deliverer whose works authenticate divine mandate.
7.37: Prophētēn hymīn anastēsei ho theos ... hōs eme ('God will raise up a prophet like me'): Deut 18:15-19 was a crucial messianic text in Second Temple Judaism. The Qumran community (4QTest, 4QFlor) applied it to an eschatological prophetic figure. Peter applied it to Jesus in Acts 3:22-23. Stephen's inclusion in Moses's own speech places the prophecy within the Pentateuchal narrative itself — Moses predicts his own succession by a greater prophet. The implicit argument: if Israel's paradigmatic sin was rejecting Moses, rejecting the prophet-like-Moses (Jesus) is the ultimate expression of the same pattern.
7.38: En tē ekklēsia en tē erēmō ('in the assembly/congregation in the wilderness'): ekklēsia is the LXX translation of Hebrew qāhāl (assembly/congregation) — the same word used for the early Christian community. Its use here connects the Sinai community to the NT ekklēsia: the same word, the same structure of covenant assembly. The 'angel who spoke to him on Mount Sinai' reflects the Jewish tradition of angelic mediation at Sinai (Gal 3:19; Heb 2:2; Josephus Ant. 15.5.3). Logia zōnta ('living words/oracles'): Torah as divine speech — the same characterization as Heb 4:12 ('the word of God is living and active'). Torah is not static text but active communication.
7.39: Hō ouk ēthelēsan hypakooi genesthai ('they were not willing to obey him'): the aorist ēthelēsan signals a decisive volitional refusal — not inability but unwillingness. En tais kardiais autōn estrapsēn eis Aigupton ('in their hearts they turned back to Egypt'): the internal orientation toward Egypt precedes the external apostasy of the golden calf. Deuteronomy's ethical reinterpretation of the wilderness generation's failures (Deut 29-30) moved from external covenant-breaking to heart-apostasy — the same move Stephen makes in connecting the golden calf to 'uncircumcised hearts' (v.51).
7.40: Poiēson hēmin theous ('make us gods who will go before us'): the citation of Exod 32:1 LXX is exact. The demand for gods 'who will go before us' (hoi proporeusontai hēmōn) reflects the function of the ark and pillar of cloud/fire — mobile divine presence leading the march. The golden calf was not necessarily intended as a different deity but as a visible representation of YHWH's presence — the same category of error as confusing a human-made representation with divine reality. Ho Mōysēs houtos ('this Moses'): the dismissive houtos from Exod 32:1 echoes the dismissive question of v.27 — Israel's first rejection of Moses prefigures the golden calf apostasy.
7.41: Emoskhopoiēsan ('they made a calf'): a compound verb created for this passage; the underlying Hebrew is ʿēgel massēkāh ('molten calf'). The celebration with sacrifices (thysia) and 'reveling' (euphraínesthai en tois ergois tōn cheirōn autōn, 'rejoicing in the works of their hands') is the standard prophetic condemnation of idol-worship (Isa 2:8; Hos 14:3; Jer 1:16). 'Works of their hands' is the prophetic tag for idols — human manufacture mistaken for divine presence. Stephen has moved from narrative to prophetic indictment mode, the tone that will carry through to v.53.
7.42: Estrepsen de ho theos kai paredōken autous ('God turned and gave them over'): the judicial abandonment pattern Paul will elaborate in Romans 1:24, 26, 28 — three times 'God gave them over' to their chosen sins. Here the giving-over is to star worship, following the golden calf. The LXX of Amos 5:25-27 that Stephen quotes differs from the MT: MT has 'Sikkuth your king and Kiyyun your star-god'; LXX/Stephen have 'the tabernacle of Moloch and the star of the god Rephan.' The astrological deities condemned by Amos were celestial powers — Moloch/Saturn, Rephan/Saturn in Egyptian-Aramaic — whose worship Stephen identifies as the consequence of turning from the invisible God to visible representations.
7.43: Meteikiō hymas epekeina Babylōnos ('I will exile you beyond Babylon'): the Amos 5:27 MT has 'beyond Damascus'; Stephen/LXX has 'beyond Babylon,' updating the geographic reference to the Babylonian exile that later proved more catastrophic. This reflects either a deliberate interpretive update (the final exile reached Babylon and beyond) or the LXX textual tradition. The point for Stephen's argument: the temple worship his audience is defending was preceded by centuries of idolatry that God judged with exile — the temple is not an unconditional guarantee of divine favor.
7.44: Hē skēnē tou martyriou ('tabernacle of testimony/witness'): called the 'tabernacle of testimony' because it housed the covenant tablets — the edut (testimony/covenant document). Kata ton typon hon heōrakei ('according to the pattern he had seen'): Exod 25:40, cited again in Heb 8:5. The 'pattern' (typos) Moses saw — the heavenly archetype — means the earthly tabernacle was a copy of a heavenly reality. This Platonic-sounding language (visible thing copied from invisible archetype) was at home in Alexandrian Jewish interpretation; Stephen may draw on Alexandrian traditions even while speaking in Jerusalem. The tabernacle's status as copy means God's true dwelling is not on earth.
7.45: En tē kataschesei tōn ethnōn ('when they took possession from the nations'): the Canaanites were displaced in the conquest; the tabernacle was brought in and placed at Shiloh (Josh 18:1), later at Nob and Gibeon. The portable sanctuary served Israel for centuries throughout the conquest and judges periods — without a permanent building, without any apparent deficiency. Stephen's point: the portable tabernacle 'worked' perfectly well for centuries. The pressure toward a permanent structure came from the human side (David's desire, v.46; Solomon's building, v.47), not from any divine necessity.
7.46: Aitēsato eurein skēnōma ('asked that he might find a dwelling place'): a text-critical decision affects the argument here. The variant 'for the house of Jacob' (tō oikō Iakōb) is attested in important early manuscripts against the more common 'for the God of Jacob' (tō theō Iakōb). The 'house of Jacob' reading shifts meaning: David sought not to build a house for God but a dwelling for Jacob's people — which God gave them in the land, not in a temple. This aligns with Nathan's oracle in 2 Sam 7:5-7, where God explicitly says he has not asked for a cedar house from anyone. David's desire to build was not authorized by God — God authorized a later successor's building.
7.47: Salōmōn de ōkodomēsen autō oikon ('but it was Solomon who built a house for him'): the adversative de ('but') is mild but pointed. Solomon, not David, built it — the building was a concession granted after God's explicit statement that he does not need a house (2 Sam 7:5-7). In rabbinic tradition the reason for David's exclusion is debated: his wars and bloodshed made him unfit (1 Chr 22:8). Stephen does not dwell on this; his point is to place the Solomonic temple in the narrative sequence after centuries of portable tabernacle worship, marking it as a later development rather than an eternal divine requirement.
7.48: Ouch ho Hypsistos en cheiropoiētois katoikei ('the Most High does not live in things made by human hands'): Hypsistos ('Most High') is a title used for YHWH in LXX but also for Zeus in the Hellenistic world — a term with ecumenical resonance. Cheiropoiētos ('hand-made') is the LXX word for idols (Lev 26:1; Isa 2:18; 16:12). Its application to the Jerusalem temple is the speech's climactic provocation: the temple Stephen's accusers are defending is labeled with the same word used for idols throughout the LXX. The Isaiah 66:1-2 citation that follows provides the prophetic warrant — this is not Stephen's innovation but Isaiah's.
7.49: Ho ouranos moi thronos, hē de gē hypopodion ('heaven is my throne, earth is my footstool'): Isaiah 66:1 LXX, opening the book's final oracle. The spatial logic demolishes the premise of temple-building: God's throne is the heavens — no building of any size could contain him. Isaiah 66 continues with a vision of universal worship (66:18-21, 'I will gather all nations and peoples'), which Stephen does not quote but which is available to his audience as background. Hypopodion (footstool): the entire earth is God's footstool — smaller than his throne. The term was also used for the ark and temple in OT tradition (Ps 99:5; 132:7), ironically subsumed here under the cosmic image.
7.50: Ouchi hē cheir mou epoiēsen tauta panta ('has not my hand made all these things?'): the rhetorical question closes the Isaianic citation. The 'hand of God' that made all things is contrasted with cheiropoiēta (things made by human hands, v.48). Creation is God's work; the temple is human work. This is not a rejection of temple worship per se — Isaiah 66 does not abolish worship — but a corrective to the theology that treats the temple as guaranteeing divine presence regardless of Israel's obedience. Jeremiah's temple sermon (Jer 7:4) made the same point: 'Do not trust in these deceptive words: This is the temple of the LORD, the temple of the LORD, the temple of the LORD.'
7.51: Sklērotrachēloi kai aperitmētoi kardiais kai tois ōsin ('stiff-necked and uncircumcised in hearts and ears'): two OT accusations combined. 'Stiff-necked' (sklērotrachēlos) is Exod 33:5 and Deut 9:6, 13 — Israel's characterization during the wilderness period, the very period Stephen has been narrating. 'Uncircumcised hearts' is Jer 4:4; 9:26; Lev 26:41; Deut 10:16 — the prophetic reinterpretation of circumcision as heart-condition rather than physical rite. 'Uncircumcised ears' recalls Jer 6:10. The circumcision they bodily possess (the covenant sign of v.8) is contradicted by uncircumcised hearts and ears. Stephen charges his audience with the exact spiritual condition that produced every failure he has narrated.
7.52: Tina tōn prophētōn ouk ediōxan hoi pateres hymōn ('which of the prophets did your fathers not persecute?'): a recognized Second Temple Jewish motif — the 'Deuteronomistic' pattern of prophet-persecution by the covenant people. The Lives of the Prophets (first century CE) catalogs violent deaths: Isaiah sawn in two, Amos beaten to death, Micah thrown off a cliff, Habakkuk stoned. Jesus's own lament (Luke 13:34 — 'Jerusalem, Jerusalem, who kills the prophets') drew on this tradition. Ton Dikaion ('the Righteous One'): the messianic title used in Acts 3:14 (Peter) — rooted in Isa 53:11 ('the Righteous One, my servant, will justify many') and the Book of Enoch's 'righteous one' for the Son of Man.
7.53: Eis diatagēn angelōn ('at the ordinance/direction of angels'): the tradition of angelic mediation at Sinai is attested in multiple sources: Deut 33:2 LXX ('his angels with him'); Gal 3:19 ('ordained through angels'); Heb 2:2 ('the message declared by angels'); Josephus Ant. 15.5.3. In Jewish theology this elevated the Torah's authority — divine origin through the highest conceivable mediators. Stephen turns it into the sharpest possible indictment: the more exalted the source, the more inexcusable the disobedience. A law mediated through angels — above any prophet, any king, any human authority — was not kept. The accusation closes the historical survey and opens the verdict.
7.54: Dieprionto tais kardiais autōn ('they were furious, cut to the heart'): diapriō literally means 'to saw through/cut apart' — the speech cut through the audience's composure. The same word appears in Acts 5:33 (the Sanhedrin's reaction to Peter and the apostles) — a Lukan verbal echo connecting the two scenes. The gnashing of teeth (ebrychon tous odontas) is the imagery of wild animals baring teeth at prey (Ps 35:16; 37:12 LXX) and in the NT is associated with final judgment (Matt 8:12; 13:42, 50; 22:13; 25:30; Luke 13:28). The Sanhedrin enacts the eschatological judgment-response toward the one who has spoken God's word — another of Stephen's ironic reversals.
7.55: Iēsoun hestōta ek dexiōn tou theou ('Jesus standing at the right hand of God'): Psalm 110:1 has the LORD say 'Sit at my right hand' — consistently applied to the exalted Christ in the NT (Acts 2:34; Heb 1:3; 8:1). But here Jesus is standing, not sitting. Several Second Temple Jewish texts describe heavenly beings as standing in the divine council (1 Enoch 40:2; 2 Enoch 21:3). The standing posture may indicate: (a) Jesus as witness-advocate for Stephen before the heavenly court — witnesses stand to testify in Jewish legal contexts; (b) Jesus rising to receive the martyr; (c) the 'Son of Man' approaching the Ancient of Days (Dan 7:13). The vision is the only NT occurrence of Jesus 'standing' at the right hand, and Stephen is the only one who sees it.
7.56: Ton huion tou anthrōpou ek dexiōn hestōta tou theou ('the Son of Man standing at the right hand of God'): the title 'Son of Man' — Jesus's characteristic self-designation in the Gospels (81 occurrences) — here is the only NT use by someone other than Jesus himself (outside the Gospels; cf. Rev 1:13; 14:14, 'one like a son of man'). Stephen's vision uses Dan 7:13-14 vocabulary ('son of man coming with the clouds of heaven'). The 'open heaven' (diēnoigmenous tous ouranous) frames the vision as prophetic theophany — the opened heaven is the standard mark of vision-reception in Ezekiel (1:1), the baptism accounts (Mark 1:10), and Revelation (4:1). Heaven's opening confirms the word spoken.
7.57: Syneschon ta ōta autōn ('they covered their ears'): a theatrical expression of refusal to hear blasphemy — physically closing the ears cut off the offending words from being processed. The high priest tearing his robes at Jesus's trial (Mark 14:63-64) and this ear-covering gesture are parallel ritual expressions of official outrage at claimed blasphemy. Hōrmēsan homothymadon ep' auton ('they all rushed upon him together'): homothymadon ('with one accord') is used earlier in Acts for the unified action of the prayer community (1:14; 2:46; 4:24); here it describes the mob action of rejection — the same word, two contrasting communities.
7.59: Kyrie Iēsou, dexai to pneuma mou ('Lord Jesus, receive my spirit'): the parallel to Jesus's last word on the cross is exact: Jesus prays Ps 31:5 ('Father, into your hands I commit my spirit,' Luke 23:46). Stephen substitutes 'Lord Jesus' for 'Father' — addressing the risen, exalted Jesus with the prayer Jesus addressed to the Father. This is either an implicit statement of Jesus's divine status or the natural expression of a vision in which Jesus is visible. The martyrological formula ('receive my spirit') became a pattern in Christian martyr tradition, appearing in later martyrdom accounts (Polycarp, Ignatius). Stephen's death establishes the template for Christian martyrdom.
7.60: Kyrie, mē stēsēs autois tēn hamartian tautēn ('Lord, do not charge this sin against them'): the parallel to Jesus's prayer of forgiveness from the cross (Luke 23:34 — 'Father, forgive them, for they do not know what they are doing') is unmistakable. The use of 'Lord' in the context of v.59's 'Lord Jesus' suggests the prayer is again addressed to Jesus. Mē stēsēs (lit. 'do not stand/set against them') is a legal idiom — do not put this on their account. The forgiveness prayer immediately before death frames Stephen's death as not only mirroring Christ's death in its pattern but extending Christ's own forgiving prayer. The young man Saul witnessed both the dying prayer and the face 'like an angel' (6:15) — details Luke places in the narrative to frame Paul's later conversion.

8.26: The Ethiopian eunuch: Ethiopia (Aithiopia) in Greek-Roman geography referred to the Nubian kingdom south of Egypt (modern Sudan); the Candace was the queen-mother title of the ruling dynasty (Meroitic ktke; several queens held this title historically — Strabo 17.1.54; Pliny NH 6.186). As court treasurer (dynastes) he held significant administrative power. As a eunuch he was excluded from full Jewish assembly participation (Deut 23:1) but Isa 56:3-8 had promised eunuchs a 'monument and a name better than sons and daughters'. Philip's baptism of him is the first recorded fulfillment of that Isaianic promise: the Spirit-led inclusion of the excluded.
8.1: Saul's approval of Stephen's execution signals his role as an official enforcer — not a mob participant but a legal representative of the Sanhedrin's authority. The dispersion (diaspora) of the Jerusalem church echoes the Assyrian and Babylonian dispersions of Israel; Luke's use of the same concept transforms what appears to be catastrophe into the mechanism of the gospel's spread. Only the apostles remain — whether because they were protected by community respect (cf. 5:26) or because they understood themselves as called to Jerusalem is not explained.
8.2: Devout men (eulabeis andres) bury Stephen and mourn loudly — kopetos megas (great lamentation) is the vocabulary of formal Jewish mourning. Torah required burial of the executed (Deut 21:23); these men risk association with a condemned criminal by performing the rite. Stephen's burial and mourning contrasts with Saul's persecution: even in the moment of suppression, the community maintains its covenantal obligations.
8.3: Saul's house-to-house search (kata tous oikous eisporeuomenos) targeting both men and women reflects the synagogue network of Jerusalem: Christian communities met in homes, and Saul's enforcement required knowing which households were centers of the movement. The dragging (surōn) into prison is violent, public, and indiscriminate by gender — a detail Luke carries as evidence of the persecution's severity before the Damascus reversal.
8.4: The scattered believers preach the word everywhere (diēlthon euangelizomenoi ton logon): the dispersion achieves the opposite of what the persecutors intended. The Jerusalem church had been stationary for several years; the persecution provides the external pressure that activates the Acts 1:8 geographic expansion. The believers who scatter are not apostles (v.1) but ordinary disciples — the Pentecostal democratization of prophetic witness now extends to everyone in the community.
8.5: Philip goes to the city of Samaria (or 'a city of Samaria'). The Samaritans were the religiously mixed population left after the Assyrian resettlement of the northern kingdom (2 Kgs 17); they maintained their own version of the Torah and worshipped at Mount Gerizim rather than Jerusalem. Jews and Samaritans had deep mutual hostility (John 4:9 — 'Jews have no dealings with Samaritans'). Philip's Samaritan mission crosses a centuries-old ethnic-religious boundary that Jesus had already foreshadowed (Luke 10:33; John 4).
8.6: The crowds paid attention to what Philip said when they heard and saw the signs he did — the combination of authoritative proclamation and miraculous attestation is the standard Lukan pattern (2:22; 14:3). Homothymadon proseirchon (with one accord paying attention) echoes the unanimity of the Jerusalem community at Pentecost; the Samaritan community is drawn into the same Spirit-movement with similar communal response.
8.7: Unclean spirits crying out as they depart: the exorcism ministry in Samaria continues the pattern of Jesus's own Galilean ministry (Luke 4:33-37; 6:18). The same formula — spirits coming out 'crying with a loud voice' — appears in the synoptic exorcisms. The healings of the paralyzed and lame echo Acts 3 (Peter's temple healing). Philip's signs replicate both Jesus's own signs and Peter's earlier ones, establishing a consistent pattern of authenticated mission.
8.8: Great joy in the city (chara megalē): the Samaritan city's joy echoes the joy that characterized Jesus's mission (Luke 2:10; 15:7, 10; 24:52) and the Jerusalem community (Acts 2:46). Joy is Luke's signature response to salvation; its presence among Samaritans confirms that the Spirit's work is not ethnically bounded. The joy of Samaria will be reported back to Jerusalem (v.14) as evidence that the gospel has genuinely crossed the cultural barrier.
8.9: Simon the Magician (mageuōn) — the verb is from mageia, the technical term for the professional practice of spells, divination, and wonder-working common throughout the ancient Mediterranean. 'Magician' in this period was not pejorative in all circles; professional magic-practitioners (magoi) were well-paid consultants in Hellenistic and Roman contexts. Simon's claim to be 'someone great' (legōn einai tina heauton megan) is the self-promotion style of Hellenistic wonder-workers competing for patronage and reputation.
8.10: 'This man is the power of God that is called Great' (hē dynamis tou theou hē kaloumenē Megalē): the crowd's designation of Simon reflects syncretistic language blending Jewish divine-power vocabulary with Hellenistic wonder-worker titulature. Dynamis (power) is a Jewish term for divine energy (cf. 'the Power' as a circumlocution for God in Matt 26:64; Mark 14:62). Calling Simon 'the Great Power of God' shows the Samaritan community applying Jewish theological vocabulary to authenticate a Hellenistic magician — the syncretism Philip's mission will displace.
8.11: Simon had amazed the Samarians for a long time with magic (eksestathenai autous tais mageiais hikanon chronon): existēmi (amaze/astonish) is used here for the effect of magic as it elsewhere describes the effect of miracles (2:7, 12; 8:9, 13). Luke's use of the same reaction-word is deliberate: the crowd cannot distinguish magic from miracle by affective response alone; what distinguishes them is the source and the content.
8.12: When they believed Philip preaching about the kingdom of God and the name of Jesus Christ, they were baptized, both men and women: the baptism formula here is 'the name of Jesus Christ' rather than the Trinitarian formula of later Christian practice. The explicit inclusion of women (gynaikēs) in the baptismal list is a Lukan social marker — the community that forms around the gospel includes those marginalized by both Jewish and Hellenistic social hierarchies.
8.13: Simon himself believed and was baptized (episteusen kai baptistheis): Luke records Simon's apparent conversion without qualification. The later episode (vv.18-24) will reveal the shallowness of that conversion; but at this point Luke presents the same language as for other conversions. Ancient commentators (Justin Martyr, Irenaeus) made Simon Magus the founder of Christian Gnosticism; Acts' portrait is more ambiguous — a man who received the sacrament but not the grace it signified.
8.14: The Jerusalem apostles send Peter and John to Samaria when they hear that Samaria has received the word: the apostolic visitation functions as quality-control and communal legitimation. The Jerusalem church was the recognized center; the Samaritan conversion needed apostolic ratification to prevent schism. The sending of Peter and John — the two senior apostles, both named — signals the seriousness with which Jerusalem receives the Samaritan mission report.
8.15: Peter and John prayed for the Samaritans to receive the Holy Spirit (hopōs labōsin pneuma hagion): the prayer for Spirit-reception implies that water-baptism had occurred without the Spirit's manifest arrival. This sequence — baptism then Spirit — is anomalous in Acts (contrast 10:44-47 where Spirit precedes baptism; 19:5-6 where Spirit follows baptism immediately). The anomaly may reflect the Samaritans' liminal status: full incorporation required both the community's baptismal rite and the apostolic confirmation of the Spirit's acceptance.
8.16: 'For he had not yet fallen on any of them; they had only been baptized in the name of the Lord Jesus' — the Spirit's non-arrival despite valid baptism is Luke's deliberate puzzle. Various theological explanations have been offered: deficiency in faith, the need for apostolic mediation, or a unique dispensational moment in the church's expansion. Luke's concern is the Spirit's role as the divine confirmation of the gospel's geographic expansion: each major crossing of a boundary (Jew → Samaritan → Gentile) receives its own Spirit-certification.
8.17: They laid their hands on them and they received the Holy Spirit: the laying on of hands (epithesis tōn cheirōn) is a Jewish commissioning gesture (Num 27:18-23 — Moses laying hands on Joshua) repurposed as a channel of Spirit-transmission in the early church. Its use here by apostles specifically ties the Samaritan reception to the Jerusalem apostolic community. The visible manifestation that followed (which Simon saw, v.18) presumably resembled what had happened at Pentecost.
8.18: Simon offered money (prosenegken autois chrēmata) for the ability to transmit the Spirit: the offer reflects his professional framework — in Hellenistic magic, extraordinary abilities could be purchased from established practitioners. The term 'simony' (selling church offices or spiritual gifts) derives from this episode. Simon misunderstands the gift as a transferable technique; his commercial approach applies the logic of the patronage economy to what is, by definition, God's sovereign gift.
8.19: 'Give me also this authority (exousian tautēn)': Simon wants the apostolic authority to confer the Spirit-gift, not just to receive it. His request reveals the category error: he is operating in a system where spiritual power hierarchies are purchasable from those higher up. Peter's response refuses the transaction entirely and reframes the economy: the gift of God (dōrea tou theou) cannot be obtained with silver.
8.20: 'Your silver perish with you' (to argyrion sou syn soi eiē eis apōleian): a formula of imprecation — the same doom for the man and his money together. Apōleia (destruction) is the NT word for eschatological judgment (Matt 7:13; Phil 1:28). Peter's severity reflects the gravity of the offense: attempting to corrupt the channel of divine grace by commercial transaction is not merely an error but a fundamental corruption of the gospel's gift-character.
8.21: 'You have no share or portion in this matter' (ouk estin soi meris oud klēros en tō logō toutō): meris (share) and klēros (lot/inheritance) are the OT vocabulary for covenantal participation — the land portions of the twelve tribes (Josh 14-19) and the Levitical 'lot' in God himself. Peter declares that Simon is outside the covenant community despite his baptism; his 'heart is not right before God' (ouk estin eutheia hē kardia sou enanti tou theou).
8.22: 'Repent therefore of this wickedness of yours (apo tēs kakias sou tautēs) and pray to the Lord that, if possible, the intent of your heart may be forgiven you' — Peter's conditional offer of forgiveness ('if possible') is remarkably hesitant. The 'intent of the heart' (epinoian tēs kardias sou) — the formulated plan, the considered proposal — is what requires forgiveness: Simon had calculated the commercial transaction. The condition may reflect genuine uncertainty about whether Simon's repentance would be sincere given the depth of his heart's orientation.
8.23: 'You are in the gall of bitterness and in the bond of iniquity' (eis cholēn pikrias kai syndesmon adikias horō se onta): the gall of bitterness echoes Deut 29:18 LXX — the root of bitterness that produces apostasy. The bond of iniquity is Isa 58:6 LXX — the chains of wickedness from which Israel is to be freed. Peter diagnoses Simon's condition with the vocabulary of the covenantal curse: he is in the state that precedes Israel's exile and judgment, not the state of the gospel's liberation.
8.24: Simon's response — 'Pray for me to the Lord, that nothing of what you have said may come upon me' — is ambiguous: is it genuine repentance or merely fear of judgment? He asks the apostles to pray rather than praying himself; he focuses on avoiding punishment rather than expressing sorrow for the act. Second-century traditions developed Simon into the archheretic of early Christianity (Justin Martyr, Apol. 1.26; Irenaeus, Adv. Haer. 1.23), but Luke leaves his fate unresolved.
8.25: Peter and John returned to Jerusalem preaching to many Samaritan villages (kōmas tōn Samaritōn euēngelizonto): on the way back they extend the mission through the villages — the apostles who came to verify and confirm the Samaritan mission end up themselves extending it. The Jerusalem-Samaria boundary, so significant in Jewish social geography, is dissolved by the apostolic mission itself. The Samaritans are now part of the one community that Peter and John oversee.
8.27: The Ethiopian eunuch was a court official (dynastēs) of the Candace queen of the Ethiopians, in charge of her treasury (epi pasēs tēs gazēs autēs). Candace was a title, not a personal name (Greek writers including Strabo knew of Candace of Meroe). The Ethiopian kingdom of Meroe (modern Sudan) was known to Greek and Roman writers as a distant, prestigious civilization. Eunuchs could hold high administrative positions in Hellenistic courts; they were also excluded from 'the assembly of the LORD' in Deut 23:1 — a tension Isa 56:3-5 directly addresses with a promise of inclusion.
8.28: The eunuch was reading aloud from the prophet Isaiah as he returned in his chariot. Ancient literacy was auditory — silent reading was rare and notable (Augustine describes Ambrose's silent reading as remarkable, Conf. 6.3). Reading aloud in a moving vehicle suggests either a high literacy level (being able to maintain focus) or a desire to hear the text. His possession of an Isaiah scroll indicates both personal religious devotion and substantial wealth — a scroll was expensive to commission.
8.29: 'The Spirit said to Philip, Go over and join this chariot' (proselthen kai kollēthēti tō harmatī toutō): the Spirit's direct instruction links Philip's encounter to the series of divine directives in Acts (10:19-20; 13:2; 16:6-10). Kollaō (join/cling to) is the word for intimate association — the same word in Luke 15:15 for the prodigal son attaching himself to a citizen, and in 1 Cor 6:16-17 for sexual and spiritual union. Philip is to make immediate, close contact — not a casual inquiry but a full engagement.
8.30: 'Do you understand what you are reading?' (ara ge ginōskeis ha anaginōskeis): the Greek wordplay — ginōskeis... anaginōskeis (understand... read) — may be Luke's own Greek or may reflect the actual conversation in Greek. The eunuch's response — 'how can I, unless someone guides me?' — reflects the standard assumption of Jewish hermeneutics: Scripture requires an authorized interpreter. The Qumran pesher texts (1QpHab) operate on the same assumption — the text's meaning is disclosed through a moreh hatsedek (Teacher of Righteousness), someone with interpretive authority.
8.31: 'He invited Philip to come up and sit with him' (parekAlesen ton Philippon anabanta kathisai syn autō): the eunuch takes the initiative — his question (v.30) has identified Philip as a potential guide, and he acts on that identification with social decisiveness. His invitation is also a social levelling: a court treasurer inviting a travelling preacher to share his chariot is a status inversion that the episode's whole arc embodies — the insider of the Candace's court is an outsider from Israel's assembly (Deut 23:1), and the gospel dissolves the distinction.
8.32: The passage he was reading — Isa 53:7-8 — is the heart of the fourth Servant Song. 'As a sheep before its shearers is silent, so he did not open his mouth' evokes the image of passive, voiceless suffering — the Servant endures judgment without self-defence. In Jewish interpretation, the Servant figure had multiple identifications: Israel corporately, an idealized Israel, a specific prophet. The eunuch's question 'of whom does the prophet say this?' (v.34) reflects the hermeneutical openness of a thoughtful reader who knows the text is about someone specific but does not know who.
8.33: Isa 53:8 — 'In his humiliation his judgment was taken away. Who can describe his generation? For his life is taken away from the earth' — the LXX of this verse is notably different from the Hebrew MT, and it is the LXX Philip would have been reading with the eunuch. The LXX's 'his judgment was taken away in his humiliation' emphasizes the injustice of the verdict; 'who will describe his generation?' becomes a meditation on the mysterious identity of this suffering figure. The text invites interpretation.
8.34: 'Tell me, about whom does the prophet say this — about himself or about someone else?' (peri heautou ē peri heterou tinos): this hermeneutical question mirrors the contemporary Jewish debate about whether Isa 53 referred to Isaiah himself, to Israel collectively, or to the coming Messiah. The targum on Isaiah interpreted the Servant as the Messiah but removed the suffering to Israel's enemies; the Qumran community saw a connection between the Servant and their own sufferings. Philip's answer — 'beginning from this scripture he told him the good news about Jesus' — is the interpretive tradition that identifies the suffering Servant as Jesus.
8.35: 'Beginning from this scripture' (arxamenos apo tēs graphēs tautēs): Philip uses Isa 53 as his hermeneutical starting point, but the gospel includes more than the single passage — he 'told him the good news about Jesus' comprehensively. The pattern is the same as Jesus with the Emmaus disciples (Luke 24:27): beginning with Moses and all the prophets, explaining the things about himself. The apostolic hermeneutic reads the OT as a whole as the anticipatory story whose protagonist is Jesus.
8.36: 'Look, water! What prevents me from being baptized?' (idou hydōr, ti kōlyei me baptisthēnai): the eunuch's question introduces the key word kōlyō (prevent/hinder) that recurs in Acts' barrier-crossing episodes (10:47: 'can anyone withhold (kōlysai) water from these people?'; 11:17: 'who was I that I could hinder (kōlysai) God?'). The eunuch's question anticipates and preempts the possible objection — his Deuteronomic exclusion as a eunuch (Deut 23:1) is the barrier that the gospel, in light of Isa 56:3-5, dissolves.
8.37: Verse 37 (Philip's confession requirement and the eunuch's response) is absent from the oldest and best manuscripts (P45, P74, Sinaiticus, Vaticanus) and is a later scribal addition, probably inserted to supply the baptismal formula implied but not stated. The verse is an early liturgical interpolation showing how the church formalized the baptism narrative to include an explicit faith-confession. Its absence from the critical text does not affect the narrative: baptism follows immediately on the eunuch's question.
8.38: The chariot stopped, both Philip and the eunuch went down into the water, and Philip baptized him: the immersion is physically full — both going down into (katebēsan eis to hydōr... anebēsan ek tou hydōrou) and up from the water. The road from Jerusalem to Gaza ran through the Judean desert; there are several small streams and ponds along this route. The spontaneous baptism with road-side water mirrors Jewish proselyte immersion (also in water, also immersive) — the eunuch's conversion uses the ritual form that already existed for Gentile entry into Israel's covenant community.
8.39: The Spirit of the Lord snatched Philip away (pneuma kyriou hērpasen ton Philippon): harpazō (snatch/seize/transport) is the verb of supernatural relocation (1 Thess 4:17 — the eschatological 'catching up'; 2 Cor 12:2-4 — Paul's heavenly vision). The eunuch 'saw him no more, and went on his way rejoicing' — his joy is the Lukan mark of salvation (cf. 8:8; Luke 15). The narrative end is the eunuch's missionary return to Ethiopia: Philip's mission is complete with a single convert who carries the gospel to Africa.
8.40: Philip found himself at Azotus (Ashdod, the Philistine city on the coastal plain) and preached through all the towns until he came to Caesarea: the coastal strip between Azotus and Caesarea was a mixed Jewish-Gentile corridor with multiple small cities (Jamnia, Joppa, Antipatris, Caesarea). Philip's extended mission through this corridor connects the Samaritan and Ethiopian missions to his long-term base in Caesarea (21:8 — 'Philip the evangelist, one of the Seven'), where he will still be active decades later.

9.1: Paul's conversion on the Damascus road is recounted three times in Acts (9:1-19; 22:6-16; 26:12-18), each with slightly different emphases depending on the audience. The three accounts together give: a blinding light (9:3; 22:6; 26:13 — brighter than the noon sun), a voice in Aramaic/Hebrew (26:14: te Hebraidi dialektō — 'in the Hebrew language'), the question 'Saul, Saul why do you persecute me?', and the self-identification 'I am Jesus'. The Damascus road experience is Paul's foundational 'seeing the Lord' (1 Cor 9:1; 15:8) that qualifies him as an apostle — he received a resurrection appearance as authoritative as those given to the Twelve.
9.2: Letters from the high priest to the Damascus synagogues: the high priest held delegated Roman authority over diaspora Jewish communities in matters of religious law. Damascus had a significant Jewish population (Josephus, Jewish War 2.561 — claims 10,000-18,000 Jewish residents, though this may be exaggerated). Hē hodos (the Way) — used here for the first time as the community's self-designation and the persecutors' identifying term — is a Jewish intertextual reference (Isa 40:3; Ps 1:6; the Qumran community also used 'the Way' for their movement).
9.3: As Saul neared Damascus, a light from heaven flashed around him (periēstrapsen autōn phōs ek tou ouranou): the Damascus theophany uses the vocabulary of OT divine-appearance texts — Ezekiel's chariot vision opens with lightning-light (Ezek 1:4, 13, 27-28); the Sinai appearance was fire and light (Exod 19:16-18). The midday setting — Saul traveling the 140-mile journey from Jerusalem — places the theophany in the harshest light, so that the heavenly brightness can be perceived even then. Luke's three accounts progressively intensify the theophanic elements.
9.4: 'Saul, Saul, why are you persecuting me?' — the double-name vocative is the OT pattern for divine address in moments of critical commission: Abraham (Gen 22:11), Jacob (Gen 46:2), Moses (Exod 3:4), Samuel (1 Sam 3:10). The repetition conveys both intimacy and urgency. The identification of persecution of the church with persecution of Jesus reveals the solidarity between Christ and community that would become the theological center of Paul's own letters (1 Cor 12:12-27; Col 1:24).
9.5: 'Who are you, Lord?' (tis ei kyrie): the kyrie (lord/sir) may be merely polite address, not yet the full theological title. The response — 'I am Jesus whom you are persecuting' — is the theological shock: the exalted heavenly figure who speaks to Saul with divine light and power is not God directly but the crucified Nazarene Saul has been hunting. The identification requires Saul to reprocess both the resurrection (which he had rejected) and his own mission (which he had believed was pious).
9.6: 'Rise and enter the city, and you will be told what you are to do' (alla anastēthi kai eiselthe eis tēn polin, kai lalēthēsetai soi ho ti se dei poiein): the future passive 'you will be told' delays the commission content, building narrative tension. The same divine necessity (dei) that governed Jesus's journey to Jerusalem now governs Saul's entry to Damascus. The detail that Saul is dependent on being told what to do reinforces his fundamental passivity in his own conversion — he acts only in response to the divine initiative.
