9.7: The men traveling with Saul heard the voice but saw no one and stood speechless (eneoi): the contrast between Saul's experience (light and voice) and the companions' experience (voice only, seeing nothing) is a classic feature of Jewish theophany accounts — the divine encounter is personal and asymmetric. The companions are witnesses to the event's reality but not to its content. The slight differences between the three Damascus accounts (9:7 — they heard; 22:9 — they did not hear) may reflect different aspects of the single event or use of the same word in different senses.
9.8: Saul rose from the ground and though his eyes were open he saw nothing (ouden eblepen): the blindness is both physical and symbolic. In OT theophany narratives, Moses could not look directly at God's glory (Exod 33:20); the blinding of Saul represents the overwhelming of his physical perception by the light that exceeded it. The leading by the hand into Damascus (cheiragōgountes auton eisēgagon eis Damaskon) reverses his mission — he had come to bind believers; he arrives bound to a hand, dependent and helpless.
9.9: Three days without sight, eating, or drinking: the three-day pattern in biblical narrative is the preparation period before transformation (Jonah 1:17; Hos 6:2; the resurrection on the third day). Saul's three-day fast parallels the prophet's preparation before revelation. The complete fast — neither eating nor drinking (mē ephagen kai ouk epien) — signals the totality of the interior upheaval: his entire orientation toward God, Israel, and the gospel has been overturned.
9.10: Ananias was a disciple in Damascus: a Christian member of the Damascus community that Saul had come to arrest. His vision-dialogue with the Lord follows the call-narrative pattern of OT prophetic accounts: divine address, human response (idou egō kyrie — 'here I am, Lord', echoing Moses in Exod 3:4 and Isaiah in Isa 6:8), then the assignment. Ananias's subsequent hesitation (vv.13-14) mirrors Moses's objections (Exod 3:11-4:13) — the pattern of prophetic commission includes resistance before acceptance.
9.11: The street called Straight (tēn rhymēn tēn kaloumenēn Eutheian) is the main east-west colonnade of Damascus, still called Derb el-Mustaqim (the Straight Street) today. The archaeological persistence of the street name over two millennia is a striking example of the kind of geographical accuracy Luke demonstrates throughout Acts. The house of Judas is not further identified; the specificity of address is itself an authentication marker — the kind of detail preserved in local community memory.
9.12: Saul has seen in a vision Ananias coming to restore his sight: God prepares both the visitor and the visited simultaneously through parallel visions. The double-vision structure (10:3-6 / 10:9-16 for Peter and Cornelius) is a characteristic Lukan device for divine coordination: no single person is adequate for the encounter; God prepares both parties simultaneously. Saul's visionary anticipation of Ananias means that when Ananias arrives, Saul recognizes the fulfillment of the promise — sight restored as foretold.
9.13: Ananias's objection — 'Lord, I have heard from many about this man, how much evil he has done to your saints in Jerusalem' — uses the vocabulary of the Damascus community's received intelligence about Saul's persecution. Hagiois (saints/holy ones) is the self-designation of the Jerusalem community (used throughout Paul's letters for the Jerusalem collection beneficiaries). Ananias's hesitation is not lack of faith but reasonable fear — he has credible information about Saul's track record.
9.14: 'He has authority from the chief priests to bind all who call on your name': the phrase 'call on your name' (tous epikaloumenous to onoma sou) is the Joel 2:32 promise-language (Acts 2:21) now applied to the community that has acted on Peter's Pentecost sermon. It defines the Christian community liturgically — they are those who invoke the name of Jesus in prayer and worship. Saul's mission was to arrest this community; the Damascus encounter will transform the persecutor into its most effective advocate.
9.15: 'He is a chosen instrument of mine (skeuos eklogēs) to carry my name before the Gentiles and kings and the sons of Israel' — skeuos (vessel/instrument) is the pottery metaphor for human beings in divine hands (Jer 18:1-6; Rom 9:21-23; 2 Cor 4:7). Eklogē (election/choice) is the Pauline covenantal term for God's unconditional choosing. The threefold audience — Gentiles, kings, Israel's sons — anticipates the Acts narrative arc: Paul before Gentile crowds, Roman governors, and King Agrippa.
9.16: 'For I will show him how much he must suffer for the sake of my name' (hosa dei auton hyper tou onomatos mou pathein): the divine necessity (dei) of Paul's suffering is announced at his commissioning — his apostolate is inseparable from his passion. The trajectory is established at the beginning: Paul's ministry will replicate the suffering-then-glory pattern of the Servant (Isa 53) and of Jesus himself (Luke 9:22). The promise is not conditional on obedience but structural to the commission.
9.17: Ananias laid hands on Saul and called him 'Brother Saul' (Saoul adelphe): the address as 'brother' is the decisive gesture of community acceptance. Ananias, who had every reason for hostility, greets the persecutor as a fellow community member — the enacted parable of the gospel's reconciliation. The laying on of hands for Spirit-reception and healing mirrors the pattern of Peter and John in Samaria (8:17); the sequence is consistent across different conversions.
9.18: Something like scales fell from his eyes (epesan autou apo tōn ophthalmōn hōsei lepides): lepides (scales) is the word for fish scales and the crusted matter that can form over diseased eyes. The restoration of physical sight is simultaneous with the restoration of spiritual orientation — the symbolic blindness of the one who had been persecuting the Lord now lifts as Ananias names him brother. He rose and was baptized immediately — no period of instruction intervenes.
9.19: Saul took food and was strengthened (elaboN trophēn enethen): the three-day fast broken. He spent several days with the Damascus disciples — the period is unspecified; Gal 1:17 indicates Paul went to Arabia after Damascus before returning, but Luke's narrative compresses the timeline. The transition from persecutor to community member required the Damascus Christians to adjust their understanding of Saul; his remaining with them rather than immediately returning to Jerusalem signals his new primary community allegiance.
9.20: Saul immediately proclaimed Jesus in the synagogues, saying 'He is the Son of God' (hoti houtos estin ho huios tou theou): 'Son of God' is the Messianic title rooted in 2 Sam 7:14, Ps 2:7, and Ps 89:26-27. In Jewish usage it was a royal-covenantal designation for the Davidic king; Saul proclaims it of Jesus in the very synagogues he had come to purge of Jesus-followers. The synagogue as the first venue for apostolic preaching (cf. 13:5, 14; 14:1; 17:1-2) reflects the Pauline pattern Luke will document throughout the journey narratives.
9.21: 'Is this not the man who made havoc in Jerusalem of those who called upon this name?' (ho porthēsas eis Hierousalēm tous epikaloumenous to onoma touto): the Damascus Jewish community's astonishment is the mirror-image of Jerusalem's later disbelief (v.26). Porthēsas (made havoc/ravaged) — Paul uses the same word for his own persecution career in Gal 1:13 (eporthoun). The community's memory of Saul is recent and vivid; his reversal is all the more striking because they remember what he was doing just days or weeks before.
9.22: Saul increased in strength and confounded the Jews in Damascus by proving that Jesus was the Christ (symbibazōn hoti houtos estin ho Christos): symbibazō (bring together, prove by comparing) is the technical term for scriptural demonstration — bringing texts together to demonstrate a conclusion. Saul's Pharisaic training made him precisely equipped for this kind of argument; his conversion gave him the conclusion that his exegetical method could now defend. The Damascus Jews are 'confounded' (synecheonto) — thrown into confusion by the argument they cannot counter.
9.23: The plot to kill Saul after 'many days' (hikanai hēmerai): the pattern of Jewish opposition culminating in assassination plots recurs throughout Acts (9:23-24; 20:3; 23:12-16; 25:3). The 'many days' accommodates the Arabian sojourn of Gal 1:17 — Paul went to Arabia (perhaps for extended reflection) and returned to Damascus before the plot forced his departure. The enemies 'were watching the gates day and night' (paretēroun de kai tas pylas) — a besieged-city siege-mentality applied to controlling a single dangerous individual.
9.24: Their plot was made known to Saul (egnōsthē de tō Saulō hē epibolē autōn): someone in the conspiracy or the city administration warned him — Luke does not explain the channel of intelligence. 2 Cor 11:32-33 adds that the governor of Damascus under King Aretas IV Philopatris was guarding the city to capture Saul, connecting the plot to broader political opposition. The combination of Jewish religious opponents and Nabataean/Roman administrative power against one man anticipates the later pattern of Paul's trials.
9.25: His disciples lowered him in a basket through an opening in the wall (dia tou teichous chalasantes auton en spyridi): spyrida is a large woven basket — the same type used in the feeding of the four thousand (Mark 8:8). Damascus was a walled city; houses in ancient cities were often built against or into the city walls, with windows overlooking the external face (cf. Josh 2:15 — Rahab's house in the Jericho wall, through which the spies escaped). The basket-lowering is the undignified comedy of the apostolic career — escaping persecution through a laundry hamper.
9.26: Saul came to Jerusalem and tried to join the disciples, but they were all afraid of him (ephobounto auton pantes, mē pisteuontes hoti estin mathētēs): the Jerusalem community's fear is reasonable — Saul's transformation is unknown to them, and the report may have seemed like a ruse to infiltrate the community. The three-year interval (Gal 1:18 — 'three years later I went up to Jerusalem') since the persecution would have dimmed no memories of what Saul had done to families in this community. Barnabas's role as mediator (v.27) is critical.
9.27: Barnabas brought Saul to the apostles and told them how he had seen the Lord on the road and the Lord had spoken to him (kai hōs en tē hodō eiden ton kyrion kai hoti elalēsen autō): Barnabas functions as the community's trust-broker. His standing — established in 4:36-37 as a man of generous integrity — provides the social capital to vouch for Saul. The double authentication (Lord appeared, Lord spoke) is the apostolic resurrection-witness credential applied to Paul's Damascus experience. Gal 1:18 identifies the visit as a fifteen-day stay to 'get acquainted with Cephas'.
9.28: Saul was with them moving freely in Jerusalem, speaking boldly in the name of the Lord (en parrhēsia elalei en tō onomati tou kyriou): parrēsia (boldness/freedom of speech) is the characteristic mark of apostolic speech throughout Acts (4:13, 29, 31; 28:31). Saul's Jerusalem presence is a public reversal — the city where he had persecuted 'the Way' is now the city where he proclaims it openly. His activity with Hellenistic Jews (v.29) matches the pattern of Stephen, whose audience he once participated in executing.
9.29: He spoke and disputed with Hellenists (elalei te kai syzētei pros tous Hellēnistas), and they were seeking to kill him: Saul now occupies the exact social position Stephen had — debating with Greek-speaking Jews who cannot counter the argument and so resort to violence. The Hellenists who heard Stephen (6:9 — the synagogues of Alexandria, Cyrene, Asia, Cilicia) are now hearing Saul, who was himself a Hellenistic Jew from Tarsus. His reversal is complete: the Hellenistic Jew who approved of Stephen's death is now in Stephen's position.
9.30: The brothers sent Saul to Tarsus by way of Caesarea (katēgagon auton eis Kaisareian kai exapesteilan auton eis Tarson): Tarsus was Saul's home city (21:39; 22:3). The Jerusalem community removes Saul from immediate danger rather than permitting martyrdom. The 'silent years' in Tarsus (probably several years, 37-43 CE approx.) are when Paul received the experiences described in 2 Cor 12:2-4 (the third heaven) and undertook the Cilician and Syrian mission implied by Gal 1:21-22. Barnabas will retrieve him from Tarsus in 11:25.
9.31: The church throughout Judea, Galilee, and Samaria had peace (eirēnen eiches hē ekklēsia... oikodomimoumenē kai poreuomenē tō phobō tou kyriou): the summary notice provides a historical breathing space. The three regions — Judea, Galilee, Samaria — form the geographic boundary of Jesus's ministry and of the Acts 1:8 mission mandate. The 'walking in the fear of the Lord' and 'the comfort of the Holy Spirit' formula characterizes a community growing in both moral seriousness and spiritual resource. Historical context: the respite may correspond to Caligula's reign (37-41 CE) when Roman-Jewish tensions elsewhere reduced local Jewish pressure on the church.
9.32: Peter visited the saints at Lydda (dierchoumenos dia pantōn katēlthen kai pros tous hagious tous katoikountas Lydda): Peter makes a pastoral tour through the coastal plain — the corridor between Jerusalem and the Mediterranean that Philip had already evangelized. Lydda (modern Lod) was a significant market town and administrative center in this region. Peter's visit is pastoral oversight — checking on established communities — rather than primary evangelism. The 'saints' who dwell there form a settled community of some duration.
9.33: Aeneas, lying on a mat for eight years, paralyzed (ex etōn oktō katakenimenos epi krabattō, hos ēn paralalymenos): the eight-year paralysis establishes the miracle's completeness (cf. the lame man of thirty-eight years in John 5:5; the lame man from birth in Acts 3:2). Krabattos (mat/pallet) is the word Jesus uses in Mark 2:9-11 — 'take up your mat and walk'. The healing of Aeneas explicitly echoes the Gospel paralytic healings, placing Peter's miracle in the chain of authenticated signs that trace back to Jesus.
9.34: 'Aeneas, Jesus Christ heals you; rise and make your bed' (iātai se Iēsous Christos; anastēthi kai strōson seautō): the healing formula names Jesus Christ as the agent — Peter is a conduit, not a healer. The Aeneas healing is the only one in Acts where the formula is so explicit; other healings name 'the name of Jesus' but this one says 'Jesus Christ heals you' — present tense, direct agency. The command to make the bed (strōson seautō) confirms full physical restoration and echoes Jesus's commands to the paralyzed men to take up their mats.
9.35: All the residents of Lydda and Sharon (Saron) saw him and turned to the Lord: Sharon is the coastal plain stretching from Lydda north toward Caesarea — a wide, fertile corridor with multiple settlements. The mass response ('all... turned') is the Lukan summary pattern for regional evangelization (cf. 13:44: 'almost the whole city gathered'). The Lydda healing functions as a regional sign: its impact extends beyond the healing to communal conversion across a whole geographical region.
9.36: In Joppa (modern Jaffa, the port city on the coast) there was a disciple named Tabitha, or in Greek Dorcas — both names mean 'gazelle'. The dual naming (Aramaic/Hebrew and Greek) is characteristic of diaspora Jews and Jewish Christians in bilingual communities. Tabitha is described as 'full of good works and acts of charity' (plērēs ergōn agathōn kai eleēmosynōn) — her identity is defined by active benevolence, particularly clothing the poor widows (v.39). She represents the diaconal ministry that Acts 6 had institutionalized.
9.37: She became ill and died (asthenesasan de autēn... apethanein): the timing of the messengers' arrival in Lydda (two days' travel from Joppa) suggests Tabitha had just died when the community decided to send for Peter. The body was washed and laid in an upper room (hyperōon) — the same word as the upper room of Acts 1:13 and Luke 22:12. Jewish burial practice normally required immediate burial; the decision to wash but not yet bury implies the community hoped Peter could reverse the death.
9.38: The disciples in Joppa heard that Peter was in Lydda and sent two men to him (apesteilan duo andras pros auton parakalountes, Mē oknēsēs dielthein heōs hēmōn): 'Do not delay coming to us'. The urgency of the appeal — 'do not hesitate/delay' — implies the community knew what Peter had done in Lydda and believed he could act similarly for Tabitha. The two-messenger pattern mirrors the Jewish legal minimum for testimony (Deut 19:15); the messengers are both witnesses to the death and authenticators of the resurrection.
9.39: All the widows stood beside Peter weeping, showing him tunics and garments Dorcas had made while she was with them (epideiknymenai chitōnas kai himatia hosa epoiei met autōn Dorkos): the widows display the garments as evidence of Tabitha's ministry and as a plea for her restoration. In the ancient world, widows were among the most economically vulnerable — typically dependent on their deceased husbands' households or on community support. Tabitha's clothing-ministry was a direct material lifeline, and the widows' grief is not merely personal but practical.
9.40: Peter put all the widows and weepers outside, knelt and prayed, then turned to the body and said 'Tabitha, rise' (Tabeitha anastēthi): the expulsion of mourners and the private prayer before the miracle directly echoes Elijah (1 Kgs 17:19-20) and Elisha (2 Kgs 4:33-35) — both of whom withdrew with the dead child alone before the miracle. The Aramaic address 'Tabitha' (not 'Dorcas') confirms local tradition; it is the same pattern as Jesus's 'Talitha koum' to Jairus's daughter (Mark 5:41), which Aramaic readers would recognize as virtually identical in sound.
9.41: Peter gave her his hand and raised her up (dous de autē cheira anestēsen autēn): the hand-raising gesture is the same as Peter's lifting of the lame man in Acts 3:7. The physical gesture of raising connects healing and resurrection — the hand-up is both literal rehabilitation and the enacted sign of resurrection-life. 'He called the saints and widows, presenting her alive' (parestēsen autēn zōsan): the word paristēmi (present) with zōsan (alive) is the language of legal presentation of evidence — Peter presents Tabitha as proof of her resurrection.
9.42: It became known throughout all Joppa, and many believed in the Lord (egnōsthē de holēn tēn Ioppēn kai episteusan polloi epi ton kyrion): the pattern of miracle → community proclamation → mass conversion is consistent with the Lydda/Sharon response (v.35). Joppa was a port city — exposure to the event and its report would be rapid and wide. The parallelism between the Lydda and Joppa miracles (paralytic healed, dead raised; both cities respond) is Luke's literary shaping of the Petrine ministry as a coherent attestation sequence.
9.43: Peter stayed in Joppa for many days with one Simon, a tanner (para tini Simōni burssei): a tanner worked with animal hides, a process requiring dead animals and various chemical processes that made the trade ceremonially unclean in Jewish law — the Mishnah (m. Ketubot 7:10) lists tanners among professions whose wives could claim divorce due to the smell. Peter's choice to stay with a tanner signals the beginning of his movement toward Gentile inclusion (the next episode is Cornelius). His location in a trade house on the social periphery is deliberate preparation for what follows in chapter 10.

10.1: Cornelius is a Roman centurion of the Cohors Italica — an auxiliary unit stationed in Caesarea Maritima (Herod's coastal capital of Judea). Inscriptions attest an Italica cohort in Syria-Palestine in the first century (CIL III 14137). He is described as eusebēs kai phoboumenos ton theon ('devout and God-fearing') — the latter a technical designation in Acts (10:2, 22, 35; 13:16, 26) for Gentile sympathizers with Judaism who had not undergone full conversion (circumcision). God-fearers attended synagogues, gave alms to Jewish causes, observed some practices, but remained Gentiles. Cornelius's acceptance of the Spirit without prior circumcision becomes the precedent cited at the Jerusalem Council (15:7-11).
10.28: Jewish food laws and purity regulations created social barriers preventing shared table fellowship between observant Jews and Gentiles. The Mishnah (m. Avodah Zarah) regulated business and social contact with idolaters; meals in Gentile homes risked defilement through non-kosher food and vessels. Peter's vision of the sheet with unclean animals (10:11-16) — 'what God has made clean, do not call common' (ha ho theos ekatharisen sy me koinou) — addresses both food laws and their social function. Peter's insight (v. 28) that he should not call any person common or unclean shows he understood the vision's primary referent was not food but people.
10.2: The category 'devout and God-fearing' (eusebēs kai phoboumenos ton theon) describes a well-attested class of Gentile synagogue-adherents in the first-century diaspora. Inscriptions from cities across Asia Minor and Aphrodisias specifically attest theosebeis ('God-fearers') as a distinct group separate from full proselytes: they attended synagogue, observed monotheism and basic Jewish ethics, and contributed to Jewish community welfare, but had not undergone circumcision. Josephus mentions God-fearing Gentiles in Antioch (J.W. 7.3.3). The 'Italian Regiment' to which Cornelius belongs identifies him as part of the Roman military aristocracy — a figure of substantial social prestige choosing Jewish religious affiliation.
10.3: The 'ninth hour' (three in the afternoon) was one of the three traditional Jewish prayer-times (Ps 55:17 — 'evening, morning and noon I cry out'; m. Berakhot 4:1). This corresponds to the time of the afternoon tamid (daily sacrifice) in the temple. Josephus describes devout Jews maintaining prayer at this hour even in Rome (Ant. 14.10.12). Cornelius's observance of the ninth-hour prayer while stationed in Caesarea — far from the temple — is the God-fearer's diaspora practice of Jewish liturgical rhythms without direct temple participation. The angel's appearance at the prayer-hour is Luke's consistent pattern: divine communications come at moments of deliberate devotion.
10.4: 'Your prayers and gifts to the poor have come up as a memorial offering before God' — the language of ascending sacrifice applied to prayer and almsgiving echoes Tobit 12:12 ('I was sent to test you; at the same time, God sent me to heal you... When you prayed, I brought a reminder of your prayer before the Holy One'). The angel Raphael's similar function in Tobit — presenting prayers before God as a memorial — provides the conceptual framework: Cornelius's prayer and alms have been 'presented' to God as acceptable worship. Philo similarly describes prayer as equivalent to sacrifice in the life of the diaspora Jew (Spec. Leg. 1.272).
10.5: Joppa (modern Jaffa, south of Tel Aviv) was one of Judaea's few Mediterranean ports, approximately 55 kilometers south of Caesarea Maritima. Simon the tanner's location 'by the sea' (v.6) is both a geographic marker and a sociological detail: tanners required water for their craft and typically lived at the margins of settlements because of the smell. Peter lodging with a tanner situates him already on the social boundary — the guest of a ritually marginalized craftsman — before the vision pushes him further across the purity lines.
10.6: The tanner's profession (burseus — leather worker) placed him in a category of ritual marginality in rabbinic law: constant contact with animal carcasses made sustained Levitical purity extremely difficult. The Mishnah (m. Ketubot 7:10) listed the tanner's trade as grounds for divorce if a wife objected to marrying into the profession; tanners were typically required to live 50 cubits from the edge of town (m. Baba Batra 2:9). Peter's willingness to lodge with Simon the tanner thus already represents a relaxed attitude toward purity-boundary-maintaining — contextualizing the rooftop vision's more radical demand.
10.7: 'A devout soldier who was one of his attendants' (stratiōtēn eusebē tōn proskarterountōn autō) — Cornelius's household includes a soldier described with the same eusebēs (devout) as Cornelius himself. The Greek term proskarterountōn (those who attend/serve continuously) describes personal attendants or bodyguards. A centurion's household in Roman military culture included personal servants, freedmen, and soldiers assigned to his administrative service. The devout soldier represents the God-fearing influence extending through Cornelius's household structure.
10.8: Cornelius's sending of three men (two servants + one soldier) on a 55-kilometer journey to Joppa mirrors the precise number the Spirit reports to Peter ('three men are looking for you,' v.19). The two-way divine coordination — angel to Cornelius in Caesarea, Spirit to Peter in Joppa — is Luke's characteristic demonstration of divine orchestration operating simultaneously on two separated individuals. The journey of approximately 55 km on Roman roads would take about two days (consistent with 'the following day' in v.9 and 'the next day' in v.23).
10.9: Rooftop prayer was a recognized practice in Judaism; the flat roofs of Palestinian houses provided both privacy and the elevated orientation toward heaven associated with prayer. Rooftops appear throughout Jewish literature as spaces for prayer, meditation, and divine encounter: Daniel 6:10 (Daniel prays toward Jerusalem from his window); Judith 8:5 (she pitched a tent on her roof for fasting); Tobit 3:11 (Tobias went to the roof to pray). Peter's midday (sixth hour) prayer may represent a personal devotional practice beyond the three standard prayer times.
10.10: The 'trance' (ekstasis — literally 'standing outside oneself') is the technical Greek term for the visionary state in which ordinary consciousness is suspended and divine perception substituted. The LXX uses ekstasis for the deep sleep that fell on Adam during the creation of Eve (Gen 2:21) and Abraham's vision (Gen 15:12). Jewish apocalyptic literature uses the term for the prophetic state of reception (1 En. 71:11; 4 Ezra 10:30-57). Luke uses it for Peter's vision here and Paul's temple vision (Acts 22:17). The combination of hunger-induced physical receptivity and the onset of ekstasis is a recognized pattern in ancient descriptions of visionary experience.
10.11: 'Heaven opened' (ouranon aneōgmenon) is the standard theophany-formula in Jewish and Christian visionary literature: Ezekiel's chariot-vision begins 'the heavens were opened' (Ezek 1:1 LXX); the Synoptics use it for Jesus's baptism (Mark 1:10); Revelation 19:11 uses it for the final vision. The 'sheet' (othonē — a linen cloth, sail-canvas) let down by four corners images both a container for the animal contents and a spatial metaphor for the four corners of the earth (Isa 11:12 — God gathering Israel 'from the four corners of the earth').
10.12: The three categories of animals (four-footed creatures, reptiles, and birds) follow the creation-taxonomy of Genesis 1:24-25 and Leviticus 11's clean/unclean divisions. The Levitical food laws (Lev 11; Deut 14:3-21) governed which animals could be eaten by Israel, functioning as a daily marker of covenant distinctiveness. The sheet contained panta ta tetrapoda (all the four-footed animals) — the 'all' (panta) is the key: both clean and unclean together, without distinction. The vision's content is not primarily about food but about the category-system of clean/unclean that the food laws instantiated.
10.13: The divine command 'Get up, Peter. Kill and eat' (anastas thuson kai phage) represents the direct overriding of Levitical food law by divine command. The imperative anasta (rise!) is the same command-form used throughout Luke-Acts for healings and resurrection-announcements. The combination thuson kai phage (kill and eat — using thyō, the sacrificial-slaughter verb) presents the meal as a quasi-sacrificial act — the food prepared for eating is to be treated as an offering, authorized directly by God rather than by Levitical regulation.
10.14: Peter's refusal — 'I have never eaten anything impure or unclean' (oudepote ephagon pan koinon kai akatharton) — uses the two key technical terms of Jewish dietary law: koinos (common, i.e., ritually non-sacred and therefore defiling) and akathartos (unclean). The rabbinic tradition preserved in m. Hullin discusses in detail which animal parts and preparation methods result in tum'ah (impurity). Peter's claim of lifelong dietary purity is plausible given his Galilean Jewish formation; it also establishes the magnitude of the vision's demand — not a minor adjustment but a complete reversal of lifelong practice.
10.15: 'Do not call anything impure that God has made clean' — the theological claim is that God's creative authority supersedes the Levitical categorization system. The same reasoning appears in Mark 7:19 ('Thus he declared all foods clean') as an editorial comment on Jesus's teaching, and in Paul's argument in Romans 14:14 ('Nothing is unclean in itself; but it is unclean for anyone who thinks it is unclean') and 1 Corinthians 10:25-26 (citing Ps 24:1 — 'the earth is the Lord's and everything in it'). The common theological ground: God's ownership of creation overrides any secondary categorization derived from covenant-law regulations.
10.16: The triple repetition of the vision follows the biblical and rabbinic convention that a divine communication occurring three times establishes certainty and eliminates the possibility of misinterpretation. Joseph's dreams were interpreted as certain because of their repetition (Gen 41:32 — 'the reason the dream was given to Pharaoh twice is that the matter has been firmly decided by God'). The three repetitions of Peter's vision follow this convention: the triple command removes any ambiguity about the divine will. The sheet's immediate withdrawal (anelēmphthē) before Peter has resolved the vision's interpretation is deliberate — he must hold the unresolved meaning in mind as the Cornelius messengers arrive.
10.17: The divine timing of the Cornelius messengers' arrival at the gate while Peter is still perplexed about the vision's meaning is Luke's characteristic demonstration of divine simultaneity: two events in two cities, coordinated by one divine director. The 'gate' (pylōna) of Simon the tanner's house is the entrance-passage of a courtyard house — the standard Palestinian domestic architecture where a gatehouse or gate-room (pylōn) provided access from the street to the inner courtyard. The Cornelius messengers standing at the gate while Peter is on the roof represents the physical distance between the purity-boundary (house interior) and the outside world.
10.18: The messengers' inquiry about 'Simon who is known as Peter' (Simōna ton epikaloumenon Petron) uses the double-name designation that Luke consistently employs throughout Acts for the apostle. The Aramaic Simon (the name Jesus gave; Matt 16:17-18) and the Greek Peter (translation of Kepha/Cephas — rock) together signal the bilingual Jewish-Hellenistic context: Peter is identifiable by either name, reflecting his movement between Aramaic-speaking Palestinian Jewish contexts and Greek-speaking diaspora contexts.
10.19: The Spirit's direct address to Peter ('Simon, three men are looking for you') provides the interpretive bridge between the food-vision and the arriving Gentile delegation. The Spirit (pneuma) who speaks directly and audibly to Peter is the same Spirit who will fall on Cornelius's household (v.44-45), connecting the two events in a single pneumatological arc. The precision of 'three men' confirms that the Spirit has real-time knowledge of the situation at the gate — the divine orchestration operates with exact detail, not general providence.
10.20: 'Do not hesitate to go with them, for I have sent them' (mēden diakrinomenos hoti egō apestalka autous) — the Spirit's claim that Cornelius's Gentile messengers are the Spirit's own ambassadors is the theological watershed: the pagan centurion's servants are here declared to be divinely commissioned. Apostellō (to send, the verb from which 'apostle' derives) — the Spirit uses apostolic language for Cornelius's household servants. The Spirit's claim of ownership over the Gentile messengers will be the basis for Peter's defense in 11:12 ('the Spirit told me to go with them').
10.21: Peter's hospitality to the Gentile messengers (v.23 — 'Peter invited the men into the house to be his guests') is itself a boundary-crossing act: Jewish purity rules discouraged hosting Gentiles in Jewish homes. The Mishnah (m. Oholot 18:7) declares that 'the dwellings of Gentiles are unclean.' Peter's invitation of the three Gentiles into Simon the tanner's house — already a ritually marginal location — represents the first step of the practical boundary-crossing that the vision authorized.
10.22: 'Respected by all the Jewish nation' (martyroumenon hypo holou tou ethnous tōn Ioudaiōn) — Cornelius's reputation with the Jewish community in Caesarea is a significant social detail. A Roman centurion who was a God-fearer and philanthropist toward the Jewish community would have been known and respected across that community. Josephus mentions similar Gentile benefactors of Jewish communities in diaspora cities (J.W. 7.3.3). The messengers' description of Cornelius positions him as the most favorably situated possible Gentile recipient of the gospel — not a hostile Roman but a respected friend of Israel.
10.23: 'Some of the believers from Joppa went along' (v.23 — 'six brothers' according to 11:12) — the witnesses traveling with Peter establish the Deuteronomic two-or-three-witnesses requirement for legally valid testimony (Deut 19:15). Their presence at Cornelius's house makes them the corroborating witnesses who will testify to the Jerusalem church in 11:12 ('these six brothers also accompanied me'). Luke consistently documents the witness-structures that give apostolic events their evidential standing before the wider community.
10.24: Caesarea Maritima was Herod the Great's showpiece city, built between 22-10 BCE: a completely artificial deep-water harbor (the largest in the ancient Mediterranean), a Roman grid city plan, temples, an amphitheater, a hippodrome, and an aqueduct. As Rome's administrative capital of Judaea, Caesarea housed the Roman prefect/procurator's residence and the garrison of Roman troops. Cornelius's house in Caesarea — the Roman city par excellence — means that the first Gentile conversion takes place not in Jerusalem's Jewish heartland but in Rome's representative presence in Palestine.
10.25: Cornelius's falling at Peter's feet (pesōn epi tous podas proskunēsen) is the obeisance-gesture associated with divine or royal figures. Proskuneō encompasses the range from prostration before a god to bowing before a king to kneeling before a superior. In the Roman military context, soldiers performed structured gestures of respect before their superiors; Cornelius's prostration before Peter represents the highest-intensity respect gesture available, appropriate for someone he has received divine instruction to contact. The same gesture appears at Revelation 19:10 and 22:8 (John falls before the angel) with the same correction: 'stand up.'
10.26: Peter's refusal of worship ('Stand up, I am only a man myself,' kai egō autos anthrōpos eimi) follows the consistent apostolic pattern of deflecting divine honors in Acts (14:14-15 — Paul and Barnabas tear their clothes at Lystra). The same refusal appears in the Greek-speaking world's descriptions of the proper response to divine attribution: Plutarch describes how virtuous rulers refused divine honors as a matter of philosophical propriety. The contrast with Herod Agrippa (12:21-23 — who accepts divine acclamation and is struck dead) will highlight the apostolic behavior as the correct posture before God-attributed glory.
10.27: The 'large gathering of people' (pollous synelēluthotas) that Cornelius had assembled represents the household-as-social-network: relatives (syngeneis) and close friends (ananagkaioi philoi — those bound by close obligation). The gathering of an extended household for a significant occasion was standard practice in Greco-Roman social life; the patron-client relationship structure meant that a centurion's household encompassed a broad social network. Cornelius's assembled household becomes the first Gentile community baptized as a group — the household as mission-unit rather than the individual as conversion-unit.
10.29: 'When I was sent for, I came without raising any objection' (anadikritōs — without discriminating/raising objections) — the word anadiakritōs echoes the Spirit's command not to 'discriminate/hesitate' (mēden diakrinomenos, v.20). Peter's report to Cornelius is the first step of the double-narrative structure Luke employs throughout the Cornelius episode: everything is reported twice (vision → report to Cornelius; Cornelius's vision → report to Peter; Peter's visit → report to Jerusalem). The double-narration serves Luke's historiographical purpose of providing multiple-witness confirmation for the theologically decisive event.
10.30: 'Three days ago I was in my house praying at three in the afternoon' — Cornelius's precise temporal reconstruction confirms the divine synchronization: the same ninth hour that was his regular prayer time (v.3) was the hour of the angelic visit. The 'man in shining clothes' (anēr en esthēti lampra) is described differently here (shining clothes) than in v.3 (where Luke simply narrates 'an angel'): the first-person report uses the phenomenological description (what Cornelius saw), while Luke's third-person narration uses the interpretive description (what it was). The same two-perspective technique appears in Acts 9 (Luke narrates Paul's Damascus encounter; Paul reports it in 22:6-16; 26:12-18).
10.31: 'God has heard your prayer and remembered your gifts to the poor' — the angel's message to Cornelius deploys two OT covenant-response formulas: God 'hearing' prayer (eisēkousthē sou hē proseuchē) echoes the Psalms' frequent affirmation of God's attentiveness to the righteous (Ps 4:3; 17:6; 34:15-17); God 'remembering' (emnēsthēsan) is the covenant-memory vocabulary of Gen 8:1 (God remembered Noah), Gen 19:29 (God remembered Abraham), Gen 30:22 (God remembered Rachel). The angel frames Cornelius's Gentile prayer and almsgiving within the covenant-response pattern that belongs to Israel.
10.32: The repeated mention of Simon the tanner 'who lives by the sea' (para thalassan) — first in the angel's message (v.6), now in Cornelius's report — is Luke's verification technique: the precision of the address (name, profession, location) is repeated exactly across the two narrative tellings, confirming the reliability of the communication. Joppa's location on the Mediterranean coast and the tanner's house 'by the sea' are geographically consistent: tanneries required constant water access.
10.33: 'We are all here in the presence of God to listen to everything the Lord has commanded you to tell us' — Cornelius's framing of the assembled household as a divine assembly (enōpion tou theou — before God) positions the Gentile gathering as a theophanic occasion. The language recalls the Sinai assembly ('Israel stood before God,' Exod 19:17) and the synagogue service as a gathering before the divine presence. Cornelius's theological characterization of his own household gathering is more sophisticated than might be expected from a pagan soldier — the God-fearer's extended engagement with Jewish theological vocabulary is evident.
10.34: The phrase 'God does not show favoritism' (prosōpolēmptēs — one who accepts/shows the face/person) is a NT hapax (only here in the NT as a noun; the related verb prosōpolēmpteō appears in Jas 2:9). The compound represents the Hebrew nāśā' pānîm ('to lift the face/show favor'), a phrase used throughout the OT for showing partiality in judgment (Lev 19:15; Deut 1:17; 16:19). Peter's theological insight — that what he had always believed about God's impartial justice in individual moral judgment now extends to ethnic inclusion in the covenant community — is the watershed theological realization of the chapter.
10.35: 'Everyone who fears him and does what is right is acceptable to him' — the principle of divine acceptance based on reverential fear and right-action rather than ethnic membership was not wholly new in Jewish tradition. The OT regularly affirmed that God's concern extended to all nations (Gen 12:3; Amos 9:7 — 'Are not you Israelites the same to me as the Cushites?'). Philo argued for God's universal justice. The Noahide commandments (rabbinic tradition: b. Sanhedrin 56a-60a) defined the obligations incumbent on all humanity before God. Peter's insight builds on existing Jewish universalism but extends it to include Gentiles in the messianic community without requiring circumcision.
10.36: 'The message God sent to the people of Israel, announcing the good news of peace through Jesus Christ' — Peter's brief sermon rehearsal (vv.36-43) for Cornelius's household follows the pattern of Paul's sermon in Acts 13:23-41 and the summary of early preaching preserved in 1 Corinthians 15:3-5. Both include: the John-Baptist context, the Galilean ministry, the Jerusalem death, the resurrection, the appearances, and the witness-claim. This consistent preaching structure (the kerygma) across multiple Acts speeches suggests Luke is working with a preserved summary of early Christian proclamation.
10.37: 'You know what has happened throughout the province of Judea, beginning in Galilee' — Cornelius's presumed knowledge of the Jesus-movement is plausible: as a centurion in Caesarea, he would have access to official reports, local gossip, and the accounts of Jewish informants about the religious teacher who had caused significant political disturbance in Jerusalem three to five years before. Josephus's reference to Jesus (Ant. 18.3.3 — the Testimonium Flavianum, even in its debated form) suggests that the movement was regionally known. Peter's appeal to what Cornelius 'knows' (hymeis oidate) grounds the proclamation in shared background knowledge.
10.38: 'God anointed Jesus of Nazareth with the Holy Spirit and power, and how he went around doing good and healing all who were under the power of the devil' — the summary of Jesus's ministry in terms of anointing (echrisen — from which Christos/Christ derives), Holy Spirit, power (dynamis), benefaction (euergetōn — the civic benefactor vocabulary), and healing against demonic opposition provides the theological framework in which the Jesus-story would be comprehensible to a Roman military audience: a divinely commissioned benefactor empowered by the divine spirit who liberated people from a hostile supernatural power.
10.39: 'We are witnesses of everything he did in the country of the Jews and in Jerusalem. They killed him by hanging him on a cross' (kremesantes epi xylou — hanging on a tree) — the apostolic witness-claim ('we are witnesses') is the foundation of the proclamation: the apostles are not transmitting doctrine but reporting events they personally observed. The crucifixion's public, state-sponsored nature meant it was verifiable by anyone who had been in Jerusalem during Passover — Cornelius as a Roman officer might have had access to Roman records of a public execution under Pontius Pilate.
10.40: 'God raised him from the dead on the third day and caused him to be seen' (edōken auton emphanē genesthai — allowed/caused him to become visible/manifest) — the third-day formula is among the earliest fixed elements of Christian proclamation (1 Cor 15:4 — 'raised on the third day according to the Scriptures'). The passive formulation ('caused him to be seen') emphasizes that the resurrection appearances were divinely controlled: God chose the witnesses, God selected the occasions. The passive voice consistently ascribes the resurrection to divine action rather than natural events.
10.41: 'Not to all the people, but to witnesses whom God had already chosen — by us who ate and drank with him after he rose from the dead' — the selectivity of the resurrection appearances (not to all Jerusalem but to specific pre-chosen witnesses) distinguishes the resurrection from hallucination or mass suggestion: the appearances were targeted and controlled by divine selection. The 'ate and drank with him' (sunepiomen kai synephagomen autō) formula is the bodily-resurrection apologetic: spiritual visions do not eat and drink; the resurrection appearances involved shared meals (Luke 24:41-43; John 21:9-13).
10.42: 'He commanded us to preach to the people and to testify that he is the one whom God appointed as judge of the living and the dead' — the title 'judge of the living and the dead' (kritēs zōntōn kai nekrōn) is the earliest attested formula for Jesus's judicial role at the final judgment (2 Tim 4:1; 1 Pet 4:5). In the Roman legal context (Cornelius's world), the appointment of a specific judicial authority (horistheis hypo tou theou — appointed/designated by God) as the definitive court of appeal would be comprehensible. The resurrection (v.40) is the basis for this judicial appointment: the one who conquered death has authority to preside over all life and death.
10.43: 'All the prophets testify about him that everyone who believes in him receives forgiveness of sins through his name' — Peter's appeal to 'all the prophets' (pantes hoi prophētai) corresponds to the 'Moses and all the Prophets' of the Emmaus road exposition (Luke 24:27) — the claim that the entire prophetic corpus witnesses to Jesus. Specific prophets cited in Acts include: Joel (2:17-21), Amos (15:16-17), Isaiah (7:17-52; 8:32-33; 28:25-27), and the Psalms (2:25-28; 4:25-26). The universal-scope 'everyone who believes' (panta ton pisteuonta) will be immediately tested and demonstrated by the Spirit's falling on the uncircumcised Gentile hearers before Peter finishes speaking.
10.44: The Spirit's interruption of Peter's sermon before its conclusion — 'while Peter was still speaking these words' (eti lalountos tou Petrou ta rhēmata tauta) — is both dramatic and theologically decisive: the Spirit does not wait for formal completion of the gospel presentation, the invitation, the prayer, or the response. The Spirit falls on faith-generated receptivity, not on a completed ritual sequence. This 'anti-ritual' aspect of the Cornelius Pentecost will be Peter's strongest argument to the Jerusalem church (11:15 — 'as I began to speak, the Holy Spirit came on them as he had come on us at the beginning').
10.45: 'The circumcised believers who had come with Peter were astonished' (exestēsan — were beside themselves, thoroughly amazed) — the word existēmi (to stand outside oneself) is related to ekstasis (trance, v.10) — the same family of words for being outside ordinary consciousness. The Jewish Christian witnesses to Cornelius's Pentecost are as 'beside themselves' with astonishment as Peter was in his trance — the divine surprise operates equally on both recipient and observer. 'Even on Gentiles' (kai epi ta ethnē) — the kai (even, also) is the shock-marker: the Pentecost gift has crossed the boundary that Jewish theology had maintained between Israel and the nations.
10.46: 'They heard them speaking in tongues and praising God' — the twin signs of Gentile Spirit-reception (glossolalia + praise) match exactly the Acts 2 Pentecost signs (2:4 — spoke in tongues; 2:11 — declaring the wonders of God in our own languages). Luke's consistent deployment of the Pentecost sign-pattern at each geographic expansion of the Spirit's reach (Jerusalem, Samaria/ch.8, Caesarea, Ephesus/ch.19) creates a narrative theology of Spirit-geography: the same Spirit who inaugurated the Jewish-Christian community at Pentecost is progressively claiming new territory without modification or diminution.
10.47: 'Surely no one can stand in the way of their being baptised with water?' (mēti to hydōr kōlysai dynatai tis) — the baptism-prevention question (kōlyō — to prevent, hinder, forbid) appears again at 8:36 ('what prevents me from being baptized?') and in early Christian baptismal liturgy (Didache 7; Justin Martyr, Apology 1.61). The rhetorical question form — 'can anyone prevent?' — is the standard formula for baptismal inclusion: the burden of proof rests on preventing, not on baptizing. The Spirit's prior gift makes water-baptism the formal seal of what has already occurred inwardly.
10.48: 'Baptised in the name of Jesus Christ' (en tō onomati Iēsou Christou) — the single-name baptismal formula in Acts (2:38; 8:16; 10:48; 19:5) has been extensively debated relative to Matthew 28:19's trinitarian formula. The two formulas are not contradictory: baptism 'in Jesus's name' identifies the authority and community into which one is baptized; the trinitarian formula specifies the divine persons who are invoked. Early Christian practice likely varied, with Acts reflecting the primitive Aramaic formula and Matthew the developed Greek liturgical form. 'They asked Peter to stay with them for a few days' — the post-baptismal table-fellowship (implied in the extended stay) sealed the social integration that the Spirit and water had enacted.

13.1: The Antioch church leadership list (13:1) is strikingly diverse: Barnabas (Levite from Cyprus), Simeon called Niger (African; Niger = black in Latin, likely from North Africa), Lucius of Cyrene (another North African), Manaen (a court associate of Herod Antipas — remarkable social connection), and Saul. This is intentional Lukan sociology: the first sending church includes every stratum — Diaspora Jew, African, royal court, persecutor-turned-apostle. Prophets and teachers are distinguished from the Twelve (1:2) — these are second-generation leaders operating under the Spirit's direction through liturgical discernment (leitourgountōn, worshipping, v. 2).
13.2: Leitourgountōn de autōn tō kyriō kai nēsteuontōn ('while they were worshipping the Lord and fasting'): leitourgousin is the LXX and NT word for priestly/cultic service (from which 'liturgy' derives). The Spirit's commission comes during communal worship and fasting — not a planning meeting or individual vision. The corporate practice of spiritual discernment through fasting was a recognized Jewish practice (Esth 4:16; Neh 1:4; Dan 9:3). The Spirit speaks through the gathered worshipping community; the corporate response of v.3 confirms it. The Antioch church commissions its two best leaders — giving up its own resources for the mission.
13.3: Epithentas tas cheiras autois apelisan ('placing their hands on them, they sent them away'): laying on of hands for commissioning was a Jewish practice (Num 27:18-23, Moses commissioning Joshua; Acts 6:6, commissioning the deacons) adopted and developed by the early church. The gesture transferred authority, blessing, and (in some contexts) Spirit-empowerment. The additional fasting and prayer before the laying on of hands is the full commissioning sequence: community discernment → prayer → fasting → laying on of hands → release. The community freely sends its most gifted leaders — a countercultural generosity for any institution, ancient or modern.
13.4: Apelthontes eis Seleukian ('they went down to Seleucia'): Seleucia Pieria was Antioch's port city, about 16 miles away on the Mediterranean — the natural embarkation point for Cyprus. The explicit phrase 'sent on their way by the Holy Spirit' (ekpemphthentes hypo tou hagiou pneumatos) reframes the geography: both the community and the Spirit send them. Cyprus was Barnabas's homeland (4:36, 'a Levite from Cyprus'). Beginning on familiar territory — where Barnabas had family, social connections, and knowledge of the Jewish community — reflects practical missionary wisdom alongside Spirit-direction.
13.5: En tais synagōgais tōn Ioudaiōn ('in the Jewish synagogues'): the 'to the Jew first' pattern (Rom 1:16) — synagogue preaching as the entry point — characterizes Paul's missionary strategy throughout Acts (13:5, 14; 14:1; 17:1-2, 10, 17; 18:4; 19:8). The synagogue was not merely a religious building; it was the center of Jewish communal life — where Torah was read, disputes adjudicated, charity organized. Beginning there gave the apostolic message its Hebrew-Bible context, its theological vocabulary, and an audience familiar with the prophetic texts the message claimed were fulfilled. Salamis was the main port of eastern Cyprus, likely having a substantial Jewish community.
13.6: Magos pseudoprophētēs Ioudaios ('a Jewish sorcerer and false prophet'): magos (magician/sorcerer) originally meant Persian priestly functionaries; it had come to mean anyone practicing divination, magic, or astrology for hire. Jewish names were frequently used in magical papyri because of the reputation of Jewish divine names for efficacy. Bar-Jesus = 'son of Jesus/Joshua' — an Aramaic patronymic. That he was attached to the proconsul's household reflects the ancient patronage relationship: diviners and magicians served elite clients as personal advisors. Paphos was the western end of Cyprus and the Roman administrative center — the seat of the proconsul.
13.7: Sergiō Paulō anthupatō ('the proconsul Sergius Paulus'): Cyprus was a senatorial province, governed by a proconsul (anthupatos) appointed by the Roman Senate. Sergius Paulus is attested in epigraphy: inscriptions from Cyprus and from Rome and Pisidian Antioch show his family's connections. The Pisidian Antioch family connection may explain why that city was Paul's next destination after Cyprus (13:14). Andri synetō ('an intelligent man'): Luke's neutral-positive characterization sets up the genuine response of v.12. Roman officials of senatorial rank often had genuine philosophical and religious interests; access to Josephus and other Jewish intellectuals was common in elite Roman circles.
13.8: Anthistato autois ho Elymas ho magos ('Elymas the sorcerer opposed them'): the parenthetical note — 'for that is what his name means' — suggests Elymas is a transcription of an Aramaic or Arabic word for 'wise man' or 'powerful one.' Various etymologies are proposed; none is certain. Bar-Jesus's dual identity (Jewish patronymic + professional Greek title) shows him operating in both Jewish and Hellenistic registers — the same cultural code-switching as Paul himself, though to opposite ends. His opposition to Paul and Barnabas is self-interested: if the proconsul converts, the sorcerer loses his patron and livelihood.
13.9: Saulos de ho kai Paulos ('Saul, also called Paul'): the name transition happens at precisely the first engagement with a Roman official in a Roman administrative center — the bilingual Jewish-Roman man operating in a Roman context shifts to his Roman name. Many Diaspora Jews used a Hebrew/Aramaic name within the community and a Greco-Roman name in the wider world; the names were not necessarily related etymologically. Saul/Saulos was his Jewish name (from the tribe of Benjamin, after King Saul, Phil 3:5); Paulus was his Roman cognomen. From this point Luke uses 'Paul' consistently — signaling the shift in narrative focus toward the Gentile mission world.
13.10: Huie diabolou, echthre pasēs dikaiosynēs ('son of the devil, enemy of all righteousness'): Paul's rebuke follows the pattern of OT prophetic invective (Ezek 13:10-16 against false prophets; Isa 5:20 against those calling evil good). 'Son of the devil' (huios diabolou) contrasts with Bar-Jesus's name ('son of Jesus/Joshua') — pointed irony. 'Perverting the right ways of the Lord' (diastrephōn tas hodous kyriou tas eutheias) echoes Hos 14:9 LXX and connects the rebuke to the 'Way' vocabulary used for the Christian movement throughout Acts. The sorcerer who distorts the Way is the antithesis of the movement's own identity.
13.11: Cheiragōgon zētōn ('groping, seeking someone to lead him by the hand'): the punishment — temporary blindness — echoes Paul's own blinding on the Damascus road (9:8-9). Paul, who was blinded when opposing the truth (9:1-4) and led by the hand into Damascus (9:8), now pronounces temporary blindness on one opposing the truth and groping for a hand to lead him. The symmetry is deliberate — Luke uses Paul's past as the template for his prophetic action. 'Mist and darkness' (achlys kai skotos) are used in the LXX for spiritual blindness (Isa 60:2; Ps 88:6 LXX). The blinding is temporary — 'for a time' (achri kairou) — leaving open the possibility of repentance.
13.12: Episteusen ekplēssomenos epi tē didachē tou kyriou ('he believed, being amazed at the teaching about the Lord'): the first recorded conversion of a Roman official of senatorial rank. What produced faith was the didachē (teaching) — the content of the apostolic message — not the miracle alone. Luke consistently connects miracles to teaching in Acts; miracles create openness, but the content produces faith. The 'teaching about the Lord' marks the proconsul's conversion as genuine doctrinal conviction, not merely wonder at a power-display — the same distinction Luke makes throughout the healing and miracle narratives of Acts.
13.13: Iōannēs de apochōrēsas autōn hypestrepsen eis Hierousalēm ('John departed and returned to Jerusalem'): Luke narrates Mark's departure without explanation or comment — a historian's restraint. Paul's later sharp refusal to take Mark on the second journey (15:38, calling it 'desertion') indicates the abandonment was significant. Ancient and modern interpreters speculate about reasons: homesickness, the rigor of the upcoming Taurus mountain crossing, theological disagreement about the Gentile mission, concern about the shifting leadership from Barnabas to Paul. Perga was the natural disembarkation point on the Pamphylian coast; continuing required commitment to the difficult inland journey. Luke records the crack without adjudicating it.
13.14: Eis Antiocheian tēn Pisidian ('to Pisidian Antioch'): a Roman colony in the province of Galatia (or Phrygia-Galatica), about 100 miles inland from Perga across the Taurus mountain range — a significant and dangerous journey. Paul's reference in 2 Cor 11:26 to 'danger from rivers, danger from bandits' may reflect this route. Founded by Seleucus I and refounded as a Roman colony under Augustus, Pisidian Antioch was a strategically important military and administrative center. The Sergius Paulus family inscription from this city — the same proconsul Paul had just converted — may indicate he gave Paul a letter of introduction to his family connections there.
13.15: Meta tēn anagnōsin tou nomou kai tōn prophētōn ('after the reading from the Law and the Prophets'): the synagogue liturgy included prescribed Torah sections (parashahs) and associated prophetic readings (haphtarahs), followed by teaching/homily by any qualified male. The invitation — 'if you have a word of encouragement (logos paraklēseōs) for the people' — reflects the openness of ancient synagogue practice to visiting teachers. The word paraklēsis (encouragement, consolation, exhortation) echoes Isa 40:1 ('Comfort, comfort my people') — positioning Paul's speech as the consolation Israel had been awaiting.
13.16: Anastas de Paulos kai kataseisas tē cheiri ('Paul stood and motioned with his hand'): standing was the orator's position in Hellenistic rhetoric; the hand gesture silenced the crowd for attention — a standard rhetorical opening in Jewish and Greco-Roman contexts. Paul's opening address — 'Fellow Israelites and God-fearers who worship God' — includes both ethnic Jews and Gentile God-fearers (Greek-speaking pagans attracted to Judaism who attended synagogue without full conversion). God-fearers were a significant Diaspora phenomenon; they would become a primary audience for the Pauline mission (13:43, 50; 17:4, 17; 18:7).
13.17: Ho theos tou laou toutou Israel exelexato tous pateras hēmōn ('the God of this people Israel chose our ancestors'): Paul's speech begins with election — God's initiative — which is the foundation of Israel's entire narrative. The structure parallels Stephen's speech (ch7): OT history from Abraham → exodus → wilderness → conquest → judges → kings → David → Jesus. But where Stephen's survey built to accusation, Paul's builds to proclamation. Each stage is God's action — 'he chose,' 'he led them out,' 'he endured,' 'he overthrew,' 'he gave' — establishing the pattern of divine initiative that the resurrection continues and completes.
13.18: Etropophoresin autous ('he endured/bore with/cared for them'): a textual variant between etropophoresin (he endured/put up with them, from Num 14:34's patience under provocation) and etrophoesen (he nourished/cared for them, from Deut 1:31 LXX: 'the LORD your God bore you as a father carries his son'). The latter has strong manuscript support and gives a more tender image of God as nursing parent carrying a child through the wilderness. Both readings are theologically rich; if the original is etrophoesen, the forty wilderness years are an act of divine parenthood rather than divine patience.
13.19: Kathēlōsen hepta ethnē ('he defeated/destroyed seven nations'): the seven Canaanite nations of Deut 7:1 — Hittites, Girgashites, Amorites, Canaanites, Perizzites, Hivites, Jebusites. Their displacement through Joshua 6-12 is compressed into one clause. The inheritance language (kataklēronomēsen, 'gave as inheritance') continues the Deuteronomic vocabulary of land-as-gift: Canaan was always gift, not conquest by right, requiring continued faithfulness from the recipients. The inclusion of the conquest in Paul's historical survey is unusual for Paul's letters but appropriate for a synagogue audience whose entire identity was rooted in the land-promise.
13.20: Hōs etesi tetrakosioisi kai pentēkonta ('about 450 years'): the chronological calculation is debated. The 450 years may count: the sojourn (400 years, Gen 15:13) + wilderness (40 years) + Joshua's conquest (10 years) = 450; or it follows a different calculation using 1 Kgs 6:1. The 'about' (hōs) acknowledges approximation; Luke follows a standard Jewish chronological tradition. The Judges period and Samuel mark the transition to monarchy — the narrative pivot toward David. Samuel functions as the last judge and the kingmaker whose anointing of David gives theological legitimacy to the Davidic line.
13.21: Ētēsanto basilea kai edōken autois ho theos ton Saoul ('they asked for a king and God gave them Saul'): the emphasis is on Israel's initiative — they 'asked' for a king (1 Sam 8:5-6); God accommodated their request while warning of its consequences (1 Sam 8:10-18). The 40-year reign is not stated in the MT; Acts 13:21 and Josephus (Ant. 6.14.9) give 40 years, following a traditional round number. The brevity of the Saul section — one verse — mirrors the prophetic tradition that viewed Saul's reign as a failed detour; the emphasis falls entirely on God's removal of Saul and elevation of David.
13.22: Kata tēn kardian mou ('according to my heart'): the composite citation blends 1 Sam 13:14 ('a man after his own heart') and Ps 89:20 LXX ('I have found David'). Neither OT passage uses the exact wording of Acts 13:22; the blending reflects synagogue practice of thematic citation (gezerah shavah — combining texts with shared vocabulary or theme). David as 'man after God's own heart' is the theological baseline: if David embodied God's purposes, his descendant who fulfills David's hopes represents the culmination of that divine-human alignment.
13.23: Apo tou spermatos touton ēgagen ho theos tō Israēl sōtēra Iēsoun ('from this man's descendants God has brought to Israel the Saviour Jesus'): the title sōtēr (Saviour) is applied to Jesus only here in Acts (though sōtēria/salvation recurs). In the Hellenistic world, sōtēr was a title for deified rulers — Ptolemy I was 'Soter,' Augustus was acclaimed Soter throughout the Greek East. In a Roman colony with an imperial cult, Paul's use of the term is a counter-proclamation: the Saviour is not Caesar or his successors but Jesus of David's line, raised by the God of Israel.
13.24: Prokēryxantos Iōannou baptisma metanoias ('John preached repentance and baptism'): John the Baptist's role as precursor is explained to an audience that may not know his story. The phrase pro prosōpou ('before the face') echoes Mal 3:1 LXX ('I will send my messenger before my face') — cited in Luke's Gospel (Luke 7:27) as the scriptural warrant for John's role. Even in a brief summary, Paul's speech invokes the prophetic frame that Luke's Gospel established for the Baptist, demonstrating that the same interpretive tradition governed both Peter's and Paul's preaching.
13.25: Trechontos de Iōannou ton dromon ('as John was completing his course'): the athletic metaphor dromos (race, course) for a life of ministry is Greek vocabulary common in Paul's letters (1 Cor 9:24-27; Phil 3:14; 2 Tim 4:7). John's self-distancing — 'I am not the one you think I am... after me comes one whose sandals I am not worthy to untie' — echoes all four Gospels' Baptist tradition. Untying sandals was the task of the lowest slave; John places himself below even that rank relative to Jesus. The consistent Baptist tradition across all the apostolic speeches confirms the stability of the core kerygmatic summary.
13.26: Hyioi genous Abraam kai hoi phoboumenoi ton theon ('sons of the family of Abraham and those who fear God'): the second direct address to the dual synagogue audience. 'This message of salvation has been sent' (exapestalē) — the passive marks divine sending, not merely apostolic initiative. Both ethnic Jews and God-fearers are equally addressed as recipients of the same salvation — a programmatic statement that the synagogue context makes bold. The 'to the Jew first' principle (v.46) does not mean exclusive priority; it means sequence, after which the Gentiles are equally included.
13.27: Touto agnoesan ('this they did not recognize/know'): the 'ignorance' motif recurs in apostolic preaching in Acts (3:17; 17:30) as an explanation for the crucifixion that is simultaneously explanatory and accusatory — the ignorance is culpable, not exculpatory. 'The words of the prophets that are read every Sabbath' — the irony: texts that predicted the Messiah's suffering were heard weekly but not recognized as fulfilled. The Deuteronomistic pattern of Israel rejecting God's messengers (Stephen's theme in ch7) is here applied specifically to the condemnation of Jesus by Jerusalem's leaders.
13.28: Mēdemian aitian thanatou heurontes ('having found no cause for a death sentence'): the innocence declaration is a consistent element of apostolic preaching (Peter in 2:23; 3:13-15). Paul uses aitia (legal cause, charge) — the same term in Jesus's trial (Luke 23:4, 14, 22, 'I find no basis for a charge against him'). The request to Pilate places the death within Roman legal framework while attributing moral causation to the Jerusalem leadership — the combination that Paul's audience, familiar with Roman legal procedure, would understand immediately.
13.29: Kathelontes apo tou xylou ('taking him down from the tree'): the cross is consistently identified as a 'tree' (xylon) in the earliest Petrine/Pauline tradition — Acts 5:30; 10:39; 13:29; Gal 3:13. The vocabulary invokes Deut 21:22-23 ('anyone hung on a tree is under God's curse') and its Pauline development in Gal 3:13 ('Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us'). The tree/cross identification was not incidental but the deliberate theological framing of the crucifixion as the bearer of covenant curse — the death that absorbs judgment on behalf of the covenant people.
13.30: Ho de theos ēgeiren auton ek nekrōn ('but God raised him from the dead'): the adversative de carries the gospel's full weight. The entire speech has been building to this reversal: the same divine initiative that led Israel out of Egypt, overthrew seven nations, raised up David, and endured forty years of wilderness rebellion — that God, with the same pattern of overturning human action, raised Jesus. 'God raised him' (ēgeiren) in exactly this formulation is the earliest Pauline credal statement (1 Thess 1:10; 1 Cor 15:4; Rom 10:9).
13.31: Hōphthē epi hēmeras pleious tois synanabasi autō apo tēs Galilaias ('he was seen for many days by those who had traveled with him from Galilee'): Paul distinguishes his testimony from theirs — they are eyewitnesses of the resurrection appearances (martyres autou); Paul's encounter came later (1 Cor 15:8, 'last of all, as to one untimely born'). By naming the Galilean disciples as 'now his witnesses to our people' (present tense), Paul grounds the resurrection claim in identifiable living witnesses — not ancient tradition but current testimony accessible to investigation.
13.32: Kai hēmeis hymas euangelizometha ('we proclaim to you the good news'): euangelizō (proclaim good news) is Paul's core missionary verb. Its object is 'what God promised our ancestors' — the content of the good news is the fulfillment of Israel's ancient promises. Paul positions himself not as the innovator of a new religion but as the fulfiller of Israel's hope. The continuity with Israel's Scripture — the entire survey of vv.17-31 — grounds the proclamation in the authoritative narrative the synagogue audience already accepts as true.
13.33: Psalm 2:7: 'You are my son; today I have become your father' — cited as fulfilled in the resurrection, not the baptism or transfiguration. The 'today' of the divine begetting is the resurrection day; Paul follows the same interpretation as Heb 1:5 and 5:5. Psalm 2 was a royal enthronement psalm in its original context — the king's formal adoption as God's 'son' at coronation. Applied to the resurrection, it becomes the enthronement of the risen king: the resurrection is Jesus's coronation as the Davidic king who fulfills Psalm 2's vision of universal dominion.
13.34: Isa 55:3 LXX: 'I will give you the holy and sure blessings of David' — the ta hosia Dauid ta pista ('holy and sure blessings') promised to David. The Isaianic promise extends the Davidic covenant universally ('come to me, all you who are thirsty'); the covenant mercies of David are not limited to ethnic Israel. The resurrection fulfills these covenant promises: the one who rose did not see decay, enabling the 'sure' (unbreakable) covenant blessings to flow through him. The connection between resurrection and Davidic covenant is Paul's distinctive contribution to the chain of OT citations.
13.35: Psalm 16:10 LXX: 'You will not let your holy one see decay' — already cited by Peter at Pentecost (Acts 2:27-28) with the identical application. The shared exegetical tradition between Peter and Paul confirms a common apostolic interpretive practice: David died and decayed; therefore the Psalm's 'holy one' (hosion) must refer to a greater David who would not. The same psalm, the same argument, two different apostles in two different cities — demonstrating that the Psalm 16 resurrection argument was not Paul's innovation but a shared early Christian tradition.
13.36: Hyperētēsas tē tou theou boulē ekoēmēthē ('having served God's purpose in his own generation, he fell asleep'): 'in his own generation' (tē idiai genea) is the interpretive key — David fulfilled his appointed purpose within the limits of his era. He served his generation, then died. Ekoēmēthē ('fell asleep'): the euphemism for death common in Judaism and Christianity, used throughout Paul's letters (1 Cor 7:39; 11:30; 15:6, 18, 20, 51; 1 Thess 4:13-15). The burial and decay of David's body is a historical fact that removes David from consideration as the Psalm's ultimate referent.
13.37: Hon de ho theos ēgeiren ouk eiden diaphthoran ('but the one God raised did not see decay'): the logical conclusion of the Psalm 16 argument. The resurrection happened before decomposition — 'the third day' tradition (1 Cor 15:4) specifies that the resurrection preceded decay. Paul's argument depends on the resurrection being a bodily event: a merely spiritual resurrection would not address the 'seeing decay' point. The physical resurrection of the body is not incidental to the argument but the specific claim on which the entire Psalm 16 application depends.
13.38: Aphesis hamartiōn katangelletai hymin ('forgiveness of sins is proclaimed to you'): aphesis (forgiveness/release) carries the Jubilee connotation — release from debt, obligation, accumulated covenant failure. This is what the Day of Atonement rituals pointed toward and could not permanently achieve (Heb 10:1-4). Paul's sermon offers what the entire sacrificial system aimed at but could not deliver: actual and final forgiveness grounded in the resurrection of the sin-bearer. The proclamation is performative — 'is proclaimed' (katangelletai) in the present tense: the announcement itself makes the forgiveness available.
13.39: Apo pantōn hōn ouk ēdynēthēte en nomō Mōyseōs dikaiōthēnai, en toutō pas ho pisteuōn dikaioutai ('from everything the law of Moses could not justify you from, in this one everyone who believes is justified'): the most theologically concentrated statement in Acts. The law of Moses could not justify — not because it was wrong but because it was not designed to justify from the record of accumulated transgression (cf. Rom 8:3, 'what the law was powerless to do'). 'Justified' (dikaioutai) is Paul's legal term for being declared righteous before God's judgment — the status that grounds assurance. The universal scope ('everyone,' pas) removes the ethnic boundary: the same justification for Jew and God-fearer, through faith.
13.40: Blepete oun mē epelthē to eirēmenon en tois prophētais ('take care that what is said in the prophets does not happen to you'): the prophets predicted both grace and judgment; the warning function belongs as much to the prophetic office as the consolation. The transition from proclamation (vv.32-39) to warning (vv.40-41) follows the prophetic pattern. Rejection of the gospel does not leave hearers where they were before hearing it; having heard places them under the prophetic warning about those who refused God's final offer.
13.41: Hab 1:5 LXX: 'Look, you scoffers, wonder and perish! For I am going to do something in your days that you would never believe, even if someone told you.' In its original context Habakkuk 1:5 announces the Babylonian invasion as unbelievable divine action. Applied here, the resurrection is the 'work' in 'your days' that surpasses belief. The same text used at Qumran (1QpHab) to describe the Teacher of Righteousness's message being rejected by the wicked priest. Paul's citation closes the speech with an ominous alternative: believe something extraordinary, or become the scoffers the prophet warned about.
13.42: Parakaleisthai ta rēmata tauta eis to metaxy sabbaton ('to have these words spoken to them the following Sabbath'): the congregation's invitation signals genuine interest — the first response to Paul's synagogue sermon is positive. The Lukan pattern in synagogue preaching is consistent: initial interest from the mixed audience, second-visit explosion of interest (v.44), then a split response — some believing, some opposing (vv.45-46). The pattern models the 'to the Jew first' mission strategy and its consistent outcome of division.
13.43: Polloi tōn Ioudaiōn kai tōn sebomenōn prosēlytōn ('many of the Jews and devout proselytes'): sebomenoi prosēlytoi — full converts to Judaism who had undergone circumcision, distinct from the uncircumcised God-fearers. The response cuts across the Jewish community's internal divisions: full-born Jews, proselytes, and apparently God-fearers (v.26) all respond positively. The instruction — 'continue in the grace of God' (prosmeneō tē chariti tou theou) — is Paul's pastoral follow-up: the conversion is a beginning requiring sustained commitment, not a single decision.
13.44: Schedon pasa hē polis synēchthē ('almost the whole city gathered'): the spread from the synagogue to the city at large within one week mirrors Pentecost — a contained gathering expanding to the entire population. 'Almost the whole city' is hyperbolic but conveys genuine social disruption: when a city's population gathers to hear a message, it is a civic event threatening established social order. The explosion of interest is the context for the Jewish leadership's jealousy (v.45) — the scale of the response threatens their social position as interpreters of Israel's Scriptures.
13.45: Zēlou eplēsthēsan hoi Ioudaioi ('the Jewish leaders were filled with jealousy'): zēlos is the same word used for the Sanhedrin's motivation in 5:17 — 'filled with jealousy' at the apostles' success. The social explanation is clear: Paul and Barnabas, visiting outsiders, have drawn a larger crowd in one week than the local synagogue leaders might see in a year. Blasphēmountes ('blaspheming/slandering'): publicly discrediting Paul and Barnabas — possibly using scriptural arguments against their claims about Jesus, or social slander, or both. The jealousy-response pattern will repeat at Corinth (18:12) and Ephesus (19:8-9).
13.46: Hymīn ēn anagkaion prōton lalēthēnai ton logon tou theou ('it was necessary to speak the word of God to you first'): divine necessity (anagkaion) — not mere strategy but divine ordering. The pivot — 'since you reject it and do not consider yourselves worthy of eternal life, we now turn to the Gentiles' — is not a rejection of Israel but a response to self-exclusion. Those who reject the message in effect pronounce their own unworthiness; the apostles accept that verdict and redirect. This pattern will repeat at Corinth (18:6) and Rome (28:25-28), each time reinforcing that the Gentile mission was initiated by Jewish refusal, not apostolic preference.
13.47: Isa 49:6: 'I have made you a light for the Gentiles, that you may bring salvation to the ends of the earth.' The Servant Song's commission — originally addressed to Israel's Servant or the prophet — is here applied directly to Paul and Barnabas. Luke 2:32 had already applied this text to Jesus ('a light for Gentile revelation'); applying it to his apostles shows how the mission of Christ continues in those he sends. As apostles commissioned by the risen Christ, Paul and Barnabas share in the Servant's mission — not replacing Jesus in the text but extending his mission to the nations.
13.48: Tetagmenoi eis zōēn aiōnion ('appointed/enrolled for eternal life'): tassō in its passive perfect participle — 'having been enrolled/appointed' (prior and completed action). The perfect tense indicates these Gentiles had already been enrolled in the divine register before they heard and believed. Tassō in the Hellenistic world was administrative vocabulary for civic enrollment — city rolls, military lists, tax registers. Those whose names were on the register were full members of the community; their membership preceded any individual act. The statement is theological predestination expressed in the idiom of imperial administration: God's citizenship roll is prior to human response.
