20.35: 'The words of the Lord Jesus how he said, It is more blessed to give than to receive' — this is the only explicit quotation of a saying of Jesus not found in the canonical Gospels. Such agrapha (unwritten sayings) were known in early Christianity; Paul's citation suggests a tradition of Jesus-sayings circulating independently of the written Gospels. The saying encapsulates the entire financial ethics of vv. 33–35 and grounds it in dominical authority.
20.36: Paul kneels and prays with the elders — kneeling prayer (theis ta gonata autou) was reserved for the most solemn supplications in Jewish and early Christian practice (cf. Dan 6:10; Eph 3:14; Luke 22:41). The corporate prayer seals the pastoral testament and anticipates the farewell lament that follows. The gesture communicates the gravity of the moment — this is not a routine pastoral visit but a final commissioning.
20.37: 'They all wept aloud and embraced Paul and kissed him' (klauthmos ... epipesontes ... katephiloun) — the emotional intensity of the Miletus farewell is unparalleled in Acts. The verb kataphileō (kissed repeatedly, affectionately) and the falling on Paul's neck echo Genesis 45:14–15 (Joseph's reunion with Benjamin and his brothers) and Luke 15:20 (the father's welcome of the prodigal). The communal grief marks the depth of the pastoral relationship Paul had built over three years.
20.38: 'Grieving especially because of the word he had spoken, that they would not see his face again' — the specific cause of grief is Paul's farewell prophecy (v. 25). The departure by ship — they 'accompanied him to the ship' — marks the physical severance. Luke's attention to this farewell scene, the most emotionally detailed in Acts, signals its theological importance: the apostolic age is closing, and the community must now sustain itself on the inherited word and appointed leadership.

25.1: Porcius Festus succeeded Felix as governor of Judea ca. 59-60 CE (the precise date is disputed; Josephus Ant. 20.182-188 records the transition but does not give a year). Festus is presented as more competent and less corrupt than Felix — he moves immediately to Jerusalem within three days to establish relationships with the local leadership, a standard gubernatorial courtesy visit. The Jerusalem leaders' immediate complaint against Paul shows the case had festered for two years during Felix's procrastination. Festus inherits an unresolved political liability.
25.2: The chief priests and Jewish leaders (hoi archiereis kai hoi prōtoi tōn Ioudaiōn) bring formal charges (enephanisan autō, 'laid before him') against Paul. This is the highest Jewish authority — the Sanhedric leadership — deploying diplomatic channels to pressure a new governor before he has formed his own opinions. The approach during a courtesy visit (before Paul has access to the new governor) is politically calculated: shape Festus's perception of Paul as a troublemaker before any defense is possible.
25.3: The Jewish leaders' request to have Paul brought to Jerusalem (aiteisthai charin kat' autou hopōs metapempsētai auton eis Hierosolyma) is framed as a favor (charin) — a patron-client request. Their actual intent — an ambush (enedran poiountes anelein auton, making a plot to kill him) — echoes the original conspiracy of 23:12-15 (forty men bound by oath not to eat until they killed Paul). The two-year gap has not dissolved the assassination plan; the leadership simply waits for a new governor who might be more accommodating.
25.4: Festus's response — 'Paul is being kept at Caesarea, and I myself will go there shortly' — is both logistically practical and politically shrewd. He has not yet been briefed on the case; agreeing to relocate Paul to Jerusalem would be premature capitulation to local pressure on day three of his tenure. The refusal to move Paul also implicitly acknowledges that Caesarea (Roman administrative capital) is the proper venue for Roman judicial proceedings, not Jerusalem (Jewish religious capital).
25.5: 'Let those of you who are in authority go down with me and accuse him there' — Festus invites the Jerusalem leadership to use the proper Roman judicial procedure: a formal accusation before the governor's tribunal. This is the accusatio system of Roman law: charges must be formally laid before a magistrate by a qualified accuser who faces calumnia (false accusation charge) if the prosecution fails. By insisting on this procedure, Festus protects himself from seeming to simply hand over a Roman prisoner to a foreign jurisdiction.
25.6: After eight or ten days, Festus goes down to Caesarea: the compressed timeline — Jerusalem courtesy visit, then immediate return to the administrative capital — shows that Paul's case was a priority inheritance from day one. The tribunal (bēma) setting in Caesarea is the same juridical context as the Gallio hearing (18:12-17) — the governor's official judgment seat, a formal Roman court with all its procedural requirements. The next day urgency shows Festus is not stalling; he moves immediately on the case.
25.7: The accusers stood around Paul and brought many serious charges (polla kai barea aitioumata katapherontes) that they could not prove (ha ouk ischyon apodeixai): the failure of proof echoes every previous hearing — Felix found no actionable charge (24:22-27), Lysias reported to Felix that Paul had been accused 'about questions of their law, but charged with nothing deserving death or imprisonment' (23:29). The pattern is deliberate Lukan apologetics: Roman legal process repeatedly fails to find criminal conduct, even when the highest Jewish leadership prosecutes with maximum effort.
25.8: Paul's threefold defense formula — 'neither against the law of the Jews, nor against the temple, nor against Caesar have I committed any offense' — covers all three possible charge categories: Jewish religious law, temple desecration (the original arrest charge, 21:28), and Roman law (lēsa maiestas, treason). The three-part formula corresponds to the charges that would need to be proven in a Roman court for conviction. Each category has already been addressed: the theological disputes are not justiciable (Gallio ruling), the temple desecration was never proven (Lysias's investigation), and there is no Caesar-crime.
25.9: Festus, wishing to do the Jews a favor (thelōn tois Ioudaiois charin katatithesthai), asks Paul if he would go to Jerusalem to be tried: the patronage vocabulary — 'to put a favor into deposit' with the Jewish leadership — reveals the political calculus. Festus needs working relationships with the Sanhedric leadership to govern effectively; conceding one trial venue is a small political cost for substantial goodwill. He asks Paul's consent, however — he cannot simply order a Roman citizen to a non-Roman court, which preserves the legal formality.
25.10: 'I am standing before Caesar's tribunal (epi tou bēmatos Kaisaros estōs eimi) where I ought to be tried': Paul's response deploys the juridical geography of Roman citizenship. The bēma Kaisaros is not merely the physical bench in Caesarea — it represents the emperor's judicial authority delegated to the governor, from which a Roman citizen cannot be removed to another jurisdiction without consent. Paul's 'ought' (dei) is a legal claim: this is the proper venue by law, not merely by preference.
25.11: 'I appeal to Caesar' (Kaisara epikaloumai): the provocatio ad Caesarem (appeal to the emperor) was a right available to Roman citizens facing capital charges. Its scope and procedure in the first century are debated — Sherwin-White's Roman Society and Roman Law in the New Testament (1963) remains the standard analysis. The appeal suspended the provincial governor's jurisdiction and required transfer to Rome. For Paul, it transforms a politically messy local trial into a imperial-level hearing, precisely the 'testimony before governors and kings' and 'in Rome' that the divine vision promised (23:11; 27:24).
25.12: Festus conferred with his council (meta tou symbouliou): the governor's advisory council (consilium) consisted of senior staff — military tribunes, senior provincial officials, senior Romans traveling in the province. It was standard procedure to consult the council before making significant legal decisions. The council's apparent agreement that the appeal is valid confirms the legal correctness of Paul's invocation. Festus's declaration — 'you have appealed to Caesar; to Caesar you shall go' — is a formal juridical ruling, not an expression of frustration.
25.13: King Agrippa and Bernice arrived at Caesarea to welcome Festus: Herod Agrippa II (ca. 27-100 CE) was the last surviving Herodian ruler. He held jurisdiction over parts of northern Palestine and Lebanon (Chalcis, then territories of Philip and Lysanias) and had the right to appoint the Jerusalem High Priest — making him deeply enmeshed in the Jewish establishment despite being Roman-client king. Bernice was his sister; their relationship was rumored to be incestuous (Juvenal, Satires 6.156-160; Josephus Ant. 20.145). She later became the mistress of Titus before his becoming emperor.
25.14: The many days of Agrippa's visit gave Festus occasion to present Paul's case: this is the political calculation Festus needed. Agrippa's deep knowledge of Jewish affairs (v. 26 — 'you are expert in all customs and controversies of the Jews') made him the ideal consultant for constructing a letter to accompany Paul to Caesar. Roman governors sending prisoners to Rome with appeals were required to provide a written summary of the charges and proceedings (litterae dimissoriae); the problem was that Festus had 'nothing certain to write' (v. 26) without understanding the case.
25.15: The Jewish leaders in Jerusalem asked for condemnation (katadikēn): the request is not for retrial but for a predetermined verdict — executive rather than judicial action. This echoes Pilate's encounter with the crowds demanding crucifixion; the pattern repeats in every Jewish-Roman encounter over Paul. The leaders are seeking a political favor (removal of Paul), not genuine justice. Their failure to get it from Festus repeats the Gallio precedent: Roman governors refuse to pre-judge cases and provide the verdicts local political pressure demands.
25.16: Roman judicial principle: 'It is not the custom of the Romans to hand over anyone before the accused has met his accusers face to face (kata prosōpon echein ton katēgoroumenon tous katēgorous) and has been given an opportunity to make a defense': Festus articulates the audiatur et altera pars principle — hear both sides. This was codified in Roman law; the Digesta (48.17.1) preserves it: no one should be condemned unheard. Festus's appeal to Roman procedural norms serves his own political defense: he followed proper procedure, he did not simply capitulate to political pressure.
25.17: So when they came here, I made no delay — the next day I sat on the tribunal and ordered the man to be brought in (ekeleusa achthēnai ton andra): Festus's compressed narrative to Agrippa emphasizes his procedural correctness and haste. He is constructing his own record for the king who will need to help him write the letter to Rome. The narrative is self-exculpatory: I followed proper procedure, I acted immediately, I gave all parties a fair hearing. This is how Roman governors reported to their superiors.
25.18: When the accusers stood up, they brought no charge in such cases as I supposed (oias egō hypenoon ponērias): Festus expected charges matching the severity of 'many serious charges' (v. 7) — presumably crimes against persons, property, or the state. Instead he encountered theological dispute. His bewilderment is genuine administrative incomprehension: what category of Roman law applies to disagreements about whether a dead man is alive? The phrasing 'no charge in such cases as I supposed' is honest bureaucratic admission.
25.19: They had certain points of dispute about their own religion (deisidaimonia) and about a certain Jesus who was dead (tethnēkotos), but whom Paul asserted to be alive (zēn): Festus uses deisidaimonia — the neutral Roman administrative term for 'religious observance' (it carries no pejorative sense here; cf. Paul's use in 17:22). The description 'a certain Jesus (tinos Iēsou) who was dead but whom Paul asserted to be alive' is the minimal Roman summary of the resurrection claim — entirely accurate as administrative description, entirely inadequate as theological evaluation. The contrast dead/alive (tethnēkōs/zēn) isolates the factual claim at the center of all the controversy.
25.20: Being at a loss how to investigate these matters (aporōn de egō tēn peri toutōn zētēsin): Festus's admission of administrative confusion — 'at a loss' (aporōn) — is unprecedented candor from a Roman governor. Roman law had procedures for crimes, fraud, and civil disputes, but no mechanism for adjudicating whether a resurrection had occurred. He had no relevant legal category. His offer to send Paul to Jerusalem was an attempt to delegate the theological question to the appropriate religious authority — a reasonable administrative instinct, though one that underestimated Paul's danger there.
25.21: Paul appealed for his case to be kept for the Emperor's decision (eis tēn tou Sebastou diagnōsin): diagnōsis is the technical term for imperial judicial review. Sebastos (Latin: Augustus) is the standard Greek title for the emperor; in 61-62 CE (approximate date of this appeal) the emperor was Nero. Paul's appeal transfers jurisdiction from Festus's provincial court to the imperial court in Rome. The emperor hearing the case would be Nero himself or his judicial delegates. This is the mechanism by which Paul reaches Rome, fulfilling 19:21 and 23:11.
25.22: I also want to hear the man myself (eboulomēn kai autos tou anthrōpou akousai): Agrippa's expressed desire to hear Paul directly is diplomatically framed as personal curiosity, but it serves the practical need for Festus's letter. Agrippa's Jewish expertise (he had been raised in Rome but was deeply involved in Jewish affairs — appointing high priests, possessing priestly vestments) made him uniquely qualified to translate Paul's case into terms Nero's court could understand. The meeting is arranged for the following day — the urgency reinforces that this is administrative work, not entertainment.
25.23: With great pomp (meta pollēs phantasias) Agrippa and Bernice came into the audience hall (akroatērion) with tribunes and prominent men of the city: phantasia (display, spectacle) is the vocabulary of Hellenistic royal ceremonial. The audience hall was a formal reception space in the praetorium (governor's palace); the assembled tribunes (five cohort commanders, representing substantial military force) and 'prominent men of the city' (andrōn tous kat' exochēn) made this a public hearing with maximum civic prestige. Luke's scene-setting creates the contrast with Paul's single chained figure before this assembled power.
25.24: Festus's formal introduction of the case: 'All the multitude of the Jews (pan to plēthos tōn Ioudaiōn) petitioned me, both in Jerusalem and here' — the scope claim is characteristic of advocates who want to convey popular pressure. Festus's introduction is the diplomatic framing required before Paul speaks: he is not being tried again (the appeal has transferred jurisdiction), but presenting his case to Agrippa for the letter's content. The petitioners 'shouting that he ought not to live any longer' echoes the Jerusalem mob of 22:22 — the visceral rejection that initiated the Roman protective custody.
25.25: Festus declares he found Paul had done nothing deserving death (mēden axion autōn thanatou peprakhenai): this is the fourth Roman verdict of innocence in Paul's case — Claudius Lysias (23:29), Felix (implicitly in 24:22-27), Festus here, and Agrippa (26:31-32). Luke's fourfold structure mirrors the fourfold innocence declarations about Jesus in Luke's passion narrative (Pilate three times, Herod once). The parallel is exact: the innocent accused of both Jesus and Paul is declared innocent by every Roman and Herodian judge who examines the case.
25.26: I have nothing certain (asphalon) to write about him to my lord (tō kyriō): the use of kyrios (lord) for the emperor is the standard honorific in official correspondence — Nero's court expected this form of address. Festus's candid admission that he has 'nothing certain to write' is the impetus for the entire Agrippa hearing: he needs content for the required litterae dimissoriae that must accompany Paul to Rome. The document must identify the charges to which the accused is responding; Festus cannot write 'religious dispute about a resurrection' without appearing to have wasted imperial time.
25.27: 'It seems unreasonable to me to send a prisoner without indicating the charges against him' (asēmous, without signs/markings): the administrative logic is precise. A prisoner sent to Rome with an appeal must be accompanied by documentation that gives the imperial court something to adjudicate. Without charge documentation, the imperial judges cannot conduct a diagnōsis (investigation). Festus is stuck between two impossibilities: he cannot charge Paul with a crime he did not commit, but he cannot send Paul to Rome without charges. The Agrippa consultation is his way out of this administrative paradox.

26.1: Agrippa said to Paul, 'You have permission to speak for yourself' (epitrepetai soi peri seautou legein): the formal grant of permission is the ritual opening of a hearing. Apologeisthai (to speak in defense) is the same verb Paul uses throughout — this is not an informal conversation but a formal apologia, the genre of self-defense speech well established in Hellenistic rhetoric (cf. Plato's Apology of Socrates). Paul stretches out his hand (ekteinas tēn cheira) — the standard orator's opening gesture, signaling the beginning of a prepared speech, not an impromptu defense.
26.2: 'I consider myself fortunate that it is before you, King Agrippa, I am going to make my defense today' (hēgēmai emauton makarion epi sou mellōn apologeisthai): the exordium (opening of a rhetorical speech) required a captatio benevolentiae — a gesture that wins the audience's goodwill. Paul's expression of fortune before Agrippa is formally correct: a flattering but not sycophantic opening that simultaneously identifies Paul's rhetorical competence (he knows the form) and establishes the specific ground for the compliment — Agrippa's Jewish expertise, which is the one thing Festus lacks.
26.3: 'You are especially familiar with all the customs and controversies of the Jews' (gnōstēn onta se pantōn tōn kata Ioudaious ethōn te kai zētēmatōn): Agrippa II's qualification as Paul's judge is his Jewish knowledge. He had been raised partly in Rome but maintained deep involvement in Jewish religious administration — he was given custody of the High Priest's vestments by Claudius, appointed high priests, and corresponded with Josephus about the accuracy of Jewish history (Life 364-366; Ant. 20.216). He was arguably the most knowledgeable person available on earth to evaluate Paul's claims about the Hebrew prophets.
26.4: 'My manner of life from my youth, spent from the beginning among my own nation and in Jerusalem, is known by all the Jews' (ginōskousin pantes hoi Ioudaioi): Paul's appeal to his publicly known biography is a legal tactic — he is calling on witnesses who could contradict him if false. His Jerusalem education (22:3 — under Gamaliel, in the city, trained in the law) was not private; he sat in the highest Pharisaic educational institution and was known to the Jerusalem religious establishment. The appeal to public knowledge preempts the charge that Paul is an obscure provincial with no credentials.
26.5: 'According to the strictest party (akribestaten hairesin) of our religion I have lived as a Pharisee' (Pharisaios): hairesis in first-century Jewish usage designated a recognizable school or party — the Pharisees, Sadducees, and Essenes are all called haireseis by Josephus (War 2.119-166; Ant. 18.11-22). Akribestaten (most precise/strict) is Paul's own assessment of Pharisaic thoroughness — they interpreted Torah more rigorously than Sadducees or the general population. Paul's Pharisaic identity is his primary credential: the most serious students of Scripture now stand before the king who knows Scripture.
26.6: 'Now I stand here on trial because of my hope in the promise made by God to our fathers' (ep' elpidi tēs eis tous pateras hēmōn epangelias genomenes hypo tou theou): the phrase 'promise to our fathers' is patriarchal covenant language — the Abrahamic promise (Gen 12:1-3; 15:5; 17:1-8), the Davidic promise (2 Sam 7:12-16), the prophetic restoration promises (Isa 40-66; Ezek 36-37; Jer 31). Paul is reframing his trial: this is not a case about a Roman legal infraction but about whether Israel's ancestral hope has been fulfilled. He claims to stand within the stream of Israel's own expectation, not outside it.
26.7: 'Our twelve tribes hope to attain as they earnestly worship night and day' (to dōdekaphylon hēmōn en ekteneia nykta kai hēmeran latreuon elpizei katantēsai): the 'twelve tribes' is the ideal of all-Israel — the northern ten tribes were scattered in 722 BCE and their restoration was an eschatological hope (Isa 11:11-12; Ezek 37:15-28; Sirach 36:10-11). Earnest night-and-day worship is the temple liturgy's continuous character — the daily burnt offerings, the prayer watches, the festival worship. Paul claims that what all of Israel's sincere worship aims toward — the resurrection of the dead and the fulfillment of the covenant — is what his hope concerns. The accusation that he stands against Israel is absurd: he shares its deepest longing.
26.8: 'Why is it thought incredible by any of you that God raises the dead?' (ti apiston krinetai par hymin ei ho theos nekrous egeirei): the rhetorical question targets the specific theological division within Judaism that made resurrection controversial. The Pharisees believed in bodily resurrection of the dead (Josephus War 2.163; Acts 23:8); the Sadducees denied it (Mark 12:18; Josephus Ant. 18.16). Agrippa presumably held the Pharisaic view. Paul's question positions the resurrection of Jesus as an instance of the general resurrection the Pharisees already affirmed; the issue is not whether God can raise the dead but whether he did so specifically in Jesus's case.
26.9: 'I myself was convinced that I ought to do many things in opposing the name of Jesus of Nazareth' (edoxen emautō pros to onoma Iēsou tou Nazōraiou dein polla enantia praxai): Paul's self-testimony of persecution is his most powerful credential for the reality of the conversion. The phrase 'I myself was convinced' (edoxen emautō) acknowledges the subjective certainty of his pre-conversion state — he did not merely go through the motions; he was genuinely committed to suppressing the Jesus movement. This pre-history makes the subsequent transformation inexplicable apart from the event that caused it.
26.10: 'I shut up many of the saints in prison and when they were put to death I cast my vote against them' (psēphon katēnenka): psēphon katēnenka (cast my vote/pebble) may indicate Sanhedric membership or authority to participate in judicial proceedings against Christians. The detail that Paul 'cast his vote' against those executed suggests participation in formal legal proceedings — consistent with the authority received from the chief priests (vv. 10, 12). His role was not mere bystander (contrast 7:58 — consenting to Stephen's stoning) but active participant in capital proceedings.
26.11: 'I punished them often in all the synagogues and tried to make them blaspheme (ēnagkazon blasphēmein); and in raging fury against them I persecuted them even to foreign cities': synagogue punishment is the malkot (flogging, 39 stripes), administered for religious infractions under the authority of the synagogue's archisynagōgos and council. Forcing converts to blaspheme — presumably to recant Jesus as Lord — echoes the Roman procedure for identifying Christians (Pliny Ep. 10.96: those who invoked the gods and cursed Christ were to be dismissed). Paul had anticipated what the Romans would later formalize.
26.12: The Damascus journey with authorization from the chief priests: the chief priests' authority to dispatch representatives to foreign synagogues was a recognized prerogative, though its precise legal basis is debated. Damascus had a substantial Jewish population (Josephus War 2.561 — 10,500 to 18,000 Jews killed there in 66 CE; the large community implies established synagogues). The authority from the chief priests gave Paul the standing to enter Damascus synagogues and conduct interrogations of suspected Jesus-followers.
26.13: 'At midday (hēmeras mesēs), O king, I saw on the way a light from heaven, brighter than the sun, shining around me and those who journeyed with me' (hyper tēn lamprotēta tou hēliou perilampsanta me phōs): the midday timing rules out misidentification of the sun; the light exceeds solar brightness. In the three accounts of Paul's conversion (9:3; 22:6; 26:13), only chapter 26 specifies the comparison with the noon sun — the superlative indicates a category different from natural phenomena. The light encompasses Paul and his companions; the encounter is not private hallucination but visible to witnesses (though the voice's full content was heard only by Paul).
26.14: 'I heard a voice saying to me in the Hebrew language (tē Hebraidi dialektō), Saul, Saul, why are you persecuting me? It is hard for you to kick against the goads (sklēron soi pros kentra laktizein)': the detail that the voice spoke Hebrew (or Aramaic — Hebraidi could indicate either) is added only in the chapter 26 account, addressing an audience familiar with Jewish tradition. 'Kick against the goads' is a Greek proverb attested in Aeschylus (Agamemnon 1624; Prometheus Bound 323) and Pindar, meaning to resist the inevitable with self-injury. Using a Greek proverb in a Hebrew speech may reflect Luke's literary shaping, or the risen Christ's willingness to meet Paul in his dual cultural context.
26.15: 'Who are you, Lord? And the Lord said, I am Jesus whom you are persecuting' (egō eimi Iēsous hon sy diōkeis): the self-identification formula echoes LXX divine self-disclosure — 'I am YHWH' (Exod 3:14 LXX: egō eimi ho ōn). The identification 'whom you are persecuting' draws the equivalence that drives Paul's subsequent theology: persecuting the church = persecuting Jesus (the body-head unity of 1 Cor 12:12-27 and Eph 1:22-23). This identification is not explanatory gloss but the core revelation: the post-resurrection Jesus remains personally identified with his people.
26.16: 'Rise and stand upon your feet (anastēthi kai stēthi epi tous podas sou); for I have appeared to you for this purpose, to appoint you as a servant and witness': the commissioning language is shaped by the OT prophet-commission pattern (Jer 1:7-8; Isa 6:6-9; Ezek 2:1-3). Hypēretēn kai martyra (servant and witness) is the Lukan summary of Paul's apostolic calling — terms that appear together in Luke 1:2 (eyewitnesses and servants of the word). The appointment is specifically to be a witness 'both of the things you have seen and of things in which I will appear to you' — covering both the Damascus road encounter and subsequent revelations (cf. 2 Cor 12:1-4; Gal 1:12).
26.17: 'Delivering you from your people and from the Gentiles — to whom I am sending you' (apostellōn se): the divine deliverance promise anticipates the repeated rescues of Acts — the Damascus escape (9:23-25), the Jerusalem rescue (9:29-30), the Philippian earthquake (16:26-34), the Corinthian protection (18:9-10), the Jerusalem riot (21:30-36), the Roman custody chain. Apostellōn (sending) is the verb behind apostolos — Paul's commission is literally an apostolic sending, with Jesus as the commissioning authority. The 'your people' and 'the Gentiles' frames the dual audience of Paul's entire missionary career.
26.18: 'To open their eyes (anoixai ophthalmous autōn), so that they may turn from darkness to light and from the power of Satan to God, that they may receive forgiveness of sins and a place among those who are sanctified by faith in me': the commission language condenses multiple OT strands: Isa 42:7 (opening eyes of the blind, release from darkness — the Servant Song); Isa 35:5 (the blind seeing); Isa 61:1 (proclaiming liberty). The cosmic dualism (darkness/light, Satan/God) is characteristic of Second Temple Jewish apocalyptic (1QM, 1QS — the War Scroll's Sons of Light vs. Sons of Darkness). Paul's commission is understood within the framework of eschatological conflict between cosmic powers.
26.19: 'Therefore, O King Agrippa, I was not disobedient to the heavenly vision' (tē ouranō optasią): Paul presents his subsequent life as simply obedience to a commission received. The rhetorical logic: if you accept that such a vision could occur (v. 8 — you believe God raises the dead), then Paul's subsequent behavior is explained without remainder. He is not a rebel against Israel, a deviant Pharisee, or a Roman-law violator; he is a man obedient to a divine commission. The self-presentation removes moral agency from the center and places divine commission there instead.
26.20: 'But declared first to those in Damascus, then in Jerusalem and throughout all the region of Judea, and also to the Gentiles, that they should repent and turn to God, performing deeds in keeping with their repentance (axios tēs metanoias erga prassontas)': the geographic sequence — Damascus, Jerusalem, Judea, then Gentiles — matches Acts' account of Paul's missionary career. The call to 'deeds worthy of repentance' echoes John the Baptist (Luke 3:8) and positions Paul's proclamation within the long tradition of ethical prophetic call. The Gentile inclusion in the repentance call is the point at which Jewish audiences regularly became hostile (22:21-22).
26.21: 'For this reason the Jews seized me in the temple and tried to kill me' (ekheirazeion en tō hierō kai epeirōnto diacheiridzesthai): diacheiridzesthai (to lay violent hands on, to kill) is strong — it is the word used for the killing of Moses (Acts 7:25 implies) and other violent deaths. The seizure in the temple closes the circle of Paul's testimony: his commission was to go to Gentiles (v. 17); the reaction of the temple establishment to that commission was violence. The persecution is evidence, not embarrassment: it is the prophetic pattern (7:52 — they killed those who announced the Righteous One).
26.22: 'To this day I have had the help that comes from God' (epikourias oun tychōn tēs apo tou theou), and so I stand here testifying both to small and great, saying nothing but what the prophets and Moses said would come to pass': epikourias (assistance, help) is a military term — the backup that keeps you in the fight. Paul's endurance across the gauntlet of Acts 21-26 is interpreted as divine sustaining. The limitation 'nothing beyond what Moses and the prophets said' is Paul's central apologetic claim: he has not invented a new religion but interpreted the ancient texts faithfully. The question is whether the interpretation is correct, not whether the texts exist.
26.23: 'That the Christ must suffer (pathētos ho Christos), and that, by being the first to rise from the dead, he would proclaim light both to our people and to the Gentiles': pathētos ho Christos (the suffering Christ / the Christ was subject to suffering) is the key proof-text formula Paul generates from his reading of 'Moses and the prophets'. The dei (must) of suffering combined with resurrection is the interpretive claim that unlocks the whole. Isa 53 (suffering servant), Ps 22 (the righteous sufferer), Isa 42:6 and 49:6 (light to the nations) — these are the scriptural pillars. Paul argues these texts were always pointing to a suffering-and-rising Messiah who brings light to Gentiles.
26.24: Festus interrupts: 'Paul, you are out of your mind! Your great learning (ta polla grammata) is driving you mad (eis manian peritrepei)!' — the charge of madness from great learning is a stock trope in Hellenistic intellectual culture: Socrates was accused of corrupting youth through too much philosophy; the Cynics were widely regarded as mad. Festus's category is the Roman administrator's: a man clearly educated (the speech has been sophisticated) who has lost the distinction between religion and reality. The accusation is culturally calibrated — it is the polite Roman way to dismiss claims that violate the categories of empirical governance.
26.25: 'I am not out of my mind, most excellent Festus (kratiste Phēste), but I am speaking true and rational words' (alētheias kai sōphrosynēs rhēmata apophthengomai): sōphrosynēs (soundness of mind, self-control, reasonableness) is the exact antithesis of mania — Festus's charge and Paul's defense are using the same philosophical vocabulary. Sōphrosynē was one of the cardinal virtues in Hellenistic ethics; Paul is claiming not merely sanity but philosophical virtue. The appeal to 'true' (alētheias) words alongside sōphrosynē frames his proclamation as both factually accurate and philosophically coherent.
26.26: 'The king knows about these things (epistamai gar autos toutōn ti ho basileus proslalon autō) and to him I speak freely (parrēsiazomenos), for I am persuaded that none of these things has escaped his notice, for this has not been done in a corner' (ou gar estin en gōnia pepragmenon touto): the appeal to Agrippa's knowledge is tactically brilliant. Paul shifts the rhetorical ground from defending himself to inviting Agrippa into his own knowledge-network. The phrase 'not done in a corner' asserts the public character of Jesus's ministry and the resurrection witness — it is not an esoteric private claim but events with public witnesses in Jerusalem (Jesus crucified under Pilate; the tomb; the appearances).
26.27: 'King Agrippa, do you believe the prophets? I know that you believe' (pisteueis... oida hoti pisteueis): the direct question to Agrippa is Paul's rhetorical turn — he moves from defendant to questioner, from addressing the bench to engaging Agrippa personally. The double assertion (do you believe? I know you do) puts Agrippa in an impossible position: to say 'no' is to alienate his Jewish constituency; to say 'yes' is to acknowledge the force of Paul's scriptural argument. The question is the culmination of the entire speech: if you accept the prophets, and the prophets described a suffering-rising-Gentile-illuminating Messiah, then the question of Jesus's identity cannot be dismissed as madness.
26.28: Agrippa's response: 'In a short time (en oligō) would you persuade me to be a Christian (Christianon poiēsai)?' — the precise meaning of en oligō is debated: 'in a little time/with little effort' vs. 'almost'. The RSV renders 'Almost you persuade me to be a Christian'; the ESV 'In a short time you would persuade me.' Either way, Agrippa deflects with humor or irony rather than engagement — a courtly deflection. The use of 'Christian' (Christianos) is the third occurrence in Acts (11:26 — Antioch; 26:28; cf. 1 Pet 4:16) — an external label, here used to describe what Paul is trying to make Agrippa become.
26.29: Paul's response: 'Whether short or long, I would to God that not only you but also all who hear me this day might become such as I am — except for these chains (ektos tōn desmōn toutōn)': the gesture toward his chains is the speech's most powerful moment — the chained prisoner wishing his accusers might share everything he has except the chains. The exemption of the chains from the wish is not self-pity but realism; the substance of what Paul wishes — the apostolic calling, the direct encounter with the risen Christ, the commission to both peoples — is what he commends. The rhetorical effect is to reverse the roles: the powerful are the impoverished; the prisoner is the one with everything worth having.
26.30: The king rose, and the governor and Bernice and those sitting with them (anestē te ho basileus kai ho hēgemōn hē te Bernikē kai hoi synkathēmenoi autois): the rising of the assembled dignitaries signals the formal end of the hearing. The departure in sequence — king, governor, then the rest — follows protocol. Luke's scene-construction gives maximum theater: the elaborate entrance of v. 23 (great pomp, tribunes, prominent men) is balanced by this formal exit. What happens in the corridor is the ironic verdict.
26.31: 'This man is doing nothing to deserve death or imprisonment' (ouden thanatou ē desmōn axion ti prassein ton anthrōpon touton): the verdict exchanged among the departing officials is the fifth Roman declaration of Paul's innocence in Acts (Lysias 23:29; Festus 25:25; now Agrippa). The pattern has been building across chapters 22-26; every competent authority who examines the case reaches the same conclusion. Luke's point is legal and theological simultaneously: the Roman empire, operating under its own procedural standards, repeatedly declares the gospel-bearer innocent.
26.32: 'This man could have been set free if he had not appealed to Caesar' (apeluhthai edynato ho anthrōpos houtos ei mē epekeklēto Kaisara): Agrippa's final assessment closes the Roman judicial sequence and pivots to the final movement of Acts. The appeal to Caesar has made Paul's Rome journey legally required — it cannot be revoked. What appears as a procedural constraint is, for Luke, the providential mechanism by which the divine plan (23:11 — 'you must testify also at Rome') is fulfilled. Paul appealed because he needed to get to Rome; Rome is where he needed to be. Roman law serves divine purpose.

22.1: Andres adelphoi kai pateres, akousate mou tēs pros hymas nyn apologias ('Brothers and fathers, hear my defense'): apologia (defense speech) carries both the everyday sense of self-defense and the philosophical sense — Plato's Apology presents Socrates's defense of his life before hostile judges. Luke's use of apologia in Paul's speeches (22:1; 25:16; 26:2) frames Paul as an apostolic Socrates: a man of conviction before accusers who cannot comprehend him. The address 'brothers and fathers' (adelphoi kai pateres) echoes Stephen's opening (7:2) — placing Paul in the same tradition of the prophet speaking to the covenant community he loves but has lost.
22.2: Tē Hebraidi dialektō ('in the Hebrew dialect/Aramaic'): first-century Palestinian 'Hebrew' virtually always means Aramaic — the spoken vernacular, not classical biblical Hebrew. The crowd's response — 'they became even quieter' (mallon pareschon hēsychian) — shows the strategic value of the language choice: Aramaic signals insider status, Jewish solidarity, and removes the suspicion that Paul has become culturally Hellenized. The same rhetorical move as Peter's 'fellow Israelites' at Pentecost (2:14) — establishing shared identity before making a challenging claim.
22.3: Anatetrammenos en tē polei tautē para tous podas Gamaliēl ('brought up in this city at the feet of Gamaliel'): Paul's three-stage identity — born in Tarsus (Diaspora), raised in Jerusalem (center), educated under Gamaliel (elite) — positions him as the most impeccably Jewish of Jews despite Diaspora birth. Gamaliel I was one of the great sages of the first century, grandson of Hillel — the most respected rabbinic lineage. 'At the feet of' (para tous podas) is the standard posture for a student seated before a teacher; it is the posture of Mary in Luke 10:39. Paul's rabbinic education under Gamaliel was the equivalent of elite legal training in first-century Jewish terms.
22.4: Hēn hodon ediōxa achri thanatou ('I persecuted this Way to the death'): 'the Way' (hē hodos) is the early Christian self-designation appearing seven times in Acts (9:2; 19:9, 23; 22:4; 24:14, 22; 26:4). The metaphor of hodos (road/way) carries OT resonance — the way of the LORD (Ps 25:4; Isa 40:3; Jer 6:16) — applied to the messianic community. Paul's confession 'to the death' (achri thanatou) does not minimize the consequences of his former actions; the candor is strategic — establishing the magnitude of his pre-conversion zeal makes the conversion itself more inexplicable by any human cause.
22.5: Ho archiereus kai pan to presbyterion ('the high priest and the whole council of elders'): Paul calls his former principals as character witnesses for his pre-conversion identity. The irony is deliberate: the very council now prosecuting him authorized his persecution — the same men who commissioned him to Damascus are now his accusers. The letters from Damascus also attest to the Jerusalem council's authority over diaspora Jewish communities, a jurisdictional reach that shows the scope of Paul's former assignment.
22.6: Peri mesēmbrian ('about noon'): the midday timing — when the sun is at maximum brilliance — makes the heavenly light's surpassing of natural sunlight all the more significant. Paul adds this detail in his Jerusalem speech that is absent from Luke's third-person account in ch.9. The noonday timing is significant in Hebrew Scripture: noon-hour divine appearances carry a sense of unmistakable clarity (cf. Amos 8:9; Isa 18:4). The specificity of the time detail is a realism marker suggesting firsthand report.
22.7: Saoul Saoul, ti me diōkeis ('Saul! Saul! Why do you persecute me?'): the double address — 'Saul, Saul' — is the Hebrew/Aramaic form of urgent personal directness, used in moments of divine confrontation: 'Abraham, Abraham!' (Gen 22:11), 'Moses, Moses!' (Exod 3:4), 'Samuel, Samuel!' (1 Sam 3:10). In each case the double address precedes a decisive divine commissioning. The question 'why do you persecute me?' identifies Christ with the persecuted community — to persecute the church is to persecute Christ himself. This is the most theologically loaded phrase in the Damascus road accounts.
22.8: Iēsous ho Nazōraios ('Jesus the Nazarene'): the geographic-historical designation preserves the particularity of the risen Christ's identity. 'Jesus of Nazareth' is the formula used consistently in Acts (2:22; 3:6; 4:10; 6:14; 22:8; 24:5; 26:9) — anchoring the risen Lord's identity to his historical humanity. The persecutor is confronted not by an abstract divine principle but by a specific, named, historically located person who has proven stronger than death and who identifies himself with the victims of Saul's violence.
22.9: Hoi de syn emoi ontes to men phōs etheasanto ('those with me saw the light'): the apparent tension with Acts 9:7 ('hearing the sound but seeing no one') is a perception-level distinction in the Greek vocabulary of hearing. Acts 9:7 says they heard the sound (phōnē = sound); Acts 22:9 says they did not hear the voice (phōnē = intelligible speech). Greek phōnē can mean both audible phenomenon and communicative speech; the companions experienced the former but not the latter. Paul alone received the address — a distinction between the auditory phenomenon (sound/light) and the communicative content (intelligible speech to Paul).
22.10: Anastas poreuthēti eis Damaskon ('rise and go to Damascus'): the divine direction is specific, immediate, and geographic — not a mystical illumination but an itinerary. The pattern follows the OT commissioning structure: encounter → prostration → divine address → specific destination → sending. Paul's question — 'What shall I do, Lord?' (ti poiēsō, Kyrie) — is the convert's first act of submitted discipleship. The willingness to receive direction ('what shall I do?') marks the radical reorientation of a formerly self-directed zealot.
22.11: Hōs de ouk eneblepon apo tēs doxēs tou phōtos ekeinou ('since I could not see because of the glory of that light'): Paul's blindness is explicitly attributed to divine glory (doxa) — theophanic language. The glory that overwhelmed Isaiah's vision (Isa 6:5) and prostrated Ezekiel (Ezek 1:28) here blinds the persecutor. Being led by the hand (cheiragōgoumenoi) is a posture of complete dependence — the man who had arrested and imprisoned others is now led as a helpless dependent. The reversal is total and physical before it is theological.
22.12: Ananias eulabes kata ton nomon, martyroumenos hypo pantōn tōn katoikountōn Ioudaiōn ('Ananias, devout according to the law, well-spoken of by all the Jews living there'): in Paul's Jerusalem speech Ananias's credentials are emphasized differently from Acts 9 (where he is simply 'a disciple'). Here he is Torah-observant and respected by the Jewish community — Jewish credentials for a Gentile audience. The commission Paul received came through a scrupulously observant Jewish mediator, not a Hellenized apostate. The emphasis refutes any claim that Paul's mission originated in apostasy from Israel.
22.13: Ho adelphos Saoul, anablepson ('Brother Saul, receive your sight'): Ananias addresses Paul as 'brother' — acknowledging him as a member of the Jewish community before addressing him as a disciple. The restoration of sight enacts Isa 42:7 ('to open eyes that are blind') — the Servant's mission applied to Paul himself before he will carry it to the Gentiles. The physical healing is the first sign of the new creation that has occurred; the restored eyes will see differently, as Luke's narrative will demonstrate.
22.14: Ho dikaios ('the Righteous One'): Ananias's designation of Jesus is one of the earliest Christological titles in Acts, appearing in Peter's Pentecost speech (3:14, 'the Holy and Righteous One') and Stephen's speech (7:52). The title draws from Isa 53:11 ('the righteous one, my servant, will justify many') and the Book of Enoch's 'Righteous One' for the heavenly Son of Man. The title is Ananias's, not Paul's own invention — confirming that Paul received the earliest Christological traditions through the community he had persecuted.
22.15: Martys autō pros pantas anthrōpous ('his witness to all people'): the universal scope of Paul's witness mandate — pros pantas anthrōpous ('to all people') — is stated explicitly at his commissioning. The Gentile mission is not a later development or accommodation; it is inscribed in Paul's original calling. His speaking before the hostile Jerusalem crowd is itself an act of witness 'to all people' — including the people most resistant to his message.
22.16: Baptisai kai apolousai tas hamartias sou epikalesamenos to onoma autou ('be baptized and wash away your sins, calling on his name'): the baptismal formula connects three elements — the physical act (baptism), the spiritual effect (washing of sins), and the spiritual instrument (calling on the name). The middle voice apolousai (wash away for yourself / have yourself washed) emphasizes the recipient's reception of an external act rather than self-cleansing. The 'calling on the name' formula connects to Joel 2:32 (Acts 2:21) — the same OT promise that structured Peter's Pentecost proclamation.
22.17: Genesthai me en ekstasei ('I fell into a trance'): ekstasis is the technical term for visionary states in Acts (10:10; 11:5) — a standing-outside-oneself in which divine communication occurs. Paul's temple trance appears only in this speech, not in Acts 9's narrative — additional biographical detail available in Paul's firsthand account. The setting is striking: in the very temple that represents Jewish covenantal identity, Paul receives a commission to leave Jerusalem. The center of Israel's worship is where Paul receives his mandate for those furthest from that covenant.
22.18: Exelthe en tachei ex Ierousalēm ('leave Jerusalem quickly'): the risen Christ's foreknowledge of Jerusalem's rejection anticipates the entire Acts narrative. The city that killed the prophets (Luke 13:34) will not receive Paul's testimony; the commission is redirected outward. Martyria (testimony/witness) is the technical term; Jerusalem's rejection of testimony is foreseen and does not negate the mission — it redirects it.
22.19: Paul's interjection — 'Lord, they know that I used to imprison and beat those who believed in you' — reveals his pastoral logic: his past as persecutor should make him a maximally credible witness to Jerusalem's community, who knew his former zeal. The argument has a compelling structure: if a man who imprisoned and beat believers now proclaims the resurrection, the only explanation is the truth of the thing he proclaims. The Lord's response (v.21) overrides this pastoral reasoning with a broader divine purpose — 'Go, for I will send you to the Gentiles.'
22.20: Kai hote exechynneto to haima Stephanou tou martyros sou ('when the blood of your witness Stephen was shed'): martys applied to Stephen is the first use in Acts approaching the later technical meaning of 'martyr' — one who witnesses unto death. Paul's confession — 'consenting and guarding the clothes of those killing him' (syneurokōn kai phylassōn ta himatia tōn anairountōn auton) — is his most explicit personal acknowledgment of guilt, placed here in the account of his conversion and his own experience of the forgiving encounter with the Risen One.
22.21: Hoti egō eis ethna makran exapostelō se ('for I will send you far away to the Gentiles'): 'far away' (makran) echoes Isa 57:19 ('peace to those far and near') and Eph 2:13's vocabulary for Gentile distance from the covenant. The Gentile commission is spoken from the Jerusalem temple — the most sacred Jewish space — to the most persecuting-turned-apostle. The irony is total: the center of Jewish covenantal worship is where Paul receives his mandate for those furthest from that covenant.
22.22: Hēkouon de autou achri toutou tou logou ('they listened to him until this word'): 'Gentiles' (ethnesin) is the trigger-word that ends the crowd's silence. The audience had settled to hear a Jewish defense; the announcement of a universal mission to Gentiles — Israel's historic outsiders — ruptures the listening. Aire apo tēs gēs ton toiouton ('take such a person from the earth') echoes the crowd's demand for Jesus (Luke 23:18, 'take him away!') — Luke's deliberate parallel between the trials of Jesus and Paul.
22.23: Rhiptountōn ta himatia kai koniorton ballontōn eis ton aera ('throwing off their cloaks and flinging dust into the air'): the gesture-complex — stripping outer garments and dust-throwing — is a theatrical expression of outrage, possibly involving quasi-ritual gestures of protestation or cursing. The dust-throwing may echo the mourning-gesture of covering oneself with dust (Josh 7:6; 2 Sam 13:19) — here deployed in anger rather than grief. Luke's vivid detail suggests eyewitness or close-source description.
22.24: Anazesthai auton mastixin ('to examine him by flogging'): the Roman interrogation technique of flagellum — a leather whip reinforced with lead weights or bone — was used to extract information from non-citizens and slaves. The tribune acts out of administrative logic: flog the troublemaker until he explains himself. He does not know or consider Paul's legal status at this point. The procedure is technically illegal for a Roman citizen not yet convicted, a fact Paul will invoke at precisely the moment of maximum legal exposure (v.25).
22.25: Ei anthrōpon Rhōmaion kai akatakriton exestin hymin mastizein ('is it lawful to flog a man who is a Roman citizen and uncondemned?'): Paul's timing is exquisite — he speaks at the moment of maximum legal exposure for the tribune, after the order has been given but before the first stroke. Akatakriton (uncondemned/without trial) is the legal term for summary punishment without due process — already invoked in 16:37 at Philippi. The citizen-claim precisely when the procedure is about to begin forces an immediate halt. Roman law (lex Valeria and lex Porcia) prohibited the physical punishment of citizens without trial.
22.26: Hora ti melleis poiein ('watch what you are about to do'): the centurion's warning to the commander shows the chain of accountability activated by the citizenship claim. He recognizes the legal risk before the commander does — he has seen enough of Roman administrative law to know that flogging a citizen is a prosecutable offense. The rapid institutional response to the legal signal — escalate, do not proceed — reflects the Roman military's practical training in liability management.
22.27: Rhōmaios ei ('are you a Roman citizen?'): the tribune's direct challenge; Paul's 'Yes, I am' is legally sufficient. Roman citizenship claims were taken seriously; the legal penalty for false claims was severe enough to make false claims relatively rare, and the penalties for flogging a genuine citizen severe enough to make commanders cautious. Paul's calm affirmation carries both legal weight and the spiritual composure of one whose identity is secured at a level deeper than civic status.
22.28: Egō de kai gegennēmai ('I was born to it'): Paul's birth-citizenship outranks the tribune's purchased citizenship — birth-citizenship was the superior form in Roman social hierarchy. How Paul's family acquired citizenship is not stated; possibilities include Tarsus's civic status, Paul's father's service to Rome, or citizenship granted to a Jewish community in Cilicia. The social inversion is deliberate: the prisoner is socially superior to the officer interrogating him, a fact that changes the entire dynamic of the interaction.
22.29: Ho chiliarchos de ephobethe epignous hoti Rhōmaios estin kai hoti auton ēn dedekōs ('the commander was alarmed when he realized he was a Roman and that he had chained him'): chaining a Roman citizen without due process was itself illegal — the commander has already committed a legal offense before the flogging question even arose. His fear is personal legal exposure, not merely administrative concern. The irony of the power dynamic is complete: the Roman officer who arrested Paul is now more legally vulnerable than the prisoner.
22.30: Bouleusē to asphales ('to find out the certain truth'): asphales (certain/secure) recurs in the legal investigation sections of Acts — the Roman system's insistence on procedural exactness. The tribune's decision to convene the Sanhedrin is his attempt to access Jewish legal expertise — the same kind of referral Pilate made to Herod (Luke 23:7). Roman administrators consistently sought local expertise for disputes arising from religious law; convening the Sanhedrin as a consulting body was within the tribune's authority.

23.1: Syneidēsei agathē pepoliteumai tō theō achri tautēs tēs hēmeras ('I have lived my life before God with a clear conscience to this day'): syneidēsis (conscience) is a Pauline technical term appearing 20 times in his letters (Rom 2:15; 9:1; 1 Cor 8:7; 2 Cor 1:12; 1 Tim 1:5, 19; 2 Tim 1:3). Its presence here confirms Luke's authentic representation of Pauline vocabulary. The cognate pepoliteumai (perfect — 'I have conducted my citizenship/life') uses civic vocabulary: the moral life is public, accountable, a form of civic participation. The claim is bold before the Sanhedrin: Paul does not acknowledge a break in moral continuity between his pre-conversion zeal and his apostolic vocation.
23.2: Ho archiereus Ananias: the high priest Ananias son of Nebedeus served c. AD 47-58 — known from Josephus (Ant. 20.9.2; Wars 2.17.9) as violent, greedy, and pro-Roman, despised by many Jews. His order to strike Paul on the mouth (tuptein autou to stoma) — before any verdict — is a procedural violation of the Sanhedrin's own rules. The Mishnah (Sanhedrin 9.1) required a formal trial before any punishment; Ananias's impulsive order exposes the hypocrisy that Paul will immediately name.
23.3: Typsai se mellei ho theos, toiche kekoniamene ('God is going to strike you, you whitewashed wall!'): the 'whitewashed wall' image echoes Ezekiel 13:10-16, where God condemns the prophets who whitewash a flimsy wall — presenting an appearance of solidity over hollowness. Jesus uses similar imagery in Matt 23:27 ('whitewashed tombs'). Paul's outburst is prophetic denunciation in the OT tradition — naming the gap between the high priest's judicial role and his actual behavior. The prediction ('God is going to strike you') was partially enacted: Ananias was assassinated by Jewish nationalists in AD 66, cut down by those he had exploited.
23.4: The bystanders' rebuke — 'how dare you insult God's high priest?' — highlights the tension between office and conduct. The office of high priest deserved honor; the conduct Paul observed deserved rebuke. Paul's response (v.5) handles this tension by appealing to Scripture — 'it is written: do not speak evil of a ruler of your people' (Exod 22:28). He accepts the correction without retracting the substance of his accusation; compliance with Torah even when it works against himself models the very standard the high priest was not meeting.
23.5: Ouk ēdein, adelphoi, hoti estin archiereus ('I did not know, brothers, that he was the high priest'): various explanations have been proposed: genuine disorientation after the previous day's chaos; Paul's poor eyesight (Gal 4:15; 6:11); the high priest's lack of formal vestments in this informal setting; or (most likely read as irony) 'I would not have recognized such a person as high priest by his behavior.' Whatever the reading, Paul responds to the correction by citing Exod 22:28 — grounding his behavior change in scriptural authority, not personal respect for Ananias.
23.6: Farisaios ... huios Farisaiōn ('a Pharisee, the son of Pharisees'): Paul's declaration of Pharisaic identity is not opportunistic but accurate (Phil 3:5 — 'a Pharisee by training'). The resurrection was the genuine center of his message; the Pharisee-Sadducee split on resurrection was the real theological issue at stake. The strategy exploits an existing fault-line — but it is not dishonest: Paul stands in the Pharisaic tradition of resurrection hope that the Sadducees rejected, and the Pharisees recognize it. Elpidos kai anastaseōs nekrōn egō krinomai ('it is concerning hope and the resurrection of the dead that I am being tried') is an accurate theological summary.
23.7: Schisma tou plēthous ('a split of the assembly'): the Sanhedrin's division is Luke's demonstration that the resurrection was a live, contested, divisive issue within Judaism itself — not merely a Christian aberration. Pharisees and Sadducees had irreconcilable positions on bodily resurrection, the existence of angels and spirits, and the nature of human destiny. Paul's strategic declaration puts him in the Pharisaic camp — which was the majority party — on the central question that motivated his entire mission.
23.8: Saddoukaioi men gar legousin mē einai anastasin mēte angelon mēte pneuma ('the Sadducees say there is no resurrection, neither angel nor spirit'): Luke's parenthetical theological summary matches Josephus (Ant. 18.1.4) exactly: 'The Sadducees deny the persistence of the soul after death, as also the rewards of the underworld, including punishments.' The Sadducees' triple denial — no resurrection, no angels, no spirits — appears to have been a principled theological position distinguishing them from Pharisees who affirmed all three based on oral tradition and broader canonical interpretation. The Sadducees restricted authority to the Pentateuch.
23.9: Ei de pneuma elalēsen autō ē angelos ('what if a spirit or an angel has spoken to him?'): the Pharisees' open question is the most theologically generous interpretation of Paul's Damascus experience that a non-believing Pharisee could offer — and it remains within their own worldview. They cannot deny resurrection as a category; they must at least consider that Paul's encounter fits their tradition of divine communication through heavenly mediators. Luke records this concession without suggesting the Pharisees became Christians; their theological solidarity with Paul's claim is real but limited.
23.10: Mē diaspassthē ho Paulos hyp' autōn ('Paul should be torn apart by them'): the physical danger — diaspaō (tear to pieces) — reflects the intensity of the theological dispute turned violent. The tribune's intervention (rescuing Paul again from a Jewish institutional setting) continues the Acts pattern of Roman authority protecting Paul from Jewish mob violence. The paradox of Roman imperial machinery repeatedly rescuing the apostle to the Gentiles is Luke's running comment on divine providence using unexpected instruments.
23.11: Tharsei ('take courage'): the Lord's nocturnal appearance uses vocabulary consistent with the divine commissioning appearances throughout Acts (18:9-10; 27:23-24). Tharsei ('be courageous/confident') is also the word Jesus used to the paralyzed man (Matt 9:2), to the disciples on the stormy lake (Matt 14:27), and to the woman healed of bleeding (Mark 5:34). Dei se kai eis Rhōmēn martyrēsai ('you must testify about me in Rome also'): the divine necessity (dei) structures Paul's entire journey from Jerusalem to Rome — imprisonment, appeals, shipwreck, all within the mandate.
23.12: Anathematisantes heautous ('bound themselves with a solemn oath'): the anathema (solemn curse/ban) was the most serious oath form in Judaism, placing the oath-taker under divine curse if the vow was broken. Binding oneself by anathema not to eat or drink until an action was completed was a recognized practice; if the condition was not met, the oath could be dissolved by a rabbi. The number 'more than forty' (pleiōn tesserakonta) is precise enough to suggest Luke had access to detailed information about the conspiracy — possibly from Paul's own later recounting or from community members who knew the story.
23.13: The specificity of 'more than forty' conspirators reflects the kind of concrete detail that comes from sources close to the events. Luke's precision in Acts — troop numbers, journey distances, nautical terms, specific officials' names — is a consistent feature that ancient and modern historians have found consistent with reliable oral and written sourcing.
23.14: Anathemati anethematisamen heautous ('we have bound ourselves with a solemn curse'): the intensive cognate construction (Hebrew absolute-infinitive equivalent in Greek) is the most emphatic form — 'we have absolutely, certainly bound ourselves.' The conspirators approach the chief priests and elders — the same institutional leadership that had been unable to stop Peter and the apostles in chs. 4-5 — now as co-conspirators in another attempt to eliminate the movement's key figure.
23.15: Hōs mellontas diaginōskein akribesteron ta peri autou ('as if you were going to examine his case more carefully'): the conspiracy requires the official judicial body's complicity — the Sanhedrin must request a retrial with a legitimate-sounding pretext. The word akribesteron ('more accurately/exactly') is the very word Felix will use to describe his own knowledge of the Way (24:22 — 'knowing the Way more accurately'). Luke's verbal connections across the trial narratives are deliberate; the word 'accurate' appears in contexts where the truth is being either sought or evaded.
23.16: Ho huios tēs adelphēs Paulou ('the son of Paul's sister'): the only reference to Paul's extended family in the entire NT. The detail is strikingly specific — not 'a relative' but 'the son of his sister.' Paul's nephew had access to both the conspiracy's information and to the barracks where Paul was held — suggesting a family with sufficient social standing to move between Jewish and Roman circles in Jerusalem. Luke does not explain how the nephew learned of the plot; he narrates the providential chain without editorializing.
23.17: Eis ton chiliarchon ('to the commander'): Paul's access to the tribune through the military chain of command — calling a centurion, requesting the centurion escort his nephew, the centurion complying — shows Paul functioning with considerable agency even as a prisoner. The Roman military hierarchy is navigable and responsive to proper requests from persons with appropriate status. Paul operates within the system's logic rather than against it, using the same structures that arrested him to protect his mission's continuation.
23.18: The centurion's speech to the commander — 'the prisoner Paul called me and asked me to bring this young man to you' — is a model of institutional communication: factual, deferential, complete. Luke's representations of Roman military communication throughout Acts 22-28 are consistently credible; the detailed knowledge of Roman procedures is one of the features that supports Luke's claim (in the 'we' passages) to firsthand experience of Paul's journey.
23.19: Epilabomenos de tēs cheiros autou ho chiliarchos kai anachorēsas kat' idian ('taking him by the hand and drawing him aside privately'): the intimate gesture — taking the young man by the hand — and the private setting signal the commander's appropriate caution. He takes the information seriously enough to handle it personally and discreetly. His care with the source and the information reflects competent intelligence management; he does not dismiss or minimize the nephew's approach.
23.20: The young man's report is specific and carries the urgency of firsthand intelligence: the conspiracy is coordinated (syntithemai = agree together), the number is precise (more than forty), the oath is binding, and the plan is ready for execution tomorrow. His instruction — 'don't give in to them' (mē peisthai autois) — is direct advocacy to the commander. Luke's repetition of the conspiracy details (vv.12-15, then vv.20-21 in the nephew's report) functions as both narrative and documentary confirmation.
23.21: Anathemati anethematisan heautous mē phagein mēde piein heōs ou anelōsin auton: the nephew's exact repetition of the oath-language confirms that he understood what he heard and that the information is reliable. 'Now they are ready and waiting for your consent' (nyn eisin hetoimoi prosdechomenoi tēn apo sou epangelian) — the commander is explicitly told that the conspirators are counting on his cooperation. The information removes the pretense of a judicial inquiry and reveals the ambush plan.
23.22: Ekeleuse mēdeni eklaein hoti tauta enephanisas pros me ('he ordered him to tell no one he had reported this to him'): source protection — the commander ensures the nephew's anonymity. The young man is never named in Luke's account; his anonymity is preserved even in the telling. The secrecy protects both the young man and the effectiveness of the counter-plan: if the conspirators learned their plot was known, they would either abort or modify it.
23.23: Stratiotai diakosioi, hippeis hebdomekonta, dexiolabous diakosioi pentēkonta ('200 soldiers, 70 cavalry, 200 spearmen'): the escort of 470 soldiers for one prisoner is remarkable. Dexiolaboi (spearmen/right-hand men) is a hapax legomenon — a technical term for a specialized auxiliary unit; its specificity supports Luke's access to official records or firsthand military knowledge. The size of the escort reflects either the seriousness of the assassination threat or the tribune's desire to demonstrate Roman efficiency to the governor with a visible transfer.
23.24: Ktemē te parastēsai hina te epibibasantes ton Paulon diasōsōsin pros Phēlika ton hēgemona ('provide horses for Paul and bring him safely to Felix'): Paul's transport by horse rather than walking reflects his legal status — a Roman citizen being transferred for formal trial, not a common prisoner being transported. The word diasōzō (bring safely) is the same word used for salvation from shipwreck (27:44; 28:1) — the commander's legal obligation to deliver the prisoner safely to the governor.
23.25: Grapsas epistolēn echousan ton typon touton ('having written a letter of the following form'): Roman official correspondence followed standardized forms: name + title, addressee + title, greeting (chairein), narrative body, legal conclusion. Whether Luke had access to the actual letter or reconstructed it from Paul's knowledge, the document's form is consistent with Roman official correspondence of the period. Its self-serving revision of events (v.27) is realistic — bureaucrats protect themselves in written records.
23.26: Klaudios Lysias tō kratistō hēgemoni Phēliki chairein ('Claudius Lysias to His Excellency Governor Felix, greetings'): this is the only place in Acts where the tribune's name — Claudius Lysias — is given. The name itself is informative: 'Claudius' suggests his citizenship was obtained under Claudius (41-54 CE); 'Lysias' is Greek. He is a Greco-Roman military officer who purchased his citizenship (22:28) and governs with reasonable competence. Kratistos ('most excellent') was the standard form of address for equestrian-rank officials (Felix held an equestrian appointment).
23.27: Sylalemphthenta hypo tōn Ioudaiōn: Lysias's account slightly reorders the events — he presents himself as knowing Paul's citizenship before rescuing him (whereas Acts 22 shows he learned the citizenship only after ordering the flogging). This self-protective revision in an official report is entirely realistic; administrators in all eras adjust the narrative in official correspondence. Luke records both versions without harmonizing them — the discrepancy between Lysias's letter and the earlier narrative is itself a historical datum about how official reports work.
23.28: Eis to synedrion autōn ('to their Sanhedrin'): Lysias correctly identifies the dispute as a matter of internal Jewish law (peri zētēmatōn tou nomou autōn, 'concerning disputes about their own law'). This conclusion — that Paul's case involves Jewish religious law, not Roman criminal law — anticipates Felix's, Festus's, and Agrippa's identical conclusions. The Roman judicial tradition consistently recognizes that Paul has not committed a Roman crime; political calculation alone prevents his release.
23.29: Mēden axion thanatou ē desmōn echonta enklēma ('having no charge deserving death or imprisonment'): the third of the innocence declarations structuring Acts 22-26 (alongside Festus in 25:25 and Agrippa in 26:31-32). The pattern mirrors the three innocence declarations in the Passion narrative (Luke 23:4, 14, 22 — Pilate's three 'no basis for a charge'). Paul's imprisonment is legally groundless by Roman standards — a fact stated repeatedly and never refuted, making the continued imprisonment a political choice, not a judicial one.
23.30: Pempsas pros se kai tois katēgorois parēngeila ('sending him to you and ordering his accusers'): the formal transfer — sending both the prisoner and his accusers to Felix — is the correct procedure for trans-jurisdictional cases in the Roman system. Trajan's rescript to Pliny (Ep. 10.96-97) shows the same procedure: accusers must appear in person before the governor. The case now enters the provincial governor's jurisdiction, where it will remain for two years (24:27) due to Felix's calculated inaction.
23.31: Antipatrida ('Antipatris'): about 35 miles north of Jerusalem, at the edge of the coastal plain where the Judean foothills end. The night march (probably 10-12 hours through the hill country) placed Paul safely past the danger zone of the narrow Jerusalem-to-coast passes where the ambush would have been set. At Antipatris the infantry could return to Jerusalem (the assassination threat was now past) while cavalry alone could escort Paul the remaining 25 miles to Caesarea.
23.32: Tous hippeis apelthein syn autō ('leaving the cavalry to continue with him'): the troop-reduction at Antipatris is militarily logical — once past the mountain passes where the forty conspirators would have set the ambush, the threat was neutralized. The cavalry alone were more than sufficient to deter any threat on the coastal plain. Luke's attention to the troop-reduction detail — a seemingly incidental logistical note — is consistent throughout the Acts travel accounts with the texture of firsthand or close-source reporting.
23.33: Aperidokan kai ton Paulon autō parestēsan ('they delivered the letter and presented Paul to him'): the formal handover — letter first, then prisoner — follows Roman administrative procedure. The governor's question about province of origin (v.34) establishes personal jurisdiction; Felix confirms his authority and proceeds. In the Roman administrative system, where a person came from determined which governor had jurisdiction; Cilicia was governed from Syria but Paul was arrested in Judea, so Felix accepted jurisdiction.
23.34: Apo poias eparchias estin ('what province he was from'): the jurisdictional question was standard procedure. Felix learns Paul is from Cilicia — which was under the same Syrian provincial structure but geographically distinct. Felix's acceptance of jurisdiction despite the Cilician origin may reflect the fact that Paul was arrested in Judea and the charges arose there. The administrative detail is consistent with Roman provincial law and shows Luke's familiarity with how jurisdictional questions were handled.
23.35: En tō praitōriō tou Hērōdou phylassesthai auton ('to be kept under guard in Herod's praetorium'): the praetorium at Caesarea — Herod the Great's coastal palace — had become the Roman governor's official residence and administrative center. Paul's custody in the praetorium is custodia militaris (military guard) rather than prison confinement — a form appropriate for a Roman citizen awaiting trial. Philippians 1:13 ('throughout the praetorium') may echo Paul's knowledge of this administrative headquarters from the two-year Caesarean imprisonment that follows.

24.1: Rhētor Tertullos tis ('a certain orator named Tertullus'): Tertullus is the first professional advocate (rhētor) explicitly named in Acts. His name is Latin — suggesting a Roman Jew, a Romanized Jew, or a Gentile hired for his rhetorical expertise. The high priest's decision to hire professional legal counsel reflects the seriousness of the case and the inadequacy of the Sanhedrin's own members to argue before a Roman governor in the forensic rhetorical style that Felix would expect. Professional orators trained in Latin and Greek forensic rhetoric were available for hire throughout the empire.
24.2: Pollēs eirēnēs tynchanontes dia sou kai katorthōmatōn ginomenōn tō ethnei toutō dia tēs sēs pronoias ('we enjoy much peace through you and reforms in this nation through your foresight'): the captatio benevolentiae (capture of goodwill) was a required opening in Roman forensic oratory — complimenting the judge on virtues relevant to the case. Tertullus's praise of Felix for 'peace' is historically ironic: Felix's tenure (52-60 CE) was characterized by multiple violent suppressions of social movements, including the Egyptian prophet's movement (Acts 21:38) and the first significant Zealot uprisings. Josephus (Wars 2.13; Ant. 20.8.5) describes Felix as cruel and rapacious. The flattery is formulaic, not factual — and both Tertullus and Felix know it.
24.3: Pantē te kai pantachou apodechometha, kratiste Phēlix, meta pasēs eucharistias ('everywhere and in every way, most excellent Felix, we acknowledge this with all gratitude'): the accumulated superlatives — 'everywhere,' 'in every way,' 'most excellent,' 'all gratitude' — pile up in conventional forensic flattery. Roman forensic oratory required such openings; courts expected them; skilled judges measured speeches by their argumentative quality rather than by their compliments. The density of the flattery signals its conventional, not sincere, character — a performance both parties recognized as such.
24.4: Kleptō se epi pleion ('I do not want to take up too much of your time'): the brevitas topos — claiming brevity before a speech — was a standard rhetorical device (Latin praeteritio). By promising brevity, the orator signals confidence in the case's strength and respect for the judge's time, both of which cultivate goodwill. Tertullus's actual speech is relatively brief; his promise of brevity is kept more consistently than most ancient orators who invoke the convention.
24.5: Loimos ('a plague/pestilence'): a stock insult in ancient rhetoric for political troublemakers and social agitators — Demosthenes had called Aeschines a loimos. Prōtostatēs ('ringleader/front-rank man'): a military term for the man in the front rank, used here metaphorically for the leader of a disruptive movement. Hairesis ('sect'): the first application of this term to Christians in Acts. In Paul's letters hairesis means 'faction, divisive party' (1 Cor 11:19; Gal 5:20); Tertullus uses it to characterize Christianity as a schismatic faction within Judaism — undermining its claim to be the legitimate fulfillment of Israel's heritage.
24.6: Ton hieron epeiresen bebēlōsai ('he tried to desecrate the temple'): the charge has been downgraded from the original accusation in 21:28 — where the crowd alleged Paul brought Trophimus the Ephesian into the inner courts (an offense punishable by death under both Jewish and Roman law, confirmed by Josephus Wars 6.2.4). By the time of the formal charge, the accusers have shifted to 'tried to desecrate' — a vaguer, attempted offense. The weakening of the specific charge is itself evidence that the original accusation could not be proven.
24.7: The verse in some manuscripts adds that Lysias 'with great violence took him from our hands, ordering his accusers to come before you' — a Western text expansion likely added to explain the transition from v.6 to v.8. Most critical editions bracket or omit it. Its insertion shows ancient readers sensed a gap in the speech's logic that needed bridging; Luke's original text apparently moved from the temple-desecration charge directly to 'examine him yourself' without this editorial addition.
24.8: Para hou dynēsē autos anakrinas ('from whom you will be able, by examining him yourself'): Tertullus refers the decisive evidence to Paul himself — an unusual rhetorical confidence. He assumes Felix's examination will confirm the charges. The confidence may be rhetorical bluff, or it may reflect the accusers' expectation that Felix would accept their characterization of Paul as a troublemaker and act accordingly without rigorous investigation. Paul's counter-speech will demolish this expectation by meeting every charge with specific facts.
24.9: Synepethento de kai hoi Ioudaioi ('the Jews also joined in the accusation'): the congregation of accusers — high priest, delegation of elders, professional orator, and supporting Jewish witnesses — provides collective testimony. In Roman law, multiple witnesses were important; the concurrence appears overwhelming. Paul's response must counter not just the legal argument but the social weight of an entire institutional leadership testifying against him.
24.10: Ek pollōn etōn onta se kritēn tō ethnei toutō epistamenos ('knowing that for many years you have been a judge over this nation'): Paul's captatio benevolentiae is noticeably shorter and more measured than Tertullus's — he compliments Felix's experience rather than his beneficence. Judicial experience is a neutral-positive quality: it implies Felix knows the law, is not easily fooled, and can distinguish genuine evidence from accusation. The appeal to competence rather than generosity is a more restrained and arguably more effective form of judicial appeal.
24.11: Ou pleiōn eisin moi hēmerai dōdeka ('it is not more than twelve days since I went up to worship'): the chronological argument is precise and falsifiable — twelve days is incompatible with the charge of organizing widespread agitation. Paul counts the days from his arrival in Jerusalem (21:15-17 — three days before meeting James) to his arrest. The brevity of the visit, verifiable by the Sanhedrin members themselves, directly undercuts Tertullus's characterization of Paul as a systematic troublemaker 'all over the world.'
